$$\mathcal{A}_{passive}(M) = M' \quad \text{(copy)}$$

The original message $M$ reaches $R$ unchanged.

**Step 3: For an active attack:**
$$\mathcal{A}_{active}(M) = M'' \quad \text{where } M'' \neq M$$

The original message $M$ is **altered** or **replaced** before reaching $R$.

**Step 4: Define the integrity violation function.**

$$\text{Integrity}_{violation} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } H(M) \neq H(M'') \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5: For replay attacks, the temporal component matters:**

$$\text{Replay}_{validity} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert T_{current} - T_{captured} \vert > \Delta T_{threshold} \\ 0 & \text{if within threshold} \end{cases}$$

The receiver rejects the message if the replayed timestamp exceeds the allowed window.

**Step 6: For DoS attacks, the availability metric is computed as:**

$$A_{service} = \frac{T_{available}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$$

A successful DoS attack drives $A_{service} \rightarrow 0$.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Replay Attack Detection

Consider an authentication session where the legitimate timestamp window is **$\Delta T = 30$ seconds**.

A user sends a request at $T_{send} = 100$ seconds (epoch time). An attacker captures this and replays it at $T_{replay} = 145$ seconds.

**Step 1:** Compute the time difference.

$$\Delta t = T_{replay} - T_{send} = 145 - 100 = 45 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with the threshold.

$$45 \text{ seconds} > \Delta T = 30 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3:** Decision.

$$\text{Result: Replay Attack DETECTED — Session rejected}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** A 15-second tolerance window is enough for legitimate network jitter (typically $< 5$ seconds for WANs) but too small for an adversary who replays minutes or hours later.

### 3.3 Logical Derivation: DoS Attack Saturation

A network channel has bandwidth $B = 1 \text{ Gbps}$. An attacker generates traffic at rate $P_{attacker} = 5 \text{ Gbps}$.

**Step 1:** Compute channel saturation ratio.

$$S = \frac{P_{attacker}}{B} = \frac{5}{1} = 5$$

**Step 2:** Effective throughput for legitimate users.

$$B_{effective} = B - P_{attacker} \cdot \rho = 1 - 5 \cdot 0.95 = -3.75 \text{ Gbps (negative)}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

Since $B_{effective} < 0$, the channel is **fully saturated** — the server's queue overflows, and legitimate packets are dropped. This constitutes a successful DoS.

$$P_{packet\_loss} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } S \leq 1 \\ 1 - \frac{1}{S} & \text{if } S > 1 \end{cases}$$

Plugging in $S = 5$:

$$P_{packet\_loss} = 1 - \frac{1}{5} = 0.8 = 80\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Active Attack Lifecycle

Below is a Mermaid block diagram illustrating how an active attack unfolds in a typical client-server communication scenario.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Legitimate Sender] --> B1[Encrypted Channel]
    B1 --> C1[Adversary Intercepts Message]
    C1 --> D1{Attack Type Selection}
    D1 -->|Type 1| E1[Masquerade: Impersonate Identity]
    D1 -->|Type 2| E2[Replay: Resend Captured Data]
    D1 -->|Type 3| E3[Modification: Alter Payload]
    D1 -->|Type 4| E4[Denial of Service: Flood Server]
    E1 --> F1[Receiver Deceived]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Compromised CIA Triad]
    G1 --> G2[Integrity Loss]
    G1 --> G3[Availability Loss]
    G1 --> G4[Authenticity Loss]
    G1 --> H1[Defense Layer Activated]
    H1 --> H2[Encryption AES / RSA]
    H1 --> H3[Hash Verification SHA-256]
    H1 --> H4[Nonce and Timestamp Check]
    H1 --> H5[Rate Limiting and Firewall]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Replay Attack Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SenderAlice
    participant NetChannel
    participant AdversaryEve
    participant ReceiverBob

    SenderAlice->>NetChannel: M with timestamp T1 and nonce N1
    NetChannel->>AdversaryEve: Intercepts M
    AdversaryEve->>AdversaryEve: Stores M for later
    Note over AdversaryEve: Waits for opportune moment
    AdversaryEve->>ReceiverBob: Replays M with same T1 and N1
    ReceiverBob->>ReceiverBob: Checks timestamp delta t
    ReceiverBob->>ReceiverBob: Detects stale nonce or expired timestamp
    ReceiverBob-->>AdversaryEve: Rejects request
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Active vs Passive Attacks

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Passive Attack Subtypes] --> P2[Eavesdropping]
    P1 --> P3[Traffic Analysis]
    P1 --> P4[Release of Message Contents]
    A1[Active Attack Subtypes] --> A2[Masquerade]
    A1 --> A3[Replay]
    A1 --> A4[Modification of Messages]
    A1 --> A5[Denial of Service]
    P2 --> C1[Threatens Confidentiality]
    P3 --> C1
    P4 --> C1
    A2 --> C2[Threatens Integrity and Authenticity]
    A3 --> C2
    A4 --> C2
    A5 --> C3[Threatens Availability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define active attacks. List any four types of active attacks mentioned in the KTU syllabus.

**Model Answer:**
Active attacks are security threats where an adversary deliberately modifies, fabricates, disrupts, or replays communication data to compromise system security. The four canonical types are:
1. **Masquerade** – impersonation of a legitimate entity.
2. **Replay** – retransmission of valid captured data.
3. **Modification of Messages** – altering the content of a legitimate message.
4. **Denial of Service (DoS)** – overwhelming the system to deny legitimate access.

> [!NOTE]
> Active attacks primarily target **Integrity, Availability, and Authenticity** of the CIA Triad.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between passive and active attacks with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Eavesdropping without modification | Alters, fabricates, or disrupts data |
| **Detection** | Difficult to detect | Easier to detect but harder to prevent |
| **Threat to** | Confidentiality | Integrity, Authenticity, Availability |
| **Example 1** | Sniffing network traffic | IP spoofing (Masquerade) |
| **Example 2** | Traffic flow analysis | SYN flood attack (DoS) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

(a) **Explain in detail the four categories of active attacks with suitable diagrams.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The four categories of active attacks as defined by **William Stallings** (standard reference for KTU) are:

**1. Masquerade:**
A scenario where one entity pretends to be a different entity. Typically involves authentication bypass via stolen credentials. Example: An attacker using a stolen username-password pair to access a corporate VPN.

**2. Replay Attack:**
Involves the passive capture of a valid transmission and its subsequent retransmission. Example: Capturing an authentication token and reusing it within its validity window. Defense: Nonces and timestamps.

**3. Modification of Messages:**
Involves altering the content of a legitimate message. Example: A man-in-the-middle modifies the destination account number in a wire transfer. Defense: Digital signatures and message authentication codes (MACs).

**4. Denial of Service:**
Prevents or inhibits the normal use of communication facilities. Example: A SYN flood attack on a web server, exhausting connection states. Defense: Rate limiting, firewalls, DDoS scrubbing services.

(b) **Discuss the CIA Triad impact of each active attack category.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Attack Type | Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability | Authenticity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Masquerade | Low impact | High impact | Medium | Severely compromised |
| Replay | Low | High | Low | Compromised |
| Modification | Medium | Severely compromised | Low | Compromised |
| DoS / DDoS | Low | Low | Severely compromised | Low |

**Explanation:**
- **Masquerade** primarily breaks **Authenticity** since the receiver believes the message came from a legitimate source.
- **Replay** breaks **Integrity** because an outdated message may be inconsistent with current state.
- **Modification** is the most direct attack on **Integrity** — the message is no longer trustworthy.
- **DoS** attacks **Availability** — the system remains intact but is rendered useless to legitimate users.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

(a) **With a neat block diagram, explain the mechanism of a replay attack and how nonces prevent it.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Replay Attack:**

1. Alice sends a legitimate authentication request $M$ with a session token to Bob.
2. The adversary **Eve** intercepts $M$ on the channel.
3. Eve **stores** $M$ and waits.
4. Later, Eve **replays** $M$ to Bob, pretending to be Alice.
5. If Bob's system does not check freshness, it accepts the message.

**Block Diagram (in words):**
$$\text{Alice} \rightarrow [\text{Channel}] \rightarrow \text{Eve (Capture)} \rightarrow [\text{Channel}] \rightarrow \text{Bob (Accept?)}$$

**Defense Using Nonces:**
A **nonce** (Number used ONCE) is a random value included in every transaction. Bob stores all recently used nonces. If Eve replays $M$, the nonce has already been seen, and Bob rejects it.

Mathematically:
$$\text{Valid} \iff \text{nonce}_i \notin \{\text{nonce}_1, \text{nonce}_2, \ldots, \text{nonce}_{n-1}\}$$

(b) **Compare active attacks with passive attacks in terms of detection difficulty, intent, and countermeasures.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Covert observation | Direct tampering/disruption |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Very high (silent) | Moderate (often leaves traces) |
| **Modification of Data** | Never | Always |
| **Countermeasures** | Encryption, traffic padding | Authentication, integrity checks, redundancy |
| **Resource Demand** | Low to moderate | Often high (e.g., botnets) |
| **CIA Triad Focus** | Confidentiality | Integrity, Availability, Authenticity |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is writing that active attacks *also* compromise confidentiality. While some active attacks may incidentally expose data (e.g., modification revealing fields), the **defining characteristic** is *alteration or disruption*, not eavesdropping. Misclassifying the CIA Triad impact will cost you **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Active attacks **modify, fabricate, replay, or disrupt** legitimate communication.
- The **four canonical types** are: Masquerade, Replay, Modification of Messages, and Denial of Service.
- Active attacks threaten the **Integrity, Availability, and Authenticity** of the CIA Triad.
- **Replay attacks** are countered using **nonces** and **timestamps**.
- **DoS/DDoS** attacks are countered via **rate limiting, firewalls, and traffic scrubbing**.
- **Masquerade attacks** are countered via strong **authentication** (multi-factor, biometrics, certificates).
- **Modification attacks** are countered via **digital signatures and MACs (Message Authentication Codes)**.
- Always mention the **CIA Triad impact** explicitly — it is a guaranteed scoring differentiator.
- In KTU exams, draw a **block diagram or sequence diagram** wherever possible — it earns 1–2 extra marks.
- Distinguish clearly between **passive** (eavesdropping) and **active** (tampering) attacks in comparative questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Services - Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Security Services - Authentication ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / ITU-T X.800 Framework) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Authentication** is the security service that provides assurance about the **identity of an entity** (user, process, system) or the **origin of a message**, thereby preventing masquerade, replay, and source-spoofing attacks. As per **ITU-T X.800** and **ISO 7498-2**, it is one of the five primary security services alongside access control, confidentiality, integrity, and non-repudiation. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies authentication into two fundamental sub-services: | Sub-Service | Scope of Assurance | Classical Threat Mitigated | |---|---|---| | **Peer Entity Authentication** | Confirms identity of a *live* communicating party during a session. | **Masquerade / Impersonation** | | **Data Origin Authentication** | Confirms identity of the *creator* of a specific data unit. | **Forgery / Source Spoofing** | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Embassy Scenario** > Imagine you walk into the Indian Embassy in Tokyo. The guard checks your **passport** (something you *have*) and asks personal questions only you can answer (something you *know*). If both check out, the embassy officer stamps your visa — confirming that **YOU** are indeed the legitimate bearer. The stamp (a *cryptographic token*) is later accepted worldwide as proof that the embassy authenticated your identity. This is exactly what digital authentication does: it combines *possession*, *knowledge*, and *inherence* factors to bind an identity to a digital exchange. ### 1.3 Authentication Factors (The "Three Pillars") > [!IMPORTANT] > Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is graded by KTU examiners based on how clearly students enumerate the three independent factors below. - **Type 1 — Something You KNOW (Knowledge Factor):** Passwords, PINs, pass-phrases, security questions. - **Type 2 — Something You HAVE (Possession Factor):** Smart cards, hardware tokens (RSA SecurID), OTPs sent to a registered phone, X.509 digital certificates. - **Type 3 — Something You ARE (Inherence / Biometric Factor):** Fingerprint, iris scan, voice print, facial geometry. **Strength Metric:** A system requiring *two* independent factors is called **2FA (Two-Factor Authentication)** and is the current industry baseline. KTU expects students to know that biometric FAR (False Acceptance Rate) and FRR (False Rejection Rate) are the standard evaluation metrics. ### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics - **HMAC Output Length** is **128, 192, or 256 bits** depending on the underlying hash function. - **Biometric FAR** is typically **0.0001 %** for high-security iris systems. - **One-Time Password (OTP) Validity** is generally **30 to 60 seconds** (TOTP standard). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Authentication The authentication process always follows a **Verification Pipeline**: 1. **Identity Claim (Identification):** The principal (claimant) presents an identifier — e.g., a User ID or a certificate serial number. 2. **Credential Submission:** The principal supplies authenticators matching the registered credentials. 3. **Verification Engine:** The verifier (server / KDC) executes a comparison algorithm — string match, cryptographic challenge-response, or biometric matching. 4. **Result Decision:** **Accept** (valid session token issued) or **Reject** (audit-log entry written, counter incremented). ### 2.2 Why Cryptographic Authentication is Needed (vs. Plain Passwords) Plaintext password transmission is vulnerable to **eavesdropping** and **replay attacks**. Cryptographic protocols solve this using: - **Nonces** (Numbers Used Once) to ensure freshness. - **Hashing** to protect stored passwords. - **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** to bind integrity + origin. - **Challenge-Response** to prevent replay of static credentials. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **must-know for the KTU ESE**. Memorize the **structure** of each equation, not just the symbols. LaTeX uses `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve table syntax. | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Hash-based Password Storage** | $h = H(p \oplus s)$ | $H$ = hash function, $p$ = password, $s$ = random salt (128 bits) | Defeats rainbow-table attacks | | **MAC Computation** | $M = H(K \Vert m)$ | $K$ = secret key, $m$ = message | Provides data origin authentication | | **HMAC Construction** | $\text{HMAC}(K,m) = H\!\left[(K_{0} \oplus opad)\Vert H\!\left[(K_{0} \oplus ipad)\Vert m\right]\right]$ | $K_{0}$ = processed key, $ipad = 0x36$, $opad = 0x5C$ | Internet-standard MAC (RFC 2104) | | **Challenge-Response** | $R = E_{K}(N)$ | $E_{K}$ = symmetric encryption, $N$ = nonce | Proves knowledge of key without revealing it | | **Nonce Freshness Check** | $\vert N_{t} - N_{r} \vert \leq \Delta$ | $N_{t}$ = timestamp nonce, $N_{r}$ = received nonce, $\Delta$ = window | Anti-replay guard | | **Biometric CER** | $CER = \frac{FAR + FRR}{2}$ | Equal Error Rate metric | Security-usability balance point | | **RSA Signature** | $S = M^{d} \bmod n$ | $d$ = private exponent, $n$ = modulus | Non-repudiable authentication | | **RSA Verification** | $M' = S^{e} \bmod n$, accept iff $M' = M$ | $e$ = public exponent | Verifier checks origin | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **E-commerce TLS Handshake:** Uses **RSA / ECDSA certificates** for server authentication — your browser checks the X.509 chain. - **Banking Transactions:** Use **HMAC-SHA-256** in SWIFT messaging. - **Smart Cards (EMV):** Combine **challenge-response with RSA signatures** for offline card authentication. - **Kerberos (Windows AD, MIT):** Uses **Needham-Schroeder symmetric-key protocol** with a Key Distribution Center (KDC) for ticket-based authentication. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — HMAC Construction **Problem Context (typical KTU ESE):** *Show that the HMAC construction resists length-extension attacks and derive its security bound.* We start with the simple but insecure construction: $$ M_1 = H(K \Vert m) $$ **Step 1 — Identify the Length-Extension Flaw.** If the underlying hash $H$ is a Merkle–Damgård construction (e.g., SHA-256), the adversary can compute $H(K \Vert m \Vert p)$ without knowing $K$, where $p$ is any padding-aligned suffix. Thus $M_1$ is broken. **Step 2 — Insert Nested Hashing (the iPad/oPad trick).** RFC 2104 proposes a nested structure: $$ \text{HMAC}_{K}(m) = H\!\left[(K_{0} \oplus opad)\Vert H\!\left[(K_{0} \oplus ipad)\Vert m\right]\right] $$ where the constants are fixed padding bytes: $$ ipad = \texttt{0x36} \;\text{repeated}\; B \;\text{times} $$ $$ opad = \texttt{0x5C} \;\text{repeated}\; B \;\text{times} $$ Here, $B$ is the **byte length of the hash function's internal block** (64 bytes for SHA-1, SHA-256). **Step 3 — Key Pre-processing ($K_0$).** If the key $K$ is shorter than $B$ bytes, it is **right-padded with zeros** to length $B$. If longer, it is first hashed: $$ K_{0} = \begin{cases} K \;\text{with zeros appended}, & \text{if } \text{len}(K) \leq B \\ H(K) \;\text{with zeros appended}, & \text{if } \text{len}(K) > B \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Inner and Outer Hash Calls.** Define the two intermediate states: $$ S_{\text{inner}} = H\!\left[(K_{0} \oplus ipad)\Vert m\right] $$ $$ S_{\text{outer}} = H\!\left[(K_{0} \oplus opad)\Vert S_{\text{inner}}\right] $$ Therefore the final MAC tag is: $$ \text{HMAC}_{K}(m) = S_{\text{outer}} $$ **Step 5 — Why This Resists Length-Extension.** The inner hash $S_{\text{inner}}$ is fed to the *outer* hash along with $K_0 \oplus opad$. An attacker attempting to extend the message must break the *outer* call, which requires a fresh secret. The original message $m$ is no longer a direct prefix of the input to the final hash, blocking the length-extension chain. **Step 6 — Security Bound (informal).** Let $\text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{coll}}(q)$ be the adversary's advantage in finding a collision in $H$ after $q$ queries. The forged-MAC advantage is bounded as: $$ \text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{mac-forge}}(t, q, \ell) \;\leq\; \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{coll}}\!\left(t', q\right) + \frac{1}{2^{L}} $$ where $L$ is the MAC output length and $t'$ is the adversary's running time. This is a **provable security reduction**. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Authentication Methods > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently award partial credit for clean classification. Use this matrix verbatim. | Dimension | Method Category | Strength | Weakness | KTU Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Knowledge-based** | Static password | Simple | Phishing, brute force | Unix `/etc/passwd` | | **Possession-based** | Smart card + PIN | 2FA | Card loss | EMV chip cards | | **Biometric** | Fingerprint | User-friendly | FAR, sensor spoofing | Aadhaar authentication | | **Cryptographic (symmetric)** | Challenge-Response with shared $K$ | Strong, no replay | Key distribution | Needham-Schroeder | | **Cryptographic (asymmetric)** | Digital Signature | Non-repudiable | PKI overhead | RSA, ECDSA | | **Token-based** | TOTP / HOTP | Time-limited | Clock skew | Google Authenticator | | **Zero-Knowledge** | ZK-SNARK / Fiat-Shamir | Reveals nothing | Computational cost | zk-Rollups | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — RSA Authentication Signature > **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** Alice's RSA private key is $d = 7$, public modulus $n = 33$, public exponent $e = 3$. She wishes to authenticate a message $M = 4$ (interpreted as a numeric hash). Compute the signature and show the verification. **Step 1 — Verify the Public-Private Pair.** Check that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. $$ \phi(33) = \phi(3) \cdot \phi(11) = 2 \cdot 10 = 20 $$ $$ e \cdot d = 3 \cdot 7 = 21 \equiv 1 \pmod{20} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the Signature.** Signature is produced using the private key: $$ S = M^{d} \bmod n = 4^{7} \bmod 33 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate $4^{7} \bmod 33$.** $4^{2} = 16$, $4^{3} = 64 \equiv 31 \pmod{33}$, $4^{6} = (4^{3})^{2} \equiv 31^{2} = 961 \equiv 961 - 29 \cdot 33 = 961 - 957 = 4 \pmod{33}$. Therefore: $$ 4^{7} = 4^{6} \cdot 4 \equiv 4 \cdot 4 = 16 \pmod{33} $$ So $S = 16$. **Step 4 — Bob's Verification.** Bob computes: $$ M' = S^{e} \bmod n = 16^{3} \bmod 33 $$ $16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - 7 \cdot 33 = 256 - 231 = 25 \pmod{33}$. $16^{3} = 16^{2} \cdot 16 \equiv 25 \cdot 16 = 400 \equiv 400 - 12 \cdot 33 = 400 - 396 = 4 \pmod{33}$. **Step 5 — Decision.** Since $M' = 4 = M$, **Bob accepts the signature** as authentic. Alice's identity is verified. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 One-Way Authentication Flow (Symmetric Key, Nonce-Based) > [!IMPORTANT] > All Mermaid node IDs follow the **Alpha Rule** (letters + digits, no reserved words). Labels are plain uppercase text, no markdown inside quotes. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CLAIMANT as Claimant A participant VERIFIER as Verifier B participant KDC as Trusted KDC CLAIMANT->>KDC: REQUEST TICKET FOR B WITH NONCE NA KDC-->>CLAIMANT: TICKET KAB{NA, KAB, IDB, TS} ENCRYPTED WITH KAS CLAIMANT->>VERIFIER: TICKET KAB{NA, KAB, IDB, TS} CLAIMANT->>VERIFIER: ENCRYPT WITH KAB {TIMESTAMP TS} VERIFIER-->>CLAIMANT: ENCRYPT WITH KAB {TS PLUS ONE} Note over CLAIMANT,VERIFIER: B VERIFIES TIMESTAMP FRESHNESS
AND DECRYPTS TICKET USING SHARED KAB ``` **Reading the diagram:** The KDC issues a sealed *ticket* using A's pre-shared key $K_{AS}$. A forwards the ticket to B, who then mutually verifies freshness using a timestamp. This is the **Needham-Schroeder symmetric key protocol** simplified for one-way authentication. ### 4.2 Mutual Authentication Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[STEP 1: A SENDS NONCE NA AND IDB TO KDC] --> A2[STEP 2: KDC RETURNS TICKET E_KAS_KAB_NA_IDB_TS PLUS E_KBS_KAB_IDA_TS] A2 --> A3[STEP 3: A FORWARDS E_KBS TICKET TO B] A3 --> A4[STEP 4: B EXTRACTS KAB AND SENDS E_KAB_TS TO A] A4 --> A5[STEP 5: A RETURNS E_KAB_TS_PLUS_1 TO B] A5 --> A6[STEP 6: B VERIFIES FRESHNESS AND BOTH PARTIES HOLD SESSION KEY KAB] A1:::authStep A2:::authStep A3:::authStep A4:::authStep A5:::authStep A6:::finalStep classDef authStep fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B3D91 classDef finalStep fill:#CEEAD6,stroke:#188038,color:#0D5226 ``` **Block-level reading:** Each step is a **functional authentication primitive**. Steps 1–2 use the KDC; steps 3–6 are direct peer-to-peer exchanges. The final step confirms mutual possession of $K_{AB}$ and rule-out of replay. ### 4.3 Biometric Authentication Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[ENROLLMENT PHASE: CAPTURE BIOMETRIC TEMPLATE T] --> S2[HASH AND STORE T IN SECURE TEMPLATE DB] S2 --> S3[VERIFICATION PHASE: USER PRESENTS LIVE BIOMETRIC X] S3 --> S4[MATCHING ENGINE COMPUTES DISTANCE D X T] S4 --> S5{D LESS THAN THRESHOLD TH} S5 -- YES --> S6[ACCEPT IDENTITY AND ISSUE SESSION TOKEN] S5 -- NO --> S7[REJECT AND INCREMENT FAIL COUNTER] ``` **Reading the diagram:** $D(X,T)$ is typically the **Hamming distance** for iris codes or the **EER-tuned score** for fingerprints. The decision threshold $\tau$ is selected at the **Equal Error Rate (EER)** crossing point, where FAR = FRR. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** *Distinguish between **Peer Entity Authentication** and **Data Origin Authentication**. Give one real-world protocol example for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Peer Entity Authentication** confirms the identity of a communicating party during a *live session* — it assures B that the entity it is currently exchanging data with is indeed A, and not an impostor. *(1 mark)* > - **Data Origin Authentication** confirms the originator of a *specific data unit* — it assures B that the message $M$ was generated by A, but does not guarantee that A is still online. *(1 mark)* > - **Examples:** Peer entity — TLS handshake using server certificates / Kerberos AS exchange. Data origin — HMAC tag on a SWIFT message or a digitally signed email. *(1 mark)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, University Question Bank]** *List and briefly explain the **three authentication factors**. Why is 2FA considered stronger than single-factor password authentication?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The three authentication factors are: *(1)* **Knowledge factor** — something the user *knows* (password, PIN); *(2)* **Possession factor** — something the user *has* (smart card, OTP token); *(3)* **Inherence / Biometric factor** — something the user *is* (fingerprint, iris). *(2 marks)* > 2FA combines two **independent** factors, so an attacker compromising one (e.g., stealing a password via phishing) still cannot pass the second check (e.g., a TOTP from the user's phone). This exponentially raises attack cost. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **Needham-Schroeder symmetric-key authentication protocol** with a neat sequence diagram. List its strengths and the **Denning-Sacco attack** that it is vulnerable to. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **HMAC construction** in detail. Show the inner and outer hash steps mathematically and justify why HMAC is resistant to length-extension attacks. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) > **Step 1 — Participants and Pre-shared Keys.** Three parties: A (claimant), B (verifier), and a **Key Distribution Center (KDC)**. Pre-shared: $K_{AS}$ (between A and KDC) and $K_{BS}$ (between B and KDC). *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Protocol Messages.** > 1. $A \to \text{KDC}: ID_A \Vert ID_B \Vert N_A$ > 2. $\text{KDC} \to A: E_{K_{AS}}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_B \Vert N_A \Vert E_{K_{BS}}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_A]]$ > 3. $A \to B: E_{K_{BS}}[K_{AB} \Vert ID_A]$ > 4. $B \to A: E_{K_{AB}}[N_B]$ > 5. $A \to B: E_{K_{AB}}[N_B - 1]$ > *(2 marks)* > > **Step 3 — Verification Logic.** B decrypts the ticket in step 3 using $K_{BS}$ to retrieve $K_{AB}$. Steps 4–5 act as a handshake to confirm that A and B both possess the new session key $K_{AB}$. *(2 marks)* > > **Step 4 — Strengths.** Centralized key management, opaque tickets protect session keys, freshness via nonces prevents replay. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Denning-Sacco Attack.** If an old session key $K_{AB}^{\text{old}}$ is compromised, the attacker can replay step 3 to B and impersonate A, because the original protocol does not bind a *timestamp* to the ticket. Kerberos fixes this by adding timestamps. *(1 mark)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) > **Step 1 — Motivation.** Simple MACs like $H(K \Vert m)$ suffer from length-extension under Merkle–Damgård hashes. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Key Processing.** The secret key $K$ is hashed (if longer than block) or zero-padded (if shorter) to obtain $K_0$ of length equal to the block size $B$. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Inner Hash.** $S_{\text{inner}} = H[(K_0 \oplus ipad)\Vert m]$ where $ipad = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times. *(2 marks)* > > **Step 4 — Outer Hash.** $S_{\text{outer}} = H[(K_0 \oplus opad)\Vert S_{\text{inner}}]$ where $opad = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times. The final MAC tag is $S_{\text{outer}}$. *(2 marks)* > > **Step 5 — Length-Extension Resistance.** The input to the outer hash begins with the secret-derived $K_0 \oplus opad$, so an attacker cannot extend the message digest chain without recovering $K_0$. The provable security bound relates the forgery advantage to the collision resistance of $H$. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper 3]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Kerberos authentication architecture**. Explain the role of the **Authentication Server (AS)** and the **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A user submits a password $P = \texttt{`pass1234'}$ to a system that stores a **salted SHA-256** hash. The random salt is $s = \texttt{0xA5A5...}$ (16 bytes). If an attacker captures the database and uses a GPU to attempt $10^{11}$ guesses per second, estimate the time required to recover the password by brute force, assuming a uniform 8-character lowercase alphabet. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) > **Step 1 — Architecture.** Kerberos uses two trusted servers: the **AS** (issues Ticket-Granting Tickets after initial login) and the **TGS** (issues service tickets for specific application servers). All communication is encrypted using symmetric keys. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Kerberos Realms.** A *realm* is a logical authentication domain (e.g., `KTU.EDU.IN`). Cross-realm trust is established via inter-realm keys. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — AS Exchange.** A → AS: $\{ID_A, ID_{TGS}\}$. AS → A: $E_{K_A}[T_{GT} \Vert K_{A,TGS}]$ where $T_{GT}$ is the encrypted TGT containing $K_{A,TGS}$ and validity period. *(2 marks)* > > **Step 4 — TGS Exchange.** A → TGS: $\{T_{GT}, \text{Authenticator}, ID_B\}$. TGS → A: $E_{K_{A,TGS}}[K_{A,B} \Vert T_{B}]$ where $T_{B}$ is the service ticket for server B. *(2 marks)* > > **Step 5 — AP Exchange.** A → B: $\{T_{B}, \text{Authenticator}\}$. B verifies and grants access. AS + TGS together form the **KDC**. *(1 mark)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) > **Step 1 — Search Space.** Lowercase English alphabet has 26 letters. An 8-character password has: > $$N = 26^{8} = 208{,}827{,}064{,}576 \;\text{combinations} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{11}$$ > *(2 marks)* > > **Step 2 — Salt Countermeasure.** Salting does not enlarge the keyspace, but it forces the attacker to compute a fresh SHA-256 per salt value, eliminating the speedup from precomputed rainbow tables. Per-guess cost remains 1 SHA-256. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Brute-Force Time.** At $r = 10^{11}$ guesses per second: > $$t = \frac{N}{r} = \frac{2.09 \times 10^{11}}{10^{11}} = 2.09 \;\text{seconds (worst case)}$$ > Expected (average) time: > $$\bar{t} = \frac{N}{2r} = 1.05 \;\text{seconds}$$ > *(3 marks)* > > **Step 4 — Conclusion.** Even with salting, lowercase 8-character passwords are **cryptographically dead**. A 12-character mixed-case + digits + symbols password gives $\approx 95^{12} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{23}$ combinations, requiring about 17 million years at the same rate. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on Authentication Questions (per KTU 2024 valuation key):** > 1. **Confusing MAC with Digital Signature** — MACs provide *data origin authentication* but **no non-repudiation**; signatures do both. (–2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting the $K_0$ step in HMAC** — writing only $H(K \oplus ipad \Vert m)$ without the outer hash. The outer $H(K \oplus opad \Vert \cdot)$ is **mandatory**. (–1 mark) > 3. **Skipping the nonce timestamp window** in Needham-Schroeder — examiners expect explicit mention of freshness to counter replay. (–1 mark) > 4. **Writing `|` inside a markdown table cell** — this breaks the table. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX contexts. > 5. **Omitting Kerberos AS vs TGS distinction** — both are part of the KDC but have *different* functional roles. Don't conflate them. ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Authentication = identity assurance**; it is the *first* security service in the X.800 stack. - **Peer entity authentication** = live party; **Data origin authentication** = data unit creator. - **Three factors:** knowledge, possession, inherence. **2FA** is the practical baseline. - **HMAC** uses **two nested hashes** with $ipad = 0x36$ and $opad = 0x5C$; it defeats length-extension. - **Needham-Schroeder** is vulnerable to the **Denning-Sacco attack** if a session key is compromised and no timestamp is used — Kerberos fixes this. - **MAC ≠ Digital Signature.** MAC → symmetric, no non-repudiation. Signature → asymmetric, non-repudiable. - **Biometric** systems are evaluated via **FAR, FRR, and CER** (Equal Error Rate). - **Salting** prevents rainbow-table attacks but does **not** strengthen weak passwords — use length and complexity. - **RSA signature** equation: $S = M^{d} \bmod n$; **verify** with $S^{e} \bmod n = M$. - For KTU 2024, always draw a **block/sequence diagram** in any 7-mark question — it earns easy 1–2 marks.

topics.title: Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Access Control** is the foundational pillar of information security that mediates every request a subject makes to access a resource (object). It answers three critical questions in sequence: *Who are you?* (Identification & Authentication), *What are you allowed to do?* (Authorization), and *Did you actually do it?* (Accountability/Auditing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "Access control is the security service that ensures that access to resources is controlled and limited to authorized users based on predefined security policies, thereby enforcing the CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) triad."

The **Reference Monitor Concept** (Anderson, 1972) defines the abstract machine that mediates all access by subjects to objects, enforcing the access control policy. It must satisfy three properties:

- **Complete Mediation:** Every access must be checked (no bypassed paths).
- **Tamper-Proof:** The monitor itself cannot be modified by malicious subjects.
- **Verifiable:** The mechanism must be small enough to be exhaustively tested.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Hotel Key Card System**
>
> Imagine a luxury hotel. The **receptionist (Authentication server)** checks your ID and gives you a **key card (token)**. Your key card only opens the rooms on the 5th floor for a specific duration (3 nights). The **central locking system (Access Control Mechanism / Reference Monitor)** checks every swipe:
> - *Who* swiped the card? (Subject identity)
> - *Which* room are they trying to open? (Object)
> - *Does* their access level allow opening this room? (Policy check)
> - The system *logs* every attempt (Audit trail).
>
> Even if you forge a key, the **tamper-proof** card reader in the elevator prevents you from skipping floors. This is exactly how a robust access control system works in operating systems, databases, and networks.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Subject** | An active entity (user, process, program) requesting access |
| **Object** | A passive resource (file, database, hardware) being accessed |
| **Principal** | The human or system entity on whose behalf operations are performed |
| **Permission/Right** | An authorized mode of access (read, write, execute) |
| **Policy** | The high-level rules defining what is allowed/denied |
| **Mechanism** | The low-level software/hardware that enforces the policy |
| **Reference Monitor** | The trusted enforcing component |
| **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** | The totality of protection mechanisms responsible for enforcing security policy |

### 1.4 The AAA Framework

All access control sits on the three pillars of **AAA**:

1. **Authentication** – Establishes identity (*"You are Alice"*).
2. **Authorization** – Determines access rights (*"Alice can read file X but not file Y"*).
3. **Accountability** – Tracks actions via audit logs (*"Alice read file X at 10:42 PM"*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without **strong authentication**, access control fails. Without **logging**, accountability is impossible, and forensics becomes a guessing game.

### 1.5 Access Control Triad — Subject, Object, Access Right

The fundamental triplet: $(s, o, r)$ where $s$ is the subject, $o$ is the object, and $r$ is the access right. An access is granted **if and only if** the policy tuple $(s, o, r) \in P$, where $P$ is the policy set.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Step Operational Flow

A complete access decision follows this structured pipeline:

**Step 1 — Identification:** The subject claims an identity (e.g., username "alice"). This is a *declaration*, not a proof.

**Step 2 — Authentication:** The system verifies the claimed identity using one or more factors:
- *Something you know* (password, PIN) → **Knowledge factor**
- *Something you have* (smart card, OTP token) → **Possession factor**
- *Something you are* (fingerprint, iris scan) → **Biometric/Inherence factor**
- Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) combines ≥ 2 factors, increasing the security from $1 - (p_1 \times p_2)$ in compromise probability.

**Step 3 — Authorization & Audit:** Post-authentication, the access control mechanism compares the $(s, o, r)$ tuple against the policy database. If permitted, the operation executes and is logged.

### 2.2 The Generalized Access Control Model

The **Lampson Access Matrix** (1971) is the canonical formal model. It is a 2D matrix:

- **Rows** = Subjects $\{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$
- **Columns** = Objects $\{O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_m\}$
- **Cell $M[S_i, O_j]$** = Set of rights (r, w, x, own) that $S_i$ has on $O_j$

### 2.3 Realization of the Access Matrix

Storing a full $n \times m$ matrix is memory-inefficient. Two principal realizations exist:

| Realization | Description | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Control List (ACL)** | One column per object; each list enumerates subjects + their rights | Object-centric, file systems (e.g., Linux `chmod`, Windows NTFS) |
| **Capability List (C-List)** | One row per subject; each list enumerates objects + their rights | Subject-centric, distributed systems (e.g., Kerberos tickets) |
| **Authorization Table** | Triples $(s, o, r)$ as rows; relational database query | Large-scale systems with sparse matrices |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Access Decision | $(s, o, r) \in P \Rightarrow \text{GRANT}$ | Core policy evaluation |
| Bell-LaPadula (Confidentiality) | $\text{No Read Up (NRU)}$: $L(s) \ge L(o)$ for *read*; $\text{No Write Down (NWD)}$: $L(s) \le L(o)$ for *write* | Military/Government data |
| Biba Model (Integrity) | $\text{No Read Down (NRD)}$: $L(s) \le L(o)$; $\text{No Write Up (NWU)}$: $L(s) \ge L(o)$ | Financial systems, critical infra |
| Clark-Wilson | Constrained + unconstrained data items; well-formed transactions | Commercial data integrity |
| Chinese Wall (Brewer-Nash) | Access history-based conflict-of-interest separation | Consulting/legal sectors |
| RBAC Role Hierarchy | $r_1 \ge r_2 \Rightarrow \text{permissions}(r_2) \subseteq \text{permissions}(r_1)$ | Enterprise hierarchies |
| Separation of Duty (SoD) | $\text{users}(r_A) \cap \text{users}(r_B) = \emptyset$ | Fraud prevention (cashier ≠ auditor) |
| Authentication Compromise (MFA) | $P_{\text{break}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Security risk estimation |
| Access Matrix Cardinality | $\vert M \vert = n \times m \times \vert R \vert$ bits | Storage estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> For Chinese Wall: if a subject has accessed objects in *Company A* (a Conflict of Interest class), they **cannot subsequently** access objects in any *other* company in that class. This is a dynamic, history-based restriction.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Access control models are not academic exercises. They power:

- **Operating Systems:** Windows uses ACLs (NTFS), Linux uses POSIX ACLs + capabilities.
- **Databases:** Row-level security, view-based access.
- **Cloud (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC):** Role + attribute-based hybrid models.
- **Network Security:** Firewalls use ACLs on packet flows; 802.1X uses port-based NAC.
- **Industrial Control (ICS/SCADA):** Mandatory access control for safety-critical zones.
- **Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207):** "Never trust, always verify" — continuous access evaluation using ABAC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Multi-Factor Authentication Compromise Probability

**Problem Setup:** A banking system uses 2-factor authentication: a 4-digit PIN (knowledge) and an OTP via SMS (possession). The PIN space is $10^4$ and the OTP space is $10^6$. Both are uniformly random. An attacker tries to compromise both factors in a single guess session.

**Step 1:** Define individual compromise probabilities.

$$
p_{\text{PIN}} = \frac{1}{10^4} = 10^{-4}
$$

$$
p_{\text{OTP}} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2:** For the attacker to succeed, both must be guessed correctly. Assuming independence:

$$
P_{\text{break}} = p_{\text{PIN}} \times p_{\text{OTP}} = 10^{-4} \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. The combined brute-force space becomes $10^4 \times 10^6 = 10^{10}$, exponentially larger than either factor alone.

$$
\text{Effective keyspace} = 10^4 \times 10^6 = 10^{10}
$$

$$
\text{Equivalent entropy bits} = \log_2(10^{10}) = 10 \times \log_2(10) \approx 33.22 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4:** For $n$ factors with probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$:

$$
P_{\text{break}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

$$
\text{Total entropy} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_2(p_i) \text{ bits}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The "MFA compromise" derivation is a *favorite* KTU question. Students often forget to convert to **bits of entropy** using $\log_2$, which is the metric the examiner expects.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DAC vs MAC vs RBAC vs ABAC

| Feature | **DAC (Discretionary)** | **MAC (Mandatory)** | **RBAC (Role-Based)** | **ABAC (Attribute-Based)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Who decides policy?** | Resource owner | Central security officer | Security officer (role definitions) | Policy engine (dynamic) |
| **Basis for decision** | Subject identity & ownership | Subject clearance + object classification | Subject's assigned role(s) | Subject, Object, Action, Environment attributes |
| **Policy flexibility** | High (owner can change) | Low (fixed lattice) | Medium (role-engineered) | Very High (context-aware) |
| **Best for** | Collaborative file sharing | Military / classified data | Enterprises, hospitals | Cloud, IoT, Zero Trust |
| **Granularity** | Per-object | Coarse (lattice levels) | Per-role | Per-attribute (fine-grained) |
| **Administration cost** | High in large orgs | Low (centralized) | Low (role reuse) | Medium (policy authoring) |
| **Example** | Linux file permissions | SELinux, military systems | AWS IAM roles, SAP | XACML-based systems |
| **Limitation** | Trojan horse, confused deputy | Inflexible, hard to scale | Role explosion | Policy complexity, latency |

### 3.3 Derivation: Bell-LaPadula Confidentiality Constraint

**Setup:** A system with security levels $\mathcal{L} = \{ \text{Unclassified} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{Top Secret} \}$, ordered as:

$$
L(\text{Unclassified}) < L(\text{Confidential}) < L(\text{Secret}) < L(\text{Top Secret})
$$

**Bell-LaPadula Axioms:**

$$
\text{(Simple Security / NRU)}: \text{ Subject } s \text{ can READ object } o \iff L(s) \ge L(o)
$$

$$
\text{(Star Property / NWD)}: \text{ Subject } s \text{ can WRITE object } o \iff L(s) \le L(o)
$$

**Worked Example:**

- Alice has clearance **Secret** → $L(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret}$
- File `report.txt` is classified **Confidential** → $L(\text{report.txt}) = \text{Confidential}$

Since $\text{Secret} \ge \text{Confidential}$: Alice **may READ** `report.txt`. ✅

Since $\text{Secret} \not\le \text{Confidential}$: Alice **may NOT WRITE** to `report.txt`. ❌ (Prevents leakage of higher-classified data to lower objects.)

> [!NOTE]
> The "No Write Down" rule prevents a *Secret*-cleared user from *writing* into a *Confidential* file, which would effectively leak secret information to readers of lower clearance. This is the cornerstone of BLP.

### 3.4 Derivation: Role Hierarchy in RBAC

**Setup:** Define a partial order $\ge$ on roles where $r_1 \ge r_2$ means $r_1$ is *senior* to $r_2$.

**Senior role inherits junior role's permissions:**

$$
r_1 \ge r_2 \Rightarrow \text{permissions}(r_2) \subseteq \text{permissions}(r_1)
$$

**Worked Example:** In a hospital RBAC system:

- $\text{Doctor} \ge \text{Intern}$
- $\text{permissions}(\text{Intern}) = \{ \text{view\_patient\_record} \}$
- $\text{permissions}(\text{Doctor}) = \{ \text{view\_patient\_record}, \text{edit\_prescription}, \text{order\_lab\_tests} \}$

A doctor automatically inherits `view_patient_record` from the intern role. **Constraint of Separation of Duty:**

$$
\text{users}(\text{Cashier}) \cap \text{users}(\text{Auditor}) = \emptyset
$$

This prevents the same person from both recording and approving a financial transaction, mitigating internal fraud.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Access Control Decision Flow (Reference Monitor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Request] --> B[Identification: Claim Identity]
    B --> C[Authentication: Verify Identity]
    C --> D{Authentication Successful?}
    D -- No --> E[Access Denied - Log Failure]
    D -- Yes --> F[Build Access Request tuple s, o, r]
    F --> G[Reference Monitor Intercepts]
    G --> H[Query Policy Database]
    H --> I{s, o, r in Policy P?}
    I -- No --> J[Access Denied - Log Failure]
    I -- Yes --> K[Grant Access - Log Success]
    K --> L[Execute Operation on Object]
    L --> M[Audit Trail Updated]
    E --> M
    J --> M
    M --> N[End of Session or Repeat]
```

### 4.2 Access Matrix Realization Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Subjects
        S1[Subject S1]
        S2[Subject S2]
    end
    subgraph Objects
        O1[Object O1]
        O2[Object O2]
    end
    S1 -- "ACL for O1: S1 rw, S2 r" --> O1
    S2 -- "ACL for O2: S1 r, S2 rwx" --> O2
    S1 -. "Capability: O1 rw, O2 r" .-> S1
    S2 -. "Capability: O1 none, O2 rwx" .-> S2
```

### 4.3 Bell-LaPadula Lattice (Confidentiality Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TS[Top Secret - Level 4] --> SEC[Secret - Level 3]
    SEC --> CONF[Confidential - Level 2]
    CONF --> UNC[Unclassified - Level 1]
    TS -. "NRU: Subject at level 4 can read all lower levels" .-> UNC
    UNC -. "NWD: Subject at level 1 can write up" .-> TS
```

### 4.4 RBAC Role Hierarchy Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    EMP[Employee Role] --> DEV[Developer Role]
    EMP --> MGR[Manager Role]
    EMP --> AUD[Auditor Role]
    DEV --> SRDEV[Senior Developer Role]
    MGR --> DIR[Director Role]
    SRDEV -. "Inherits Developer permissions" .-> DEV
    DIR -. "Inherits Manager permissions" .-> MGR
    AUD -. "Separation of Duty: cannot be Manager" .-> MGR
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ABAC Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER[User with Attributes: dept, role, clearance] --> PEP[Policy Enforcement Point PEP]
    OBJ[Resource with Attributes: owner, classification, type] --> PEP
    ENV[Environment Attributes: time, IP, location] --> PEP
    PEP --> PDP[Policy Decision Point PDP]
    PDP --> PIP[Policy Information Point PIP - fetches missing attributes]
    PIP --> PDP
    PDP --> PAP[Policy Administration Point PAP - stores rules]
    PAP --> PDP
    PDP --> RESP[Decision: Permit or Deny]
    RESP --> PEP
    PEP --> EXEC[Execute or Block Access]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Trust & ABAC:** The architecture above is the basis of modern **XACML** (eXtensible Access Control Markup Language) and **NIST Zero Trust Architecture (SP 800-207)**. Every access decision considers subject, object, action, and environment — not just static roles.

### 4.6 Comparison: DAC vs MAC vs RBAC Decision Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    REQ[Access Request] --> Q1{Access Model?}
    Q1 -- DAC --> O[Check Object ACL on owner permissions]
    O --> D1[Owner-centric Decision]
    Q1 -- MAC --> M[Check Subject Clearance vs Object Classification]
    M --> D2[Lattice-based Central Decision]
    Q1 -- RBAC --> R[Check Subject Role Membership and Role Rights]
    R --> D3[Role-Centric Decision]
    D1 --> RES[Result]
    D2 --> RES
    D3 --> RES
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between Identification and Authentication in the context of access control. Why is authentication considered stronger than identification?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Identification is the process by which a subject **claims an identity** (e.g., providing a username like "alice"). It establishes *who the subject claims to be*. It is a declarative act and can be falsified trivially.

Authentication is the process of **verifying** the claimed identity using proof factors: *something you know* (password), *something you have* (token), or *something you are* (biometric). The system checks the presented credentials against stored reference data.

Authentication is stronger than identification because identification alone is just a *claim* with no proof, while authentication requires **evidence** that binds the subject to the claimed identity. Multi-factor authentication exponentially increases the cost of forgery.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is an Access Control List (ACL)? How does it differ from a Capability List?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Access Control List (ACL)** is a per-object data structure that stores the list of subjects authorized to access the object along with their permitted rights. Example: a file `report.docx` may have an ACL listing `(Alice: read, write)`, `(Bob: read)`.

A **Capability List (C-List)** is the dual: a per-subject data structure listing the objects the subject can access and with what rights. Example: Alice's capability list is `(report.docx: read, write)`, `(data.csv: read)`.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | ACL | Capability List |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Per object | Per subject |
| Efficient query | "Who can access this file?" | "What can this user access?" |
| Revocation | Easy (edit object's ACL) | Difficult (must find in every subject) |
| Use case | File systems, firewalls | Distributed systems, Kerberos |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bell-LaPadula Model** in detail. Define the security lattice, the simple security property, and the *-property. Illustrate with a worked example of how a *Secret*-cleared user interacts with a *Confidential* file.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Biba Model** for integrity. Compare it with Bell-LaPadula and show that Biba is, in fact, a "dual" of BLP.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Bell-LaPadula Model (7 marks):**

The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model, proposed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in **1973** for the U.S. Department of Defense, is a formal state-transition model that enforces **confidentiality** in computer systems.

**The Security Lattice (1 mark):**

The model defines a partially ordered set (poset) of security levels $\mathcal{L}$, for example:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \{\text{Unclassified} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{Top Secret}\}
$$

Each subject $s$ is assigned a *clearance* $L(s) \in \mathcal{L}$, and each object $o$ is assigned a *classification* $L(o) \in \mathcal{L}$. Categories (compartments) may also be used to enforce need-to-know.

**The Three Access Modes (1 mark):**

- *Read* (observe): the subject observes the object's content.
- *Write* (alter): the subject modifies the object.
- *Execute* (invoke): the subject runs the object as a process.

**Simple Security Property (No Read Up – NRU) (2 marks):**

A subject $s$ may **read** an object $o$ if and only if:

$$
L(s) \ge L(o)
$$

That is, a subject's clearance must dominate the object's classification. A *Secret*-cleared user can read *Unclassified*, *Confidential*, and *Secret* objects, but not *Top Secret* ones.

**Star Property (No Write Down – NWD) (2 marks):**

A subject $s$ may **write** to an object $o$ if and only if:

$$
L(s) \le L(o)
$$

This prevents a higher-cleared subject from writing classified information into a lower-classified object, where it could be read by users of lower clearance.

**Worked Example (1 mark):**

- Alice: $L(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret}$
- File `budget.docx`: $L(\text{file}) = \text{Confidential}$

Check **NRU**: $L(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret} \ge \text{Confidential}$ ✅ → Alice **can READ** the file.
Check **NWD**: $L(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret} \not\le \text{Confidential}$ ❌ → Alice **CANNOT WRITE** to the file (prevents leakage of Secret info into a Confidential container).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently *invert* the NWD rule. **No Write Down** means a high-cleared user *cannot* write into a low-classified object. They write into objects at *or above* their level. Memorize the mnemonic: **"Read UP, Write UP"** for higher-cleared users — you read *up* (anything ≤ your level) and write *up* (anything ≥ your level) — wait, the standard formulation is **No Read Up** (read *down* is allowed) and **No Write Down** (write *up* is allowed). Be careful with the directionality!

**Part (b) — Biba Model (7 marks):**

**Background (1 mark):** Proposed by Kenneth Biba in **1977**, the Biba Model enforces **integrity** rather than confidentiality. It is the integrity analog of BLP.

**Integrity Lattice (1 mark):**

Integrity levels are ordered from "low integrity" (untrusted) to "high integrity" (highly trusted):

$$
I(\text{Untrusted}) < I(\text{Somewhat Trusted}) < I(\text{Trusted}) < I(\text{Highly Trusted})
$$

**Biba's Two Key Properties (3 marks):**

1. **No Read Down (NRD):** A subject $s$ may read an object $o$ only if $I(s) \le I(o)$. This prevents low-integrity subjects from being "contaminated" by reading high-integrity data and potentially misusing it.

2. **No Write Up (NWU):** A subject $s$ may write to an object $o$ only if $I(s) \ge I(o)$. This prevents a low-integrity subject from corrupting a high-integrity object.

**Comparison: BLP vs Biba (2 marks):**

| Aspect | BLP | Biba |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| Read rule | NRU: $L(s) \ge L(o)$ | NRD: $I(s) \le I(o)$ |
| Write rule | NWD: $L(s) \le L(o)$ | NWU: $I(s) \ge I(o)$ |
| Direction | Reads flow down, writes flow up | Reads flow up, writes flow down |
| Use case | Military secrets | Financial records, OS kernels |

**Duality Proof (conceptual, 1 mark):**

If we substitute integrity levels for confidentiality levels and reverse the inequality directions, Biba's constraints emerge from BLP's by:

$$
I(s) \leftrightarrow L(s), \quad I(o) \leftrightarrow L(o), \quad \ge \leftrightarrow \le
$$

This is the formal sense in which Biba is a "dual" of BLP.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students write "Biba enforces confidentiality" — this is **wrong**. Biba enforces *integrity*. BLP enforces *confidentiality*. The examiner will deduct a full mark for this confusion.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** model as defined by the NIST standard (INCITS 359). Describe the four model components: Core RBAC, Hierarchical RBAC, Constrained RBAC, and Symmetric RBAC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Chinese Wall Model** (Brewer-Nash) with a real-world example. How does it combine confidentiality with conflict-of-interest protection?

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — RBAC Model (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), standardized by NIST (Sandhu et al., **1996**; ANSI INCITS 359-2012), is a **policy-neutral** access control model where permissions are assigned to **roles**, and users acquire permissions by being assigned to roles. This dramatically simplifies administration in large enterprises.

**Core RBAC (1.5 marks):**

Core RBAC defines the fundamental relations:

- **Users** ($U$): human or autonomous agents.
- **Roles** ($R$): job functions within the organization.
- **Permissions** ($P$): approvals to perform operations on objects.
- **Sessions** ($S$): a mapping of a user to a subset of roles they activate.

Formally:

$$
UA \subseteq U \times R \quad \text{(User-Role Assignment)}
$$

$$
PA \subseteq P \times R \quad \text{(Permission-Role Assignment)}
$$

**Hierarchical RBAC (1.5 marks):**

Defines a partial order $\ge$ on roles such that senior roles inherit junior role permissions:

$$
r_1 \ge r_2 \Rightarrow \text{permissions}(r_2) \subseteq \text{permissions}(r_1)
$$

Two variants: *General* (any partial order, multiple inheritance) and *Limited* (single inheritance chain).

**Constrained RBAC (1.5 marks):**

Adds **Separation of Duty (SoD)** constraints:
- *Static SoD (SSD)*: enforced at user-role assignment time, two mutually exclusive roles cannot both be assigned to the same user.
- *Dynamic SoD (DSD)*: enforced at session activation, a user cannot activate two conflicting roles in the same session.

**Symmetric RBAC (1.5 marks):**

Permission-Role Review: For each role, the set of users with that role can be determined, and vice versa. This is required for accountability audits.

**Real-World Example:**

A hospital RBAC system:
- Role `Doctor` → permissions: `view_patient`, `write_prescription`
- Role `Nurse` → permissions: `view_patient`, `update_vitals`
- Role `Receptionist` → permissions: `schedule_appointment` (cannot view patient details)

Senior `Surgeon` role inherits `Doctor` permissions and adds `perform_surgery`.

**Part (b) — Chinese Wall Model (7 marks):**

**Background (1 mark):**

The Chinese Wall Model, proposed by **Brewer and Nash in 1989**, was designed for consultancy and financial advisory firms where conflict-of-interest rules must dynamically restrict access.

**Three-Level Hierarchy (2 marks):**

- **Objects**: Individual data items (e.g., a single company's financial file).
- **Company Groups (CG)**: All objects related to a single company.
- **Conflict of Interest Classes (COI)**: Sets of company groups that compete (e.g., `{Bank A, Bank B, Bank C}` in investment banking).

**Access Rules (3 marks):**

1. **Simple Security Rule:** A subject $S$ can read object $O$ only if either:
   - $O$ is in the same company group as some object $S$ has already accessed, **OR**
   - $O$ belongs to a COI class that $S$ has never accessed before.

2. **Confinement Property (*-property):** A subject $S$ can write to an object $O$ only if $S$ has read *no other object* whose company group is different from $O$'s company group. This prevents information laundering between competing firms.

**Real-World Example (1 mark):**

A consulting analyst at a Big-4 firm has been assigned to audit **Company X (a bank)**. After accessing Company X's records, the analyst **cannot** subsequently access records of any other bank in the same COI class (e.g., Company Y, also a bank). They can, however, access records of a non-competing company (e.g., a manufacturing firm) in a different COI class.

The Chinese Wall is **dynamic** — unlike MAC's static lattice, the policy adapts based on the subject's access history.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse *Company Group* with *COI Class*. A COI class contains multiple company groups. The rule operates at the **company-group level** for writes and at the **COI-class level** for reads. Mixing these up costs 2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Access Control**

- ✅ **AAA Framework** = Authentication + Authorization + Accountability.
- ✅ **Reference Monitor** must be *Complete*, *Tamper-Proof*, and *Verifiable* (Anderson 1972).
- ✅ **Access Matrix** rows = subjects, columns = objects, cells = rights (Lampson 1971).
- ✅ **ACL** = per-object, **Capability** = per-subject, **Authorization Table** = triples.
- ✅ **DAC** = owner decides; **MAC** = central authority (lattice); **RBAC** = role decides; **ABAC** = attributes + context decide.
- ✅ **BLP (Confidentiality):** NRU + NWD. Mnemonic: "Read up the lattice, write up the lattice" — actually *read down*, *write up*. **No Read Up, No Write Down**.
- ✅ **Biba (Integrity):** NRD + NWU. Dual of BLP. Prevents contamination.
- ✅ **Clark-Wilson:** Well-formed transactions, separation of duty, focused on commercial integrity.
- ✅ **Chinese Wall (Brewer-Nash):** History-based, COI classes, dynamic policy.
- ✅ **RBAC Hierarchy:** Senior role inherits junior role's permissions.
- ✅ **SoD Constraint:** $U(r_1) \cap U(r_2) = \emptyset$ for mutually exclusive roles.
- ✅ **MFA Compromise:** $P_{\text{break}} = \prod p_i$. Two factors exponentially raise attacker cost.
- ✅ **Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207):** Continuous authentication, least privilege, ABAC-driven.
- ✅ **Common confusions to avoid:**
  - BLP ↔ Biba (confidentiality vs integrity)
  - NRU ↔ NRD (read direction)
  - Company Group ↔ COI Class in Chinese Wall
  - DAC owner can grant — but cannot override MAC lattice in hybrid systems.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Access Control (PERAT756)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Confidentiality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Confidentiality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Confidentiality** is a foundational pillar of information security that guarantees that private information is not made available or disclosed to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes. It ensures that data is protected from intentional or accidental unauthorized access throughout its lifecycle — at rest, in transit, and during processing.

In the context of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability), confidentiality is the **first pillar** that prevents sensitive information from falling into the wrong hands. In cryptographic terms, confidentiality is achieved through the transformation of plaintext into ciphertext using mathematical algorithms (ciphers) and secret keys.

$$ C = E_K(P) $$

Where:
- $P$ = **Plaintext** (original readable data)
- $C$ = **Ciphertext** (encrypted output)
- $E_K$ = **Encryption function** parameterized by key $K$
- $K$ = **Secret Key** (the parameter governing the transformation)

The corresponding decryption operation recovers the plaintext:

$$ P = D_K(C) $$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sealed Envelope

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are writing a confidential love letter. You place the letter inside an envelope and **seal** it with a unique **wax stamp** that only your recipient possesses the matching mold for. Even if a mail carrier or neighbor intercepts the envelope during transit, they cannot read the contents because they lack the unique seal pattern (the **key**). This is precisely how cryptographic confidentiality works: the **envelope** is the **encryption algorithm**, the **letter** is the **plaintext**, the **wax seal pattern** is the **key**, and the **sealed envelope** is the **ciphertext**.

### 1.3 Core Pillars of Data Confidentiality

| Pillar | Mechanism | Real-World Engineering Use |
|--------|-----------|----------------------------|
| **Data Encryption** | AES, RSA, ChaCha20 | HTTPS web traffic, disk encryption |
| **Access Control** | ACLs, RBAC, ABAC | Operating systems, databases |
| **Data Masking** | Tokenization, redaction | Production logs, payment systems |
| **Steganography** | Hidden channel embedding | Covert military communications |

### 1.4 Standard Confidentiality Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The strength of a confidentiality mechanism is measured by:
> - **Key Length ($L_K$)** — measured in **bits** (128, 192, 256 for AES)
> - **Entropy ($H$)** — measured in **bits per symbol**
> - **Work Factor ($W$)** — average time/computation to break the cipher
> - **Diffusion & Confusion** — qualitative properties defined by **Claude Shannon (1949)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Confidentiality Threat Model

Confidentiality operates under a precise adversary model. Consider an attacker **Eve** who:
1. **Eavesdrops** on the communication channel (passive attack).
2. **Tampers** with the message stream (active attack).
3. Possesses **bounded computational power** (Kerckhoffs's Principle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883):** *A cryptosystem should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is public knowledge.* Modern cryptographic design follows this principle rigorously.

### 2.2 Classification of Confidentiality Mechanisms

#### A. Symmetric-Key (Secret-Key) Cryptography
A **single shared secret key** $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption:

$$ C = E_K(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_K(C) $$

Examples: **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)**, **DES**, **3DES**, **ChaCha20**.

**Key Distribution Problem:** With $n$ users, the number of unique keys required is:

$$ N_{keys} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} $$

#### B. Asymmetric-Key (Public-Key) Cryptography
Two mathematically related keys exist: a **public key** $K_{pub}$ for encryption and a **private key** $K_{priv}$ for decryption.

$$ C = E_{K_{pub}}(P) \quad \text{and} \quad P = D_{K_{priv}}(C) $$

Examples: **RSA**, **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)**, **ElGamal**.

#### C. Hybrid Cryptography
Combines the efficiency of symmetric ciphers with the secure key exchange of asymmetric ciphers — used universally in **TLS/SSL**.

### 2.3 Shannon's Properties of a Good Cipher

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffusion:** Each plaintext digit should influence many ciphertext digits (propagates bit changes).
>
> **Confusion:** The relationship between key and ciphertext should be as complex as possible (obscures statistical patterns).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Description | Engineering Unit |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|------------------|
| Plaintext → Ciphertext | $C = E_K(P)$ | Encryption function | — |
| Ciphertext → Plaintext | $P = D_K(C)$ | Decryption function | — |
| Symmetric Key Count | $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Number of pairwise keys | dimensionless |
| Brute-Force Attempts | $A = 2^{L_K}$ | Total possible keys | attempts |
| Information Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Uncertainty of source | bits/symbol |
| XOR Operation | $P \oplus K \oplus K = P$ | Self-inverse property | — |
| Work Factor (AES-128) | $W \approx 2^{127}$ | Avg. operations to break | operations |
| Modular Exponentiation | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | RSA encryption | — |
| RSA Key Recovery | $n = p \cdot q$ | Modulus factorization | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** The XOR operator $\oplus$ is the atomic building block of nearly all modern stream and block ciphers. Its **self-inverse property** means encryption and decryption are mathematically identical operations when the same key is reused in a stream cipher.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Data confidentiality is not theoretical — it underpins:
- **Banking:** Protecting card PINs and transaction data (PCI-DSS compliance).
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-mandated encryption of patient health records.
- **Defense:** Secure communication between command and field units.
- **Cloud Computing:** Customer data isolation in multi-tenant environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brute-Force Search Space for AES-128

The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** with a **128-bit key** allows any of $2^{128}$ possible keys to be used. To derive this formally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Key Length in bits} &= L_K = 128 \\
\text{Each bit can take} &= 2 \text{ values} \quad (0 \text{ or } 1) \\
\text{Total number of key combinations} &= \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots \times 2}_{128 \text{ times}} \\
&= 2^{128}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expressing this in decimal:

$$
2^{128} \approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38} \text{ possible keys}
$$

This is the **brute-force search space** an attacker must exhaust to break AES-128 by trial.

### 3.2 Derivation: Self-Inverse Property of XOR (Foundation of Stream Ciphers)

The **XOR** (exclusive OR) operator is foundational to data confidentiality. We prove its self-inverse property:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & \text{plaintext } P, \text{ key } K \\
\text{Encryption: } & C = P \oplus K \\
\text{Decryption: } & P' = C \oplus K \\
\text{Substituting } C: & P' = (P \oplus K) \oplus K \\
\text{XOR is associative, so:} & P' = P \oplus (K \oplus K) \\
\text{Since } K \oplus K = 0 \text{ (bit cancels):} & P' = P \oplus 0 \\
\text{Since } x \oplus 0 = x: & P' = P \\
\therefore \quad P' &= P \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Symmetric Encryption over an 8-bit Block

Consider plaintext $P = \texttt{01101001}$ and key $K = \texttt{10110010}$.

**Step 1 — Encryption (XOR with key):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \texttt{01101001} \\
K &= \texttt{10110010} \\
C = P \oplus K &= \texttt{11011011}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission:** $C = \texttt{11011011}$ is sent over the (insecure) channel.

**Step 3 — Decryption (XOR with same key):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \texttt{11011011} \\
K &= \texttt{10110010} \\
P = C \oplus K &= \texttt{01101001}
\end{aligned}
$$

The original plaintext is recovered **bit-perfect**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Symmetric Key Distribution Problem

If $n = 5$ users need to communicate pairwise using symmetric keys:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \binom{n}{2} = \binom{5}{2} = \frac{5!}{2!(5-2)!} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 4}{2 \times 1} \\
&= \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ unique keys}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n = 100$ users, this balloons to **4,950 unique keys** — illustrating the **key distribution problem** that asymmetric cryptography was invented to solve.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Cryptography

| Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| Key Count (n users) | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $2n$ |
| Speed | **Very Fast** (AES ≈ GB/s) | **Slow** (RSA ≈ MB/s) |
| Key Length | 128–256 bits | 2048–4096 bits |
| Confidentiality Use | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| Example | AES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC |
| Vulnerability | Key distribution | Factoring (RSA), DLP (ECC) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Confidentiality within the CIA Triad

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeCIA["CIA TRIAD"]
    nodeC["CONFIDENTIALITY"]
    nodeI["INTEGRITY"]
    nodeA["AVAILABILITY"]
    nodeSym["SYMMETRIC CIPHERS AES DES 3DES"]
    nodeAsym["ASYMMETRIC CIPHERS RSA ECC ELGAMAL"]
    nodeHash["HASH FUNCTIONS SHA256 MD5"]
    nodeMAC["MAC AND HMAC"]
    nodeFW["FIREWALLS"]
    nodeRED["REDUNDANCY"]
    nodeCDN["CDN AND LOAD BALANCERS"]

    nodeCIA --> nodeC
    nodeCIA --> nodeI
    nodeCIA --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeSym
    nodeC --> nodeAsym
    nodeI --> nodeHash
    nodeI --> nodeMAC
    nodeA --> nodeFW
    nodeA --> nodeRED
    nodeA --> nodeCDN
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Confidentiality System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodePlain["PLAINTEXT DATA P"]
    nodeAlgo["ENCRYPTION ALGORITHM E_K"]
    nodeKey["SECRET KEY K"]
    nodeCipher["CIPHERTEXT C OVER INSECURE CHANNEL"]
    nodeAtk["ATTACKER EVE PASSIVE OR ACTIVE"]
    nodeDec["DECRYPTION ALGORITHM D_K"]
    nodeKey2["SECRET KEY K SHARED SECURELY"]
    nodeOut["RECOVERED PLAINTEXT P"]

    nodePlain --> nodeAlgo
    nodeKey --> nodeAlgo
    nodeAlgo --> nodeCipher
    nodeCipher --> nodeAtk
    nodeAtk --> nodeCipher
    nodeCipher --> nodeDec
    nodeKey2 --> nodeDec
    nodeDec --> nodeOut
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing a Confidentiality Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["DATA TO PROTECT"]
    nodeQ1["IS IT BULK DATA LARGER THAN 1 KB"]
    nodeSym["USE SYMMETRIC AES 256"]
    nodeQ2["IS SECURE KEY EXCHANGE NEEDED"]
    nodeAsym["USE ASYMMETRIC RSA 2048 OR ECC P 256"]
    nodeHybrid["USE HYBRID TLS HANDSHAKE"]
    nodeFinal["CIPHERTEXT READY FOR TRANSMISSION"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|"YES"| nodeSym
    nodeQ1 -->|"NO"| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|"YES"| nodeAsym
    nodeQ2 -->|"NO, KEY EXCHANGE REQUIRED"| nodeHybrid
    nodeSym --> nodeFinal
    nodeAsym --> nodeFinal
    nodeHybrid --> nodeFinal
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

**Q1. Define data confidentiality. List any two mechanisms used to ensure data confidentiality.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Data confidentiality is the property of information security that ensures private data is not disclosed to unauthorized entities. It guarantees that sensitive information is accessible only to parties with proper authorization.
>
> **Two mechanisms:**
> 1. **Encryption** — transforming plaintext into ciphertext using algorithms such as AES or RSA.
> 2. **Access Control** — restricting access through ACLs, RBAC, or biometric authentication.
>
> **[3 Marks: Definition (1) + Two Mechanisms (1 each)]**

---

**Q2. State and briefly explain Kerckhoffs's Principle.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883) states that the security of a cryptosystem must lie entirely in the secrecy of the key, not in the secrecy of the algorithm. The algorithm may be published, analyzed, and peer-reviewed; only the key must remain secret.
>
> **Significance:** This principle enables open cryptographic standardization (e.g., AES was selected via an open NIST competition) and allows public cryptanalysis to validate strength.
>
> **[3 Marks: Statement (2) + Significance (1)]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose 3–4 marks by (a) confusing **confidentiality with integrity**, (b) skipping the **key distribution problem** in symmetric crypto, and (c) failing to give **real-world examples**. Always anchor definitions to the **CIA Triad** and cite at least one standard algorithm.

---

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the concept of data confidentiality. Discuss symmetric and asymmetric cryptographic techniques with suitable examples. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition:** Data confidentiality ensures that information is not disclosed to unauthorized parties during storage, transmission, or processing. It is the **first pillar** of the CIA Triad.
>
> **Symmetric Cryptography:**
> - Uses a **single shared secret key** $K$ for both encryption $C = E_K(P)$ and decryption $P = D_K(C)$.
> - **Example:** AES-128, AES-256, ChaCha20.
> - **Strength:** Very fast; suitable for bulk data encryption (e.g., full-disk encryption with BitLocker).
> - **Weakness:** Key distribution problem — $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ keys needed for $n$ users.
>
> **Asymmetric Cryptography:**
> - Uses a **key pair**: public key $K_{pub}$ (for encryption) and private key $K_{priv}$ (for decryption).
> - **Example:** RSA (based on integer factorization), ECC (based on elliptic curve DLP).
> - **Strength:** Solves key distribution; enables digital signatures.
> - **Weakness:** Computationally expensive (~1000× slower than AES).
>
> **Comparison & Real-World Use:** HTTPS uses **hybrid** cryptography — RSA/ECC for key exchange, AES for bulk data encryption.
>
> **[7 Marks: Definition (1) + Symmetric (2) + Asymmetric (2) + Comparison/Example (2)]**

**(b) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the encryption-decryption model. Explain why Kerckhoffs's Principle is fundamental to modern cryptography. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Block Diagram of Encryption Model:**
>
> ```
>  Plaintext P  --->  [Encryption Algo E_K]  --->  Ciphertext C  ---> (Insecure Channel)  --->  [Decryption Algo D_K]  --->  Plaintext P
>                          ↑                                              ↑
>                       Key K                                          Key K
> ```
>
> - The plaintext $P$ is fed into the encryption algorithm $E$ along with key $K$.
> - The algorithm produces ciphertext $C$, transmitted over an insecure channel where an attacker may intercept it.
> - The legitimate receiver applies the decryption algorithm $D$ with the same (symmetric) or paired (asymmetric) key to recover $P$.
>
> **Kerckhoffs's Principle:**
> - Stated by Auguste Kerckhoffs in 1883: *"The security of a cryptosystem should not depend on keeping the algorithm secret."*
> - Only the **key** must be kept secret; the algorithm may be public.
> - **Why fundamental?** Enables peer review, open standardization (AES, SHA), public cryptanalysis to find flaws, and avoids the catastrophic failure of proprietary "security through obscurity" systems.
>
> **[7 Marks: Diagram (3) + Encryption-Decryption Explanation (2) + Kerckhoffs's Principle (2)]**

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. Explain with an example how a hybrid cryptosystem (like TLS) combines both. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Differences:**
>
> | Aspect | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
> |--------|-----------|------------|
> | Keys Used | Single shared key | Key pair (public + private) |
