> | Speed | Very fast (hardware accelerated) | Computationally slow |
> | Key Distribution | Insecure over open channels | Public key freely shared |
> | Scalability | Poor ($O(n^2)$ keys) | Excellent ($O(n)$ keys) |
> | Confidentiality + Auth? | Confidentiality only | Both (with signatures) |
> | Example | AES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC |
>
> **Hybrid Cryptosystem (TLS Example):**
> 1. Client connects to server (HTTPS).
> 2. Server sends its **public key** (RSA/ECC) inside a digital certificate.
> 3. Client generates a random **session key** $K_s$ (symmetric).
> 4. Client encrypts $K_s$ with the server's **public key** and sends it back.
> 5. Both parties now share $K_s$ securely.
> 6. All subsequent bulk data (webpages, files) is encrypted using **AES-256** with $K_s$.
>
> This gives the **efficiency of symmetric** crypto for data and the **secure key exchange of asymmetric** crypto.
>
> **[7 Marks: Differentiation Table (3) + TLS Hybrid Steps (4)]**

**(b) Define entropy in the context of information theory. Compute the entropy of a source that emits four symbols A, B, C, D with probabilities 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125 respectively. [7 Marks]**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition:** Entropy $H(X)$ measures the average amount of information (uncertainty) produced by a stochastic source of data, expressed in **bits per symbol**.
>
> Shannon's formula:
>
> $$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i $$
>
> **Computation:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> H(X) &= -\left[ p_A \log_2 p_A + p_B \log_2 p_B + p_C \log_2 p_C + p_D \log_2 p_D \right] \\
> &= -\left[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 \right] \\
> &= -\left[ 0.5 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.125 \times (-3) + 0.125 \times (-3) \right] \\
> &= -\left[ -0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375 \right] \\
> &= -\left[ -1.75 \right] \\
> &= 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Interpretation:** Each symbol from this source carries **1.75 bits** of information on average. Higher entropy = harder to compress = greater confidentiality potential (since patterns are less predictable).
>
> **[7 Marks: Definition (2) + Formula (1) + Step-by-step Calculation (3) + Interpretation (1)]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Data Confidentiality**

- ☐ **Confidentiality** = prevention of unauthorized disclosure; **first pillar of CIA Triad**.
- ☐ **Encryption** transforms $P \rightarrow C$ via $E_K$; **Decryption** reverses it via $D_K$.
- ☐ **Kerckhoffs's Principle:** Security rests on the **key**, not the algorithm.
- ☐ **Symmetric crypto** uses one shared key; fast; suffers from **key distribution problem** ($N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ keys).
- ☐ **Asymmetric crypto** uses a key pair ($K_{pub}, K_{priv}$); solves distribution; slower.
- ☐ **Hybrid systems (TLS)** combine RSA/ECC for key exchange + AES for bulk data.
- ☐ **AES-128** has a brute-force keyspace of $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$.
- ☐ **XOR** ($\oplus$) is self-inverse: $P \oplus K \oplus K = P$ — foundational to stream ciphers.
- ☐ **Shannon's properties:** Diffusion + Confusion define cipher strength.
- ☐ **Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ quantifies source uncertainty in bits/symbol.
- ☐ Standard confidentiality algorithms: **AES (128/192/256)**, **RSA (2048+)**, **ECC (P-256/P-384)**, **ChaCha20**.
- ☐ Real-world deployments: HTTPS/TLS, VPNs (IPsec), full-disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault), messaging apps (Signal Protocol).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Integrity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Integrity** in the context of secure communication refers to the property that ensures that data has not been altered, modified, or tampered with in an unauthorized or accidental manner during its transit across a communication channel or during storage. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PERAT756 – Secure Communication**, data integrity is one of the three pillars of the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** and is typically achieved through the use of cryptographic primitives such as **hash functions**, **Message Authentication Codes (MACs)**, and **digital signatures**.

Mathematically, a data integrity mechanism guarantees that if a sender transmits a message $M$ and a verifier computes an integrity tag $T = f(M, K)$ (where $K$ is a secret key in symmetric schemes, or a private key in asymmetric schemes), then any unauthorized modification $M' \neq M$ will result in $f(M', K) \neq T$ with overwhelmingly high probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PERAT756 / Module 1**
> Data integrity is the **assurance that information has not been modified in transit**. The three primary cryptographic tools used in the KTU syllabus to enforce data integrity are:
> 1. **Cryptographic Hash Functions** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2 family, SHA-3)
> 2. **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** — including **HMAC** and **CMAC**
> 3. **Digital Signatures** (RSA-DSA, ECDSA, ElGamal signature scheme)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tamper-Evident Wax Seal"

Imagine a medieval king sending a royal decree across his kingdom in a parchment scroll. To prevent any messenger or thief from modifying the contents, the king rolls the scroll, pours melted wax on the binding, and presses his **royal ring (a unique pattern)** into the wax. The wax seal has two key properties:

- **It is a "fingerprint" of the message** — the seal is computed based on the contents of the scroll.
- **Any tampering is immediately visible** — if a thief opens the scroll to alter the text, the wax breaks, and the seal is destroyed. The recipient instantly knows the message has been compromised.

A **cryptographic hash** is precisely this "digital wax seal." It is a fixed-length digital fingerprint of arbitrary-length data. Any single bit change in the original message completely transforms the hash output (the famous **Avalanche Effect**), making tampering immediately detectable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Distinction**
> - **Data Integrity** = "Has the message been changed?" (Detects accidental & malicious modification)
> - **Data Origin Authentication** = "Did this message really come from Alice?" (Verifies sender's identity)
> - **Non-Repudiation** = "Alice cannot later deny sending it." (Provides legal proof)
> 
> These three properties are often **confused by students**, but they are **distinct goals** that may require different cryptographic tools. A plain hash function provides **only integrity**, while a MAC provides **integrity + origin authentication**, and a digital signature provides **integrity + origin authentication + non-repudiation**.

## 1.3 Standard Security Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hash Output Length | $n$ bits | 128 (MD5), 160 (SHA-1), 256/384/512 (SHA-2) | Number of bits in the digest |
| Collision Resistance | $2^{n/2}$ | $2^{64}$ for 128-bit hash | Work to find a collision via birthday attack |
| Preimage Resistance | $2^{n}$ | $2^{128}$ for 128-bit hash | Work to invert the hash |
| Second Preimage Resistance | $2^{n}$ | $2^{128}$ | Work to find a 2nd input with same hash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bit-length $n$** of the hash output is the single most important security parameter. The KTU examiner expects you to know that for a hash of $n$ bits, collision resistance is only $2^{n/2}$ (birthday bound), NOT $2^{n}$. This is a **favourite KTU question** and a common valuation pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Data Integrity (Detailed)

### 2.1.1 Cryptographic Hash Functions

A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input of arbitrary finite length to a fixed-length output (the *digest* or *fingerprint*). It must satisfy the following properties:

**Property 1 — Preimage Resistance (One-Wayness):**
For any given hash value $h = H(M)$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find an input $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$.

**Property 2 — Second Preimage Resistance:**
Given a specific input $M_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find a different input $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

**Property 3 — Collision Resistance:**
It must be computationally infeasible to find **any two distinct inputs** $M_1$ and $M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Properties?** A common KTU trick question asks: "If a hash is collision-resistant, is it also preimage resistant?" The answer is **YES (theoretically)** — but the converse is **NOT true**. Collision resistance is the *strongest* of the three properties. MD5 is broken for collisions (2004, Wang et al.) but **NOT yet fully broken for preimages**.

**Property 4 — Avalanche Effect:**
A small change (e.g., flipping one bit) in the input should drastically change the output. Formally, for random $M$ and $M'$ differing in 1 bit, approximately **50% of the output bits** of $H(M)$ and $H(M')$ should differ.

**Property 5 — Determinism:**
The same input $M$ must always produce the same hash value $H(M)$. This is essential for verification.

### 2.1.2 Message Authentication Codes (MAC)

A **MAC** is a keyed hash function. The sender computes $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ using a **shared secret key** $K$, and the receiver recomputes the MAC on the received message and compares it. The MAC provides both **integrity** and **origin authentication** (only holders of $K$ can produce a valid tag).

The standard construction taught in KTU Module 1 is **HMAC (Hash-based MAC)**:

$$H_{K}(M) = H\Big((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M)\Big)$$

where:
- $H$ = underlying hash function (e.g., SHA-256)
- $K$ = shared secret key
- $ipad$ = inner pad = byte $0x36$ repeated to block size
- $opad$ = outer pad = byte $0x5C$ repeated to block size

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite**: HMAC's security is **provably reducible** to the security of the underlying hash function. Even if SHA-256's collision resistance were partially weakened, HMAC-SHA-256 would still be secure as long as the compression function remains strong. This is why HMAC is the **industry standard** (used in TLS, IPsec, JWT).

### 2.1.3 Digital Signatures

A **digital signature** is the **public-key equivalent** of a MAC. The sender uses a **private key** to sign, and anyone with the corresponding **public key** can verify. This provides **integrity + authentication + non-repudiation** — the strongest of the three guarantees.

General flow:
- **Sign:** $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK}(M)$ or $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK}(H(M))$ (signing the hash is the practical approach)
- **Verify:** $\text{Verify}_{PK}(M, \sigma) = \text{Valid} \text{ or } \text{Invalid}$

## 2.2 The Merkle–Damgård Construction

Most legacy hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2) follow the **Merkle–Damgård iterative structure**, which is essential KTU content.

**Steps of the Construction:**

1. **Padding:** The input message $M$ is padded so that its length becomes a multiple of the block size $b$ (e.g., 512 bits for SHA-1, 1024 bits for SHA-512). Padding includes appending a "1" bit, then "0" bits, and finally the 64-bit (or 128-bit) representation of the original message length.

2. **Parsing:** The padded message is split into $L$ blocks: $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_L$, each of size $b$ bits.

3. **Iterative Compression:** Each block is processed by a **compression function** $f$ that takes the current $b$-bit chaining variable $H_{i-1}$ and the next message block $M_i$ and outputs the new chaining variable $H_i$:
   $$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$$
   This starts with an **Initial Vector (IV)** $H_0$ (a fixed constant published in the standard) and ends with the final hash value $H_L$.

4. **Output:** The final value $H_L$ is the hash digest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: SHA-3 (Keccak) does **NOT** use Merkle–Damgård. It uses a **sponge construction**, which is structurally different and immune to **length-extension attacks** that plague MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-2. The KTU examiner may ask you to **compare the two** in a 7-mark question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU ESE** — The following table contains every formula you are likely to be tested on for Module 1 (Data Integrity).

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Parameter | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Preimage Resistance (work to find) | $W_{\text{pre}} = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = hash length in bits | One-wayness guarantee |
| 2 | Second Preimage Resistance | $W_{\text{2nd}} = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = hash length | Prevents forging alternate messages |
| 3 | Collision Resistance (Birthday) | $W_{\text{coll}} \approx 2^{n/2}$ | $n$ = hash length | **Smaller than preimage!** |
| 4 | Birthday Attack Trials | $k \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{n}}$ | Expected #hashes to find collision | Used to break MD5 (n=128) in $2^{64}$ |
| 5 | Merkle–Damgård Step | $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ | $H_0 = \text{IV}$ | Core of MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2 |
| 6 | HMAC Construction | $H_K(M) = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | block size $B$ | TLS, IPsec, FIPS-198 |
| 7 | HMAC Output Size | Equals hash output $n$ bits | $n$ = 256 for HMAC-SHA256 | Standard tag length |
| 8 | SHA-256 Block Size | $B = 512$ bits | 16 words of 32 bits | Compression function input |
| 9 | SHA-256 Hash Size | $n = 256$ bits = 32 bytes | 8 words of 32 bits | Digest output |
| 10 | SHA-512 Block Size | $B = 1024$ bits | 16 words of 64 bits | High-security variant |
| 11 | SHA-512 Hash Size | $n = 512$ bits = 64 bytes | 8 words of 64 bits | High-security digest |
| 12 | SHA-256 Rounds | 64 rounds | Round constants $K_t$ | Internal mixing |
| 13 | MD5 Digest Size | $n = 128$ bits | 4 words of 32 bits | **Cryptographically broken — do not use** |
| 14 | SHA-1 Digest Size | $n = 160$ bits | 5 words of 32 bits | Deprecated since 2011 (SHAttered attack, 2017) |
| 15 | Digital Sign Length | $\vert \sigma \vert = n$ (key size in bits) | RSA, ECDSA | Non-repudiation token |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Git** uses **SHA-1** (transitioning to SHA-256) to detect tampering of source code commits.
> - **TLS 1.3** uses **HMAC-SHA-256** for the handshake integrity.
> - **Bitcoin** uses **double SHA-256 (SHA-256(SHA-256(x)))** for block hashing.
> - **Digital certificates (X.509)** use **SHA-256** in their signatures.
> - **AWS S3** uses **MD5 / SHA-256** ETags to verify uploaded data integrity.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why is it needed? |
|---|---|
| Padding with length | Prevents **length-extension attacks** and ensures the last block encodes the message length. |
| Chaining variable $H_{i-1}$ | Ensures that the hash of the **entire message** depends on **every block** — flipping one bit propagates the change everywhere. |
| Compression function $f$ | The actual mixing core; it must be a **pseudorandom function (PRF)** for the overall hash to be secure. |
| Public fixed IV | Allows anyone to recompute the hash deterministically for verification. |
| Key in HMAC | Without a key, anyone can compute the hash. The key restricts tag generation to authorized parties. |
| Two nested hashes in HMAC | Defends against length extension on the underlying Merkle–Damgård hash. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Birthday Paradox for Hash Collisions

The **birthday attack** is the most important quantitative concept in data integrity, and the KTU examiner **frequently tests** the student's ability to compute the number of trials needed to find a collision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Background**: The birthday paradox states that in a group of just 23 random people, there is a **>50% probability** that two share the same birthday. By the same logic, finding a collision in an $n$-bit hash requires only about $2^{n/2}$ trials, not $2^n$.

### 3.1.1 Probability That All $k$ Hashes Are Distinct

The probability that $k$ randomly chosen outputs from a space of size $N = 2^n$ are **all distinct** is:

$$P_{\text{distinct}}(k) = \prod_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{N-i}{N} = \frac{N \cdot (N-1) \cdot (N-2) \cdots (N-k+1)}{N^{k}}$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$P_{\text{distinct}}(k) = \frac{N}{N} \cdot \frac{N-1}{N} \cdot \frac{N-2}{N} \cdots \frac{N-k+1}{N}$$

$$= 1 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2}{N}\right) \cdots \left(1 - \frac{k-1}{N}\right)$$

**Approximation for small $k$ relative to $N$:** Using the Taylor expansion $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$\ln P_{\text{distinct}}(k) \approx -\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{i}{N} = -\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\text{distinct}}(k) \approx e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)}$$

**Probability of at least one collision:**

$$P_{\text{collision}}(k) = 1 - P_{\text{distinct}}(k) \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)}$$

### 3.1.2 Solving for the 50% Threshold

Set $P_{\text{collision}} = 0.5$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} \implies e^{-k(k-1)/(2N)} = 0.5$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693$$

$$\frac{k(k-1)}{2N} \approx 0.693$$

For large $k$, $k-1 \approx k$, so:

$$\frac{k^{2}}{2N} \approx 0.693 \implies k^{2} \approx 1.386 \cdot N \implies k \approx 1.177\sqrt{N}$$

Since $N = 2^{n}$:

$$k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: To find a collision in an $n$-bit hash with probability ≥ 50%, an attacker needs only about $\mathbf{1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}}$ hash evaluations. For $n = 128$ (MD5), this is roughly $1.177 \cdot 2^{64} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{19}$ operations — feasible for state-level attackers (Wang et al. actually did it in 2004).

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Question:** A company uses MD5 ($n = 128$ bits) to verify the integrity of software updates. (a) How many trial messages must an attacker generate to find a collision with 50% probability? (b) The company upgrades to SHA-256 ($n = 256$ bits). How many trials are needed now? (c) Comment on the security improvement.

### Part (a) — MD5 ($n = 128$)

Using the birthday formula:

$$k_{\text{MD5}} \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{128}} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{64}$$

Numerically:

$$2^{64} = 1.8447 \times 10^{19}$$

$$k_{\text{MD5}} \approx 1.177 \times 1.8447 \times 10^{19} \approx 2.17 \times 10^{19} \text{ trials}$$

### Part (b) — SHA-256 ($n = 256$)

$$k_{\text{SHA-256}} \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{256}} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{128}$$

$$2^{128} = 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$$

$$k_{\text{SHA-256}} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{38} \text{ trials}$$

### Part (c) — Security Comparison

The **ratio** of work is:

$$\frac{k_{\text{SHA-256}}}{k_{\text{MD5}}} = \frac{1.177 \cdot 2^{128}}{1.177 \cdot 2^{64}} = 2^{64}$$

This is approximately $1.8 \times 10^{19}$ — a **19 quintillion-fold** increase in work. SHA-256 offers $2^{64}$ times stronger collision resistance than MD5 because **doubling the hash output length squares the attacker's work**. This is the single most important quantitative fact about hash functions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Integrity Mechanisms

| Property | Plain Hash | MAC (HMAC) | Digital Signature | Encryption (AES) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Provides Integrity | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Implicitly (but not standardized) |
| Provides Authentication | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Provides Non-Repudiation | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Requires Shared Secret | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (symmetric K) | ❌ No (uses PKI) | ✅ Yes (symmetric K) |
| Key Type | None | Symmetric | Asymmetric | Symmetric |
| Speed | Very Fast | Fast | Slower (RSA), Fast (ECDSA) | Fast |
| Verifiable by | Anyone | Only key holder | Anyone with public key | Only key holder |
| KTU Example | SHA-256 | HMAC-SHA-256 | RSA-SHA256, ECDSA | AES-GCM (provides AEAD) |
| Protects Confidentiality | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trick Question**: "Does encryption provide integrity?" The **strict academic answer is NO** — encryption is designed for confidentiality. However, certain **authenticated encryption modes** like **AES-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode)** and **ChaCha20-Poly1305** provide **Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)**, which gives both confidentiality AND integrity in a single primitive. The KTU 2024 syllabus touches on this in Module 4 (not Module 1), but a 3-mark question may reference it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The General Data Integrity Verification Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Sender Alice] --> nodeM1[Original Message M]
    nodeM1 --> nodeA[Hash Function H or MAC Algorithm]
    nodeK[Secret Key K] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeT[Integrity Tag T]
    nodeM1 --> nodeB[Concatenate]
    nodeT --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC[Transmitted Message M plus T over Insecure Channel]
    nodeC --> nodeR[Receiver Bob]
    nodeR --> nodeM2[Received Message M prime]
    nodeR --> nodeT2[Received Tag T prime]
    nodeM2 --> nodeCMP[Recompute Tag using same K and H]
    nodeK2[Same Secret Key K] --> nodeCMP
    nodeCMP --> nodeCALC[New Tag T calc]
    nodeT2 --> nodeEQ[Comparator: T calc equals T prime]
    nodeCALC --> nodeEQ
    nodeEQ --> nodeOK[Tag Matches Integrity Verified Message Authentic]
    nodeEQ --> nodeFAIL[Tag Mismatch Integrity Violation Discard Message]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Merkle–Damgård Iterative Hash Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMSG[Input Message M] --> nodePAD[Step 1 Padding append 1 bit zeros length encoding]
    nodePAD --> nodeSPLIT[Step 2 Split into L blocks M1 M2 ML of size b bits]
    nodeSPLIT --> nodeIV[Step 3 Initialize chaining variable H0 equals IV fixed constant]
    nodeIV --> nodeF1[Step 4 Compression Function f H i minus 1 M i]
    nodeSPLIT --> nodeF1
    nodeF1 --> nodeH1[H1 intermediate state b bits]
    nodeH1 --> nodeF2[Compression Function f H1 M2]
    nodeSPLIT --> nodeF2
    nodeF2 --> nodeH2[H2 intermediate state b bits]
    nodeH2 --> nodeFDOT[Continue for all L blocks]
    nodeFDOT --> nodeHL[Final Hash H L equals the digest n bits]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — HMAC Nested Hash Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeM[Message M] --> nodeXOR1[Concatenate with K XOR ipad]
    nodeK[Secret Key K] --> nodeXOR1
    nodePAD1[ipad inner pad 0x36 repeated to block size B] --> nodeXOR1
    nodeXOR1 --> nodeHIN[Apply Inner Hash H to the result]
    nodeHIN --> nodeXOR2[Concatenate with K XOR opad]
    nodeK2[Same K] --> nodeXOR2
    nodePAD2[opad outer pad 0x5C repeated to block size B] --> nodeXOR2
    nodeXOR2 --> nodeHOUT[Apply Outer Hash H to the result]
    nodeHOUT --> nodeTAG[HMAC Tag T of n bits output]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Attack Classification on Hash Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Attacker Goal] --> nodeB{What does the attacker know?}
    nodeB -->|Only a target hash h| nodeC[Preimage Attack: Find M such that HM equals h. Work 2 to the n]
    nodeB -->|A specific message M1| nodeD[Second Preimage Attack: Find M2 not equal M1 such that HM1 equals HM2. Work 2 to the n]
    nodeB -->|Nothing: free choice| nodeE[Collision Attack: Find any M1 M2 with HM1 equals HM2. Work 2 to the n over 2 via Birthday Paradox]
    nodeC --> nodeEX1[Example: Inverting SHA 256 to recover a password]
    nodeD --> nodeEX2[Example: Forging an alternate software update with same MD5 checksum]
    nodeE --> nodeEX3[Example: SHAttered attack 2017 produced two PDF files with identical SHA 1 hash]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Digital Signature Lifecycle (Integrity + Non-Repudiation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM[Original Document M] --> nodeH[Step 1 Compute Hash h equals H of M using SHA 256]
    nodeH --> nodeSIG[Step 2 Sign with Private Key SK: sigma equals Sign SK of h]
    nodeSK[Alice Private Key SK] --> nodeSIG
    nodeSIG --> nodeSEND[Step 3 Send M plus sigma to Bob over insecure channel]
    nodeSEND --> nodeBOB[Step 4 Bob receives M prime and sigma prime]
    nodeBOB --> nodeVH[Step 5 Bob computes h prime equals H of M prime]
    nodeBOB --> nodeVSIG[Step 6 Bob uses Alice Public Key PK to recover h from sigma: h recovered equals Verify PK of sigma]
    nodeVH --> nodeCMP[Step 7 Compare h prime with h recovered]
    nodeVSIG --> nodeCMP
    nodeCMP --> nodeOK[Match: Integrity OK and Alice cannot deny signature Non Repudiation Achieved]
    nodeCMP --> nodeFAIL[Mismatch: Message was tampered OR signature is invalid REJECT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams**
> When asked to "draw a block diagram of HMAC" in the exam, the examiner expects:
> 1. **Two boxes** for the two hash operations
> 2. **Clear labelling** of $K \oplus ipad$ and $K \oplus opad$
> 3. **Arrow directions** showing the data flow (innermost hash first, then outer)
> 4. The **block size $B$** and **hash output $n$** annotated
> 
> Mermaid diagrams may not be accepted in handwritten exams — adapt these to **labelled rectangular blocks with arrows** when writing on paper.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing confidentiality with integrity** — encryption ≠ integrity. Students often write "AES provides integrity" in answer sheets. **Mark deduction: −2 to −3 marks.**
> 2. **Writing $2^n$ instead of $2^{n/2}$ for collision resistance** — this is the **#1 valuation trap**. Always use the birthday bound.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the key in HMAC** — if your HMAC diagram doesn't show the secret key, you lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Calling SHA-1 "secure"** — SHA-1 was **shattered** in 2017 (Google + CWI Amsterdam). Always state its **deprecated** status.
> 5. **Confusing digital signatures with MACs** — MACs use **symmetric** keys (shared secret); signatures use **asymmetric** keys (private/public pair). Mixing these up costs heavy marks.
> 6. **Not specifying hash length** — saying "use SHA-256" without stating $n = 256$ bits is considered an incomplete answer.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define data integrity. List any three cryptographic mechanisms used to ensure data integrity in modern communication systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data integrity is the property that guarantees that data has not been altered, modified, or destroyed in an unauthorized manner during transmission across a communication channel or during storage. It ensures that the received data is exactly what was sent, bit-for-bit.

**Three mechanisms (1 mark each, any three):**

1. **Cryptographic Hash Functions** — A hash function $H$ maps variable-length input $M$ to a fixed-length digest $h = H(M)$. Any modification to $M$ produces a completely different $h$ (avalanche effect). Examples: SHA-256, SHA-3.

2. **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** — A keyed hash function $\text{MAC}_K(M)$ that uses a shared secret key $K$ to produce a tag. The receiver recomputes the MAC and compares. Example: **HMAC-SHA-256**.

3. **Digital Signatures** — A public-key primitive where the sender signs using a private key $SK$ and the receiver verifies using the corresponding public key $PK$. Provides integrity, authentication, **and non-repudiation**. Example: **RSA-PSS, ECDSA**.

*(Alternative acceptable answers: Authenticated Encryption modes such as AES-GCM, CMAC, or Poly1305.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the avalanche effect? Why is it a critical property of a cryptographic hash function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The avalanche effect is the property of a cryptographic hash function whereby a small change in the input (e.g., flipping a single bit) causes a drastic and unpredictable change in the output digest. Ideally, for a one-bit change in the input, approximately **50% of the output bits** should flip.

**Why it is critical (1.5 marks):**

1. **It ensures collision resistance** — if small input changes produced small output changes, an attacker could systematically search for collisions. The avalanche effect makes the function behave like a random oracle, preventing such searches.

2. **It enables tamper detection** — even a single-bit modification to a transmitted message results in a completely different hash. The verifier can detect this with overwhelming probability.

3. **It prevents partial-match attacks** — without the avalanche effect, an attacker who knows $H(M_1)$ could predict $H(M_2)$ for similar $M_2$. The avalanche effect destroys this correlation.

**Example:** SHA-256 of "Hello" = `185f8db32271fe25f561a6fc938b2e26...` while SHA-256 of "hello" (lowercase h) = `2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e...` — completely different despite a 1-bit difference in ASCII encoding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format**: Part B questions are **14-mark** problems with **internal choice** (i.e., the student chooses between two alternative questions, OR the question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks). Below, both alternatives **A** and **B** are provided with complete sub-parts and step-by-step model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the **HMAC algorithm**. State clearly the role of $ipad$ and $opad$, and explain why HMAC uses **two nested** hash operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses a 160-bit hash function. **(i)** Calculate the number of trials an attacker would need on average to find a collision with 50% probability. **(ii)** If the system is upgraded to a 256-bit hash, by what factor does the attacker's work increase? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — HMAC Algorithm (7 marks)

**HMAC Construction (2 marks):**

The HMAC of a message $M$ using a secret key $K$ and an iterated hash function $H$ is defined as:

$$H_{K}(M) = H\Big((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M)\Big)$$

where:
- $K$ is the secret key, padded with zeros to the block size $B$ of the hash function (e.g., $B = 512$ bits for SHA-256, $B = 1024$ bits for SHA-512).
- $ipad$ = the byte $0x36$ repeated $B$ times (inner pad).
- $opad$ = the byte $0x5C$ repeated $B$ times (outer pad).
- $\parallel$ denotes concatenation.
- $H$ is the underlying cryptographic hash function.

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

1. **Key preparation:** $K$ is extended to $B$ bits by appending zeros.
2. **XOR with $ipad$:** $K_{in} = K \oplus ipad$ is concatenated with message $M$ to form $(K_{in} \parallel M)$.
3. **Inner hash:** $H_{\text{inner}} = H(K_{in} \parallel M)$. This produces an $n$-bit output.
4. **XOR with $opad$:** $K_{out} = K \oplus opad$ is concatenated with $H_{\text{inner}}$ to form $(K_{out} \parallel H_{\text{inner}})$.
5. **Outer hash:** $H_{\text{final}} = H(K_{out} \parallel H_{\text{inner}})$. This is the HMAC tag of $n$ bits.

**Why two nested hash operations? (2 marks):**

The double hashing structure defends against **length-extension attacks** that are inherent to Merkle–Damgård hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2). The outer hash consumes the inner hash's output as a single block, so the attacker cannot append additional message blocks to extend the input. Additionally, the security of HMAC is **provably reducible** to the security of the underlying compression function $f$ — a strong theoretical guarantee.

**Roles of $ipad$ and $opad$:** $ipad$ binds the key to the message in the first hash, while $opad$ ensures the resulting digest is again bound to the key in the outer hash. The two different constant values ($0x36$ vs $0x5C$) ensure that the two stages of hashing are **cryptographically independent**.

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Birthday Attack Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Collisions in 160-bit hash (3 marks):**

Using the birthday formula with $n = 160$:

$$k \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^{n}} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{80}$$

Numerically:

$$2^{80} = 1.2089 \times 10^{24}$$

$$k \approx 1.177 \times 1.2089 \times 10^{24} \approx 1.42 \times 10^{24} \text{ trials}$$

**(ii) Upgrade to 256-bit hash (4 marks):**

For $n = 256$:

$$k_{256} \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{128}$$

$$2^{128} = 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$$

$$k_{256} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{38} \text{ trials}$$

**Factor of increase:**

$$\frac{k_{256}}{k_{160}} = \frac{1.177 \cdot 2^{128}}{1.177 \cdot 2^{80}} = 2^{128-80} = 2^{48}$$

$$2^{48} = 2.815 \times 10^{14} \approx 281 \text{ trillion}$$

**Conclusion:** Upgrading from 160-bit to 256-bit hash multiplies the attacker's work by a factor of $2^{48} \approx 2.8 \times 10^{14}$, making the attack **computationally infeasible** with current technology.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Compare and contrast the three primary mechanisms — **hash function**, **MAC**, and **digital signature** — for ensuring data integrity. Your answer must include the cryptographic keys used, the security properties provided, and at least one real-world protocol that uses each mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Merkle–Damgård construction** used in MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-2. With a diagram, describe the iterative compression process, and explain why this construction is vulnerable to **length-extension attacks**. Why is SHA-3 immune to this attack? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — Comparison of Mechanisms (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Hash Function | MAC | Digital Signature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key used** | None (keyless) | Symmetric shared secret $K$ | Asymmetric: private $SK$, public $PK$ |
| **Integrity** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Authentication** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (origin authentication) | ✅ Yes |
| **Non-repudiation** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (private key is unique to sender) |
| **Computation** | Very fast | Fast | Slower (RSA), fast (ECDSA) |
| **Verifiable by** | Anyone | Only key holder | Anyone with public key |
| **Standard example** | SHA-256 | HMAC-SHA-256 | RSA-PSS, ECDSA |
| **Real-world protocol** | Git commit hashes, BitTorrent, IPFS, Bitcoin | TLS 1.3 handshake, IPsec, JWT signing | X.509 certificates, PGP email, code signing |

**Detailed Explanation (2 marks):**

- A **hash function** is keyless, so it provides integrity but **cannot authenticate the source** — anyone can compute $H(M)$.
- A **MAC** uses a shared symmetric key, so only parties who hold $K$ can produce or verify the tag. This provides both integrity and origin authentication, but **cannot provide non-repudiation** because both parties share the same key (the receiver could have forged the message).
- A **digital signature** uses an asymmetric key pair. Only the sender (holder of $SK$) can produce $\sigma$, but anyone with $PK$ can verify. This **legally binds** the sender to the message, providing non-repudiation.

**Real-world examples:**
- Hash: **Git** uses SHA-1 (transitioning to SHA-256) to detect source-code tampering.
- MAC: **TLS 1.3** uses **HMAC-SHA-256** in its handshake for message integrity.
- Signature: **X.509 digital certificates** use **RSA-SHA256** or **ECDSA-SHA256** signatures to bind a public key to an identity.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — Merkle–Damgård Construction (7 marks)

**Merkle–Damgård Construction Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Padding the message $M$:** The original message is padded so that its total length is a multiple of the block size $b$ (e.g., $b = 512$ for SHA-1, $b = 1024$ for SHA-512). The padding consists of:
   - A single "1" bit
   - Followed by the minimum number of "0" bits needed
   - Followed by a 64-bit (or 128-bit) representation of the original message length
   
   This ensures the **last block encodes the message length**, thwarting trivial collision attacks.

2. **Parsing:** The padded message is split into $L$ blocks of $b$ bits each: $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_L$.

3. **Iterative compression:** A fixed **Initial Vector (IV)** $H_0$ is chosen (published in the standard). For each block, the compression function $f$ is applied:

$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$

The final value $H_L$ is the **n-bit hash digest**.

**Diagram (2 marks):** A horizontal flow chart showing $H_0 \to f \to H_1 \to f \to H_2 \to \cdots \to H_L$ with message blocks $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_L$ feeding into each $f$ (see **Mermaid Diagram 2 in Section 4.2** above).

**Vulnerability to Length-Extension Attack (0.5 mark):**

In a length-extension attack, given $H(M)$ and $\vert M \vert$ (the message length), the attacker can compute $H(M \parallel M')$ for some chosen $M'$ **without knowing $M$**. This works because the Merkle–Damgård construction reveals the internal state $H_L$ as the output, allowing the attacker to "extend" the chain by feeding in additional blocks.

**Why SHA-3 is immune (0.5 mark):**

SHA-3 (Keccak) uses a **sponge construction** with an **absorbing phase** and a **squeezing phase**. The internal state is **larger** than the output (e.g., 1600 bits state for 256-bit output), and the output is **truncated** from a state that still contains unused capacity. The attacker cannot reconstruct the full internal state from the output, so length-extension fails.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Data Integrity**

- ☐ **Data integrity** = assurance that data has not been modified in transit or storage.
- ☐ Three pillars of cryptographic integrity: **Hash, MAC, Digital Signature.**
- ☐ A **hash function** $H$ maps variable-length input to fixed-length output ($n$ bits).
- ☐ Five required properties of a hash: **preimage resistance, second-preimage resistance, collision resistance, deterministic, avalanche effect.**
- ☐ Collision resistance is **$2^{n/2}$** (birthday bound), **NOT $2^n$** — this is the most tested formula in KTU exams.
- ☐ The **birthday paradox** gives $k \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ trials to find a collision with 50% probability.
- ☐ **MD5** ($n = 128$) — **broken** for collisions since 2004. Do NOT use in new systems.
- ☐ **SHA-1** ($n = 160$) — **deprecated** since 2017 (SHAttered attack). Avoid.
- ☐ **SHA-256** ($n = 256$, $B = 512$) — current industry standard, secure.
- ☐ **SHA-512** ($n = 512$, $B = 1024$) — high-security variant.
- ☐ **SHA-3 (Keccak)** uses **sponge construction**, immune to length-extension.
- ☐ **Merkle–Damgård** is used by MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2; vulnerable to **length-extension**.
- ☐ **HMAC** = $H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ — used in TLS, IPsec, JWT.
- ☐ **$ipad = 0x36$** (repeated $B$ times), **$opad = 0x5C$** (repeated $B$ times).
- ☐ A **MAC** provides **integrity + authentication** (but **no non-repudiation**).
- ☐ A **digital signature** provides **integrity + authentication + non-repudiation**.
- ☐ **Digital signatures** use **asymmetric** keys (private/public); **MACs** use **symmetric** shared keys.
- ☐ **Encryption ≠ integrity.** Use authenticated encryption (AES-GCM) for both.
- ☐ **Avalanche effect** = ~50% of output bits flip when 1 input bit is flipped.
- ☐ Standard real-world use: Git (hash), TLS 1.3 (HMAC), X.509 (signatures), Bitcoin (double SHA-256).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-repudiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-repudiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Repudiation (NR)** is a cryptographic security service defined under the **ISO 7498-2 / ITU-T X.800** security architecture that provides **proof of the integrity and origin of data**, thereby preventing the sender from falsely denying having sent the message (**origin non-repudiation**) and preventing the recipient from falsely denying having received it (**receipt non-repudiation**).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, *PERAT756 – Secure Communication* treats non-repudiation as one of the **five pillar security services** alongside Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, and Access Control. It is the only service that holds legal-admissible evidentiary value in commercial, financial, and e-governance transactions.

### 1.1.1 The Two Facets of Non-Repudiation

| Facet | Abbreviation | Guarantee Provided | Primary Mechanism |
|:------|:-------------|:-------------------|:------------------|
| **Non-Repudiation of Origin** | **NRO** | The sender cannot deny having sent the message. | **Digital Signature** over the message digest |
| **Non-Repudiation of Receipt** | **NRR** | The recipient cannot deny having received the message. | **Acknowledgement token** signed by the recipient |
| **Non-Repudiation of Submission** | **NRS** | The sender cannot deny having submitted the message to a delivery authority. | TTP timestamped receipt |
| **Non-Repudiation of Delivery** | **NRD** | The recipient cannot deny the delivery authority handed over the message. | TTP delivery token |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Module 1 of *PERAT756* explicitly lists *Non-repudiation* under the foundational security services. Most Part A (3-mark) questions in ESE cycles target the **distinction between authentication and non-repudiation** — keep this contrast crisp in memory.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **Anand** and you hand a sealed, registered-post letter to your landlord, **Mr. Menon**, stating that you are vacating the flat on 30th June.

- If later Mr. Menon claims he **never received** the letter → the **postal receipt with his signature** proves he did.
- If later you claim you **never sent** the letter → the **postal register entry** (timestamp + your signature) proves you did.

> That postal receipt + post-office ledger is the *physical-world* equivalent of a **non-repudiation service**. In the digital world, the role of the post office is played by a **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** and the role of your signature is played by a **digital signature** rooted in **public-key cryptography**.

### 1.3 Why Authentication Alone Is NOT Enough

This is a **favourite KTU ESE question**. Authentication only proves *who* you are *at the moment of communication*. It does not produce **durable, verifiable, third-party-checkable evidence** that can be produced *later* in a dispute. Non-repudiation specifically generates this **persistent evidence token**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** *Authentication = proving identity to the other party live. Non-Repudiation = producing evidence that can be verified by any third party years later.*

## 1.4 Standardized Metrics & References

- **ISO/IEC 7498-2:1989** — Defines NR as a security service.
- **ISO/IEC 13888-1, 13888-2, 13888-3** — Three-part standard covering non-repudiation mechanisms using symmetric, asymmetric, and trusted-third-party techniques respectively.
- **ITU-T X.509** — Defines certificate structure that anchors digital signatures in a PKI.
- **RFC 4949** — Internet Security Glossary, formally defines NR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Actors in a Non-Repudiation Protocol

A non-repudiation protocol is a **three-party (or two-party with TTP-mediated) exchange** governed by cryptographic primitives. The actors are:

1. **Originator (O)** — The entity that originates the message $M$.
2. **Recipient (R)** — The entity intended to receive $M$.
3. **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** — A semi-trusted arbitration authority that generates, stores, and verifies evidence tokens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A TTP is **semi-trusted** in the cryptographic literature: it is trusted **not to collude** with either party, but the protocol is designed so that it **cannot unilaterally fabricate evidence** without the cooperation of the other party. This is the key to *fair* non-repudiation.

## 2.2 Building Blocks of a Non-Repudiation Service

The service is built by **stacking** the following primitives, each of which contributes a specific property:

| Layer | Cryptographic Primitive | Property Contributed | Why it is Needed |
|:------|:-----------------------|:---------------------|:-----------------|
| **L1** | **One-way Hash Function** $H(\cdot)$ (e.g., **SHA-256** producing **256-bit / 32-byte** digests) | Message integrity & fixed-size evidence | Avoids signing arbitrarily large messages |
| **L2** | **Asymmetric (Public-Key) Cryptography** $(pk_i, sk_i)$ | Sender authentication of evidence | Only the sender's $sk$ can produce the signature |
| **L3** | **Digital Signature Algorithm** (e.g., **RSA-PSS**, **ECDSA**, **EdDSA**) | Verifiable, transferable, non-reputable token | Combines L1 and L2 |
| **L4** | **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) + X.509 Certificates** | Binding of identity to public key | Prevents impersonation of public keys |
| **L5** | **Trusted Timestamping Authority (TSA)** | Proof of existence at a point in time | Defeats replays and "key-was-revoked" defences |
| **L6** | **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** | Arbitration & secure storage of evidence | Provides independent verification path |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The textbook difference between *integrity* and *non-repudiation* is that **integrity** can be guaranteed by a Message Authentication Code (MAC) using a **symmetric** key shared between two parties, but **non-repudiation requires an asymmetric** key operation because the verifying party must not possess the ability to forge the evidence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the LaTeX `\vert` delimiter inside tables to avoid breaking markdown.

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Components / Units |
|:-:|:--------|:-------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Hashing** | $d = H(M)$ | $M$ = message, $d$ = digest (e.g., **256 bits** for SHA-256) |
| 2 | **Signing** | $\sigma = Sign_{sk_O}(d) = Sign_{sk_O}(H(M))$ | $sk_O$ = sender's private key |
| 3 | **Verification** | $Verify_{pk_O}(d, \sigma) \in \{true, false\}$ | $pk_O$ = sender's public key |
| 4 | **Signature Size (RSA-2048 + SHA-256)** | $\vert \sigma \vert = 2048 \text{ bits} = 256 \text{ bytes}$ | Independent of $\vert M \vert$ |
| 5 | **Non-Repudiation Token of Origin (NRO token)** | $NR_{O} = \bigl( M,\ \sigma_{O},\ t_{O},\ Cert_{O} \bigr)$ | $\sigma_O = Sign_{sk_O}(H(M))$, $t_O$ = timestamp, $Cert_O$ = X.509 certificate |
| 6 | **Non-Repudiation Token of Receipt (NRR token)** | $NR_{R} = \bigl( H(M),\ \sigma_{R},\ t_{R},\ Cert_{R} \bigr)$ | $\sigma_R = Sign_{sk_R}(H(M))$, signed by recipient |
| 7 | **ElGamal Signature Equation** (verification form) | $r \equiv g^{k} \pmod{p}$ and $s \equiv k^{-1}(H(M) - x \cdot r) \pmod{p-1}$ | $p$ large prime, $g$ generator, $x$ private key |
| 8 | **RSA Signature Verification** | $m^{e} \equiv s \pmod{n}$ | $m = H(M)$, $s = \sigma$, $(e, n) = pk$ |
| 9 | **DSA Signature Verification** | $v \equiv g^{u_1 \cdot h} \cdot y^{u_2} \pmod{p}$ where $u_1, u_2$ derived from $r, s$ | $h = H(M)$ |
| 10 | **Certificate Validity Window** | $t_{now} \in [t_{notBefore},\ t_{notAfter}]$ | ISO 8601 timestamps inside X.509 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-repudiation is not an abstract academic concept — it underpins:

- **E-commerce contracts** — Every digitally signed PDF purchase order is a non-repudiable instrument admissible in court under India's *Information Technology Act, 2000* §10A and the EU's *eIDAS Regulation 910/2014*.
- **Banking and SWIFT messaging** — The **SWIFT PKI** issues certificates that allow banks to sign MT103 payment messages.
- **Code signing** — Microsoft Authenticode, Apple notarization, and Android APK signing v2/v3 all rely on NR.
- **Blockchain / cryptocurrency** — Every transaction carries a non-repudiable signature (e.g., **ECDSA over secp256k1** in Bitcoin) that is verifiable by any node in the network.
- **E-Governance** — Aadhaar e-KYC, GST filings, and MCA-21 ROC filings all embed digital signatures.

## 2.5 Operational Goals of an NR Protocol (Mark-Worthy)

> [!TIP]
> For a 7-mark "Explain non-repudiation" question, the examiner looks for the **four goals** below — quote them verbatim.

A complete non-repudiation protocol must satisfy:

1. **Non-repudiability** — Neither party can deny the exchange.
2. **Verifiability** — A third-party (judge/arbitrator) can independently verify the evidence.
3. **Fairness** — At the end of the protocol, either both parties have the required evidence or neither does.
4. **Timeliness** — Both parties can force the protocol to complete in bounded time without requiring the other's cooperation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Genesis of Evidence: Building a Non-Repudiation Token from Scratch

The following is the **full mathematical derivation** of the standard ISO/IEC 13888-1 non-repudiation token generation procedure. This is the **canonical worked example** for KTU Part B questions.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Message Hashing

Let $M$ be the message to be signed. The originator first computes a fixed-length message digest using a cryptographic hash function $H(\cdot)$ that satisfies **pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, and collision resistance** (the three security properties of a *strong* hash):

$$
d = H(M)
$$

> **Why?** Signing $M$ directly with an asymmetric algorithm is computationally expensive ($O(\vert M \vert^3)$ for RSA in the worst case). Hashing reduces the input to a constant size and ensures that any tampering with $M$ changes $d$ with overwhelming probability $1 - 2^{-n}$ where $n$ is the digest length.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Digital Signature Generation

The originator signs the digest $d$ using their private key $sk_O$:

$$
\sigma = Sign_{sk_O}(d) = Sign_{sk_O}\bigl( H(M) \bigr)
$$

For an **RSA** signature scheme with private key $sk_O = (d_{priv}, n)$ and public key $pk_O = (e, n)$:

$$
\sigma = d^{d_{priv}} \bmod n
$$

For an **ECDSA** signature scheme with private key $sk_O = d_{int}$ and curve parameters $(p, a, b, G, n, h)$:

$$
\sigma = (r, s), \quad r = (k \cdot G).x \bmod n, \quad s = k^{-1}(H(M) + d_{int} \cdot r) \bmod n
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Token Composition

The **Non-Repudiation Token of Origin (NRTO)** is then assembled as the tuple:

$$
NR_{O} = \bigl( M,\ \sigma,\ t_{O},\ C_{O},\ L_{O} \bigr)
$$

where

- $M$ = the message itself (or a secure reference to it)
- $\sigma$ = the digital signature
- $t_{O}$ = the trusted timestamp from the TSA
- $C_{O}$ = the X.509 certificate of the originator binding $pk_O$ to identity
- $L_{O}$ = a linkage information field containing $H(M)$, algorithm OID, and policy reference

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Transmission and Acknowledgement

The recipient, after verifying $\sigma$ against $pk_O$ and the certificate chain, produces their own token $NR_{R}$:

$$
NR_{R} = \bigl( H(M),\ \sigma_{R},\ t_{R},\ C_{R} \bigr)
$$

where $\sigma_{R} = Sign_{sk_R}\bigl( H(M) \bigr)$ is the recipient's signature over the same hash, certifying receipt.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Disputable Resolution

If a dispute arises, a **judge J** (acting as an arbitrator with access to the TTP) executes the following verification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5a: } & \text{Retrieve } C_{O} \text{ and verify chain to a trusted root CA.} \\
\text{Step 5b: } & \text{Confirm } t_{now} \in [t_{notBefore}(C_O),\ t_{notAfter}(C_O)] \text{ and that } t_{O} \text{ falls within that window.} \\
\text{Step 5c: } & \text{Recompute } d' = H(M) \text{ from the stored } M. \\
\text{Step 5d: } & \text{Compute } d'^{e} \bmod n \text{ and check that it equals } \sigma. \\
\text{Step 5e: } & \text{Accept the claim if all checks pass; reject otherwise.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Worthy Insight:** A common KTU examiner trick is to ask why we don't just use a MAC. The answer is precisely in Step 5d: the judge J must be able to verify $\sigma$ using only **public information** ($e, n$). A MAC requires the symmetric key $k$ which both parties hold, so either party could have produced the tag — defeating non-repudiation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Authentication vs. Integrity vs. Non-Repudiation

| Property | Confidentiality | Authentication | Integrity | Non-Repudiation |
|:---------|:---------------:|:--------------:|:---------:|:---------------:|
| **Hides content from third party** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Proves identity of sender** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Detects modification** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Evidence admissible to third party** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Achievable with symmetric crypto only** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (MAC) | ❌ (must use PKI) |
| **Provides time-of-existence proof** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (with TSA) |

## 3.3 Generation of Trust: The Role of a Trusted Third Party (TTP)

A **TTP** is a cryptographic authority trusted by *both* parties to perform one or more of the following:

- **Key certification** — Issuing X.509 certificates.
- **Timestamping** — Binding a hash to a wall-clock time.
- **Evidence storage** — Acting as a *Notary* for NR tokens.
- **Arbitration** — Resolving disputes by producing the stored evidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Single-Point-of-Failure Note:** A TTP is also a single point of failure and a high-value target for attackers. Modern protocols use **distributed trust**, **threshold cryptography** (e.g., a $(t, n)$-threshold signature scheme requiring $t$-of-$n$ TTPs to collude to forge), or **blockchain-anchored timestamps** to mitigate this.

## 3.4 Formal Verification of Fairness (Mark-Worthy Mathematical Statement)

Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a non-repudiation protocol. Define:

- $E_O^i$ = Evidence held by Originator after step $i$
- $E_R^j$ = Evidence held by Recipient after step $j$

Then **fairness** is formally stated as:

$$
\forall \text{ honest parties } P \in \{O, R\}, \quad P \text{ terminates with } E_P \iff \text{other party has } E_{other}
$$

and **timeliness** is:

$$
\forall i, \quad \exists t_{\max} \text{ such that step } i \text{ completes by time } t_{\max}
$$

These two properties together constitute the **Gürgens–Rudolph** formal definition (2005) used in the academic literature on NR protocols.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (for KTU numerical practice)

**Problem:** Sender uses **RSA-2048** with $e = 65537$, $n = pq$ where $p$ and $q$ are the secret primes. The hash of the message is $H(M) = 0x00ABCD1234\ldots$ (truncated to 256 bits and padded to 2048 bits via PKCS#1 v1.5 or PSS). Compute the signature size in bytes.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block size} &= 2048 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Signature size} &= 2048 / 8 = 256 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Hash size (SHA-256)} &= 256 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Total token overhead} &= 32 \text{ (hash) } + 256 \text{ (sig) } + 48 \text{ (cert) } + 8 \text{ (timestamp) } \\
&= 344 \text{ bytes per NR token}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This number (≈344 bytes) is exactly the kind of *compact derivation* that a KTU 14-mark question can ask you to reproduce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Non-Repudiation Protocol Flow

The Mermaid diagram below captures the **full message exchange** between Originator (O), Recipient (R), and Trusted Third Party (TTP), including the evidence tokens exchanged at each stage. All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant O as Originator O
    participant TTP as Trusted Third Party
    participant R as Recipient R
    participant J as Judge J

    O->>O: Step A: Compute d1 = H(M) using SHA-256
    O->>O: Step B: Sign d1 with sk_O to get sigma_O
    O->>TTP: Step C: Submit (M, sigma_O) for timestamping
    TTP-->>O: Step D: Return NRO token = (M, sigma_O, tO, CertO)
    O->>R: Step E: Send (M, NRO token) over the channel
    R->>R: Step F: Verify sigma_O using pk_O from CertO
    R->>R: Step G: Recompute d2 = H(M) and compare with d1
    R->>TTP: Step H: Request receipt acknowledgement and timestamp
    TTP-->>R: Step I: Return NRR token = (H(M), sigma_R, tR, CertR)
    R->>O: Step J: Deliver NRR token as proof of receipt

    Note over O,R: Normal flow complete: both hold evidence

    O--xR: Step K: Suppose R denies receipt (dispute)
    O->>J: Step L: Submit NRR token and NRO token to Judge
    J->>TTP: Step M: Verify tokens against stored records
    TTP-->>J: Step N: Return verification result true or false
    J-->>O: Step O: Adjudicate based on verified evidence
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NR Service

The diagram below shows the **functional layering** of an NR system. The originator side generates evidence; the recipient side verifies it; the TTP provides external anchoring.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORIGINATOR_SIDE[Originator Side - Sender]
        A1[Message M] --> A2[Hash Engine H of M]
        A2 --> A3[Signature Engine using sk_O]
        A3 --> A4[NRO Token Builder]
        A4 --> A5[Token = M, sigma_O, tO, CertO, L_O]
    end

    subgraph TRANSPORT[Transport Layer]
        A5 -. Signed Message .-> B1
    end

    subgraph RECIPIENT_SIDE[Recipient Side - Verifier]
        B1[Received Token] --> B2[Certificate Chain Validator]
        B2 --> B3[Hash Recomputation H of M]
        B3 --> B4[Signature Verification Verify pk_O]
        B4 --> B5{Valid}
        B5 -- Yes --> B6[Accept and Build NRR]
        B5 -- No --> B7[Reject and Log]
        B6 --> B8[NRR Token = H of M, sigma_R, tR, CertR]
    end

    subgraph TTP_SIDE[Trusted Third Party Services]
        C1[Timestamping Authority TSA]
        C2[Certificate Authority CA]
        C3[Notary Evidence Vault]
        C4[Arbitration Service]
    end

    A4 -. request timestamp .-> C1
    A4 -. request cert .-> C2
    B6 -. request timestamp .-> C1
    C3 -. dispute lookup .-> C4
    C4 -. verdict .-> J[Judge J]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix: When to Use NR vs. Plain Authentication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Does the message need to be admissible as evidence later}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is real-time identity sufficient}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Non-Repudiation with Digital Signature]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Symmetric Authentication with MAC or HMAC]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Asymmetric Authentication without Persistence]
    Q3 --> Q6[Anchor in PKI plus TTP timestamp]
    Q4 --> Q7[Use shared symmetric key K of adequate length]
```

## 4.4 Lifecycle of a Digital Signature inside an NR System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> KeyGen: Generate key pair of sk_O and pk_O
    KeyGen --> CertIssue: CA issues X.509 cert for pk_O
    CertIssue --> Sign: Compute d = H of M then sigma = Sign sk_O of d
    Sign --> Transmit: Attach sigma and cert to M
    Transmit --> Verify: Recipient runs Verify pk_O of d and sigma
    Verify --> Accepted: Check passes
    Verify --> Rejected: Check fails
    Accepted --> EvidenceStore: Archive NR token with TTP
    EvidenceStore --> DisputePossible: Judge can retrieve and verify later
    DisputePossible --> [*]
    Rejected --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The question bank below is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for PERAT756. The Part A questions target **CO1 / Bloom Level L1–L2 (Remember & Understand)**. The Part B questions target **CO1–CO2 / Bloom Level L3–L4 (Apply & Analyze)** and carry the standard 7 + 7 internal-choice structure.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**Define non-repudiation. Differentiate it from authentication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Non-repudiation is a security service that provides **proof of the integrity and origin of data** such that neither the sender nor the receiver can later deny having participated in the communication. It is implemented primarily using **digital signatures anchored in a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** and may additionally be **timestamped by a Trusted Third Party (TTP)**.

| Aspect | Authentication | Non-Repudiation |
|:-------|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Time validity** | Only valid at the moment of exchange | Persists indefinitely as evidence |
| **Verifiable by** | The other communicating party only | Any third party (e.g., a judge) |
| **Cryptographic basis** | MAC, password, biometric, certificate | Digital signature (asymmetric) |
| **Dispute resolution** | Not designed for it | Specifically designed for it |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for diagram/list of types, 1 mark for crisp difference.)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**List and briefly explain the different types of non-repudiation services as defined in ISO 7498-2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Non-Repudiation of Origin (NRO)** — Protects against the originator's false denial of having sent the message. (1 mark)
2. **Non-Repudiation of Receipt (NRR)** — Protects against the recipient's false denial of having received the message. (1 mark)
3. **Non-Repudiation of Submission (NRS)** — Protects against the originator's denial of having submitted the message to a delivery authority.
4. **Non-Repudiation of Delivery (NRD)** — Protects against the recipient's denial that the delivery authority delivered the message. (Remaining 1 mark for naming all four with one-line explanations.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by confusing *integrity* (provided by MAC) with *non-repudiation* (requires asymmetric digital signature). Always mention the **PKI / TTP** when explaining NR.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout #2:** When asked to "explain a non-repudiation protocol," many students draw only the *authentication* handshake (challenge-response) and forget to show the **evidence token exchange** and the **TTP arbitration step**. Always end the diagram with a *Judge verifies via TTP* arrow.

---

### **Question A — Choice 1** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of non-repudiation. Discuss its various types with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of a Trusted Third Party (TTP) in implementing non-repudiation. How does the ISO/IEC 13888-3 standard address TTP-based non-repudiation? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Non-repudiation is a security service defined in ISO 7498-2 that provides cryptographic proof of the integrity and origin of data, ensuring that neither party to a communication can later deny their participation. It is achieved by binding the identity of the originator to the message content via a **digital signature** $Sign_{sk_O}(H(M))$, and is verifiable by any third party possessing $pk_O$ and the corresponding X.509 certificate.

2. **Types (3 marks):** The four ISO-defined types are:
   - **NRO** (Non-Repudiation of Origin) — proven by $\sigma_O = Sign_{sk_O}(H(M))$.
   - **NRR** (Non-Repudiation of Receipt) — proven by $\sigma_R = Sign_{sk_R}(H(M))$.
   - **NRS** (Non-Repudiation of Submission) — proven by TTP submission receipt.
   - **NRD** (Non-Repudiation of Delivery) — proven by TTP delivery receipt.

3. **Diagram (2 marks):** Use the *Originator → TTP → Recipient → TTP* flow as shown in §4.1 above, and label each token exchanged as $NRO$ and $NRR$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Role of TTP (3 marks):** A TTP performs four functions — *key certification* (CA), *timestamping* (TSA), *evidence storage* (Notary), and *arbitration* (dispute resolution). It transforms a two-party exchange into a three-party verifiable exchange.

2. **ISO/IEC 13888-3 (2 marks):** This third part of the standard specifies non-repudiation mechanisms that use a **trusted third party** for the generation, distribution, and verification of NR tokens. It defines two roles for the TTP:
   - **Inline TTP** — involved in every message exchange (high latency, high trust).
   - **Online/offline TTP** — involved only at token generation (offline) and at arbitration (online).

3. **Mechanism outline (2 marks):** Under 13888-3, the originator first obtains a **timestamped NRO token from the TTP**, transmits the token to the recipient, who then requests a **timestamped NRR token** from the TTP. Both tokens are stored in the TTP's evidence vault and can be retrieved by a judge in case of dispute.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functioning of a digital signature-based non-repudiation service. Include the role of hashing, key pairs, and certificates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the *Non-Repudiation Token* (NR Token) format. Why is a Trusted Timestamp Authority (TSA) essential for the validity of an NR Token? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the layered architecture from §4.2 (Hash → Sign → Token → Transmit → Verify → Evidence Store).

2. **Hashing step (1 mark):** Compute $d = H(M)$ using SHA-256 (256-bit digest). The hash binds the signature to the message content — any modification to $M$ changes $d$ with overwhelming probability.

3. **Key pair step (1.5 marks):** The originator's private key $sk_O$ produces $\sigma = Sign_{sk_O}(d)$. The mathematically linked public key $pk_O$ is used by any verifier to check $Verify_{pk_O}(d, \sigma) = true$. The asymmetry ensures that only $sk_O$ can sign.

4. **Certificate step (1.5 marks):** The X.509 certificate $C_O$ issued by a trusted CA binds $pk_O$ to the originator's identity (e.g., name, email, organization). Without this binding, a verifier cannot trust that $pk_O$ actually belongs to the claimed sender.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **NR Token Format (3 marks):** The token is a tuple
   $$NR_{O} = \bigl( M,\ \sigma,\ t_{O},\ C_{O},\ L_{O} \bigr)$$
   where $M$ is the message (or its secure reference), $\sigma$ is the digital signature, $t_O$ is the trusted timestamp, $C_O$ is the X.509 certificate of the originator, and $L_O$ is the linkage field containing the hash algorithm OID, the signature algorithm OID, and the policy reference.

2. **Why TSA is essential (2 marks):** A digital signature alone proves that *at some point in time*, the originator had access to $sk_O$. It does not prove **when** the signature was produced. Without a TSA timestamp, an attacker could replay an old signature, or the originator could fraudulently claim that the signature was produced **after** their certificate was revoked. The TSA binds the signature to a specific moment in time, making the NR token legally and technically durable.

3. **TSA workflow (2 marks):** The originator submits $H(M)$ to the TSA; the TSA adds a trusted timestamp $t_O$ signed with the TSA's own private key; the result is returned. The verifier can check the TSA's signature against the TSA's public key (rooted in a trust anchor) and confirm that $t_O$ lies within the validity window of $C_O$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — read this aloud the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Non-repudiation** = security service providing **undeniable proof** of origin, receipt, submission, or delivery of a message.
- [ ] It is governed by **ISO 7498-2** (definition) and **ISO/IEC 13888-1/2/3** (mechanisms).
- [ ] **Authentication ≠ Non-repudiation.** Authentication is live and bilateral; NR is durable and trilateral.
- [ ] **MACs do not provide NR** because both parties hold the symmetric key. NR requires **asymmetric digital signatures** so that only the signer can produce $\sigma$.
- [ ] The **four types of NR** are: **NRO, NRR, NRS, NRD**.
- [ ] The standard **NRO Token** is $NR_O = (M, \sigma, t_O, C_O, L_O)$.
- [ ] The standard **NRR Token** is $NR_R = (H(M), \sigma_R, t_R, C_R)$.
- [ ] A **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** plays four roles: **CA, TSA, Notary, Arbitrator**.
- [ ] A **TSA** (Timestamping Authority) is mandatory for legally admissible NR evidence.
- [ ] **PKI / X.509** binds public keys to identities and is the trust root of NR.
- [ ] **Fairness** + **Timeliness** = the two formal correctness properties of an NR protocol.
- [ ] **Distributed TTP / Threshold Cryptography** mitigates the single-point-of-failure risk.
- [ ] Real-world deployers: **Aadhaar e-Sign, e-Filing of IT returns, MCA-21, SWIFT, Bitcoin (ECDSA).**
- [ ] **Key sizes** to memorise: RSA-**2048** → 256-byte signature; ECDSA-**256** → 64-byte signature; SHA-**256** → 32-byte hash.
- [ ] **Exam mantra:** *“Hash for efficiency, Sign for accountability, Certify for trust, Timestamp for durability, TTP for arbitration.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Availability Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Availability Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Information Security** and **Secure Communication**, the **Availability Service** is formally defined as the property of a system, network, or information resource that ensures authorized users can access the resource in a reliable, timely, and uninterrupted manner whenever required. Availability is one of the three foundational pillars of the universally accepted **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability), which forms the cornerstone of modern cybersecurity frameworks and standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001, NIST SP 800-53).

Mathematically, Availability (denoted as $A$) is expressed as the ratio of the total operational (uptime) time of a system to the total time period under consideration, encompassing both uptime and downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT756 - Module 1):** Availability is treated as a primary *security service* alongside confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to differentiate between these core services and identify the corresponding threat models and countermeasures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city's public water supply system**. Regardless of how pure the water is (integrity) and how restricted the access is to authorized citizens (confidentiality), the system fails its fundamental purpose if the taps run dry when citizens turn them on. The water supply must be *continuously accessible* to the people who are authorized to use it. This uninterrupted, on-demand serviceability is precisely what **Availability** guarantees in a digital system.

A second, more relatable analogy: consider a **24/7 hospital emergency room (ER)**. The ER must be operational *at all times* because lives depend on it. The hospital invests in backup power generators, redundant staff, and parallel server systems (for hospital records) to ensure that the ER is never "down." Availability, in the cyber world, applies the same philosophy to data, applications, and communication channels.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

The following are the universally accepted *availability class levels*, measured in **percentage of uptime per year** and corresponding *maximum allowable downtime*. These are standard reference values that KTU examiners expect students to memorize:

| Availability Class | Uptime (%) | Max Downtime/Year | Max Downtime/Month |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two Nines | 99% | 3.65 days | 7.31 hours |
| Three Nines | 99.9% | 8.77 hours | 43.83 minutes |
| Four Nines | 99.99% | 52.60 minutes | 4.38 minutes |
| Five Nines | 99.999% | 5.26 minutes | 26.30 seconds |
| Six Nines | 99.9999% | 31.56 seconds | 2.62 seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Reference:** The "Five Nines" ($99.999\%$) availability is the de-facto gold standard for **carrier-grade telecommunications** and critical infrastructure (e.g., public telephone switching, banking settlement systems). It permits only **5.26 minutes** of downtime per year.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Reliability Parameters

To understand Availability quantitatively, three foundational reliability engineering parameters must be mastered:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time interval a system operates normally between two consecutive failures. Measured in **hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair / Mean Time To Recovery):** The average time required to restore a failed system to full operational state. Measured in **hours**.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** For non-repairable systems, the average expected lifetime before failure. Measured in **hours**.

## 2.2 The Core Availability Equation

The single most important equation for this topic, frequently appearing in KTU numerical problems, is:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

This equation reveals an engineering trade-off: to maximize availability, one can either **increase MTBF** (build more reliable hardware/software) or **decrease MTTR** (improve diagnostics, automate failover, maintain hot spares).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

The following table contains every critical formula required for solving KTU Part B numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Single component uptime ratio |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Probability system is down |
| 3 | Annual Downtime (hrs) | $D_{\text{year}} = (1-A) \times 8760$ | Total hours offline per year |
| 4 | Annual Downtime (min) | $D_{\text{year}} = (1-A) \times 525600$ | Total minutes offline per year |
| 5 | Series System Availability | $A_s = A_1 \times A_2 \times \cdots \times A_n$ | Components in dependency chain |
| 6 | Parallel System Availability | $A_p = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)\cdots(1-A_n)$ | Redundant component chain |
| 7 | Dual Parallel (2 identical) | $A_p = 1 - (1-A)^2 = 2A - A^2$ | Two identical parallel modules |
| 8 | M-of-N Redundancy | $A = \sum_{k=m}^{n} \binom{n}{k} A^k (1-A)^{n-k}$ | k-out-of-n voting systems |
| 9 | Steady-State Availability | $A_\infty = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Using failure rate $\lambda$ & repair rate $\mu$ |
| 10 | Failure Rate Relation | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Exponential distribution assumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Notation Reminder:** In all handwritten or printed KTU answer sheets, students must write $\lambda$ (lambda) and $\mu$ (mu) clearly. The vertical bar (e.g., $|x|$) should be written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid markdown rendering issues in digital submissions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Availability service is the backbone of mission-critical sectors:
- **Banking & Finance:** ATM networks and stock exchanges demand $\geq 99.99\%$ availability; outages translate directly to monetary loss.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital Information Systems (HIS) must remain online; unavailability can risk patient lives.
- **Telecommunications:** Mobile core networks (4G/5G) target $99.999\%$ availability.
- **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Service Level Agreements (SLAs) explicitly define availability as a contractual metric, with credits issued for breaches.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS) & SCADA:** Unavailability in a power grid's SCADA system can cause blackouts.

## 2.5 Operational Logic Flow (Bulleted Breakdown)

- **Step 1 — Identify the asset:** Determine what must remain available (data, service, hardware, channel).
- **Step 2 — Quantify the requirement:** Assign a target availability class (e.g., $99.9\%$).
- **Step 3 — Identify threats:** Map applicable threats (DoS, hardware failure, natural disaster).
- **Step 4 — Apply countermeasures:** Implement redundancy, backup, failover, load balancing.
- **Step 5 — Measure and verify:** Continuously monitor SLA compliance and Mean Time metrics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Basic Availability Ratio to MTBF/MTTR Form

**Problem Setup:** Define availability as the long-run fraction of time a repairable system is operational. Let $T_{\text{up}}$ denote expected uptime and $T_{\text{down}}$ denote expected downtime per operational cycle.

**Step (a):** A single operational cycle consists of one uptime interval and one downtime interval:

$$\text{Cycle Time} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

**Step (b):** Substitute the standard reliability terms. $T_{\text{up}}$ is the **MTBF** (the operating time before failure), and $T_{\text{down}}$ is the **MTTR** (the time spent in repair before returning to operation):

$$\text{Cycle Time} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

**Step (c):** By the operational definition of availability as the fraction of cycle time spent in the operational state:

$$A = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{\text{Cycle Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Conclusion:** The dimensionless ratio $A$ lies in the open interval $(0, 1)$, where $A \to 1$ implies near-perfect availability and $A \to 0$ implies a chronically failing system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series System Availability

**Problem Setup:** Two components $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in **series**, meaning the system functions *only if both* $C_1$ *and* $C_2$ are operational simultaneously (e.g., a router feeding a switch feeding a server).

**Step (a):** Let $A_1$ be the probability that $C_1$ is up, and $A_2$ be the probability that $C_2$ is up. Since these are typically treated as independent events in basic KTU problems:

$$P(\text{both up}) = P(C_1 \text{ up}) \times P(C_2 \text{ up})$$

**Step (b):** Translate probabilities to availability:

$$A_s = A_1 \cdot A_2$$

**Step (c):** Generalize to $n$ components in series:

$$A_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i = A_1 \times A_2 \times \cdots \times A_n$$

**Engineering Insight:** A series chain is *availability-weakening* — adding any component with $A < 1$ *reduces* overall system availability. This is why critical paths in telecommunication networks are designed to be as short as possible.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parallel System Availability

**Problem Setup:** Two components $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in **parallel** (active-active redundancy), meaning the system functions as long as *at least one* component is operational.

**Step (a):** The system *fails* only if *all* parallel components fail. Compute the joint failure probability:

$$P(\text{system fails}) = P(C_1 \text{ fails}) \times P(C_2 \text{ fails})$$

**Step (b):** Express failure probability in terms of unavailability $U_i = 1 - A_i$:

$$P(\text{system fails}) = (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$$
