**Step (c):** System availability is the complement:

$$A_p = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$$

**Step (d):** Expand the product:

$$A_p = 1 - (1 - A_1 - A_2 + A_1 A_2)$$

**Step (e):** Simplify:

$$A_p = A_1 + A_2 - A_1 A_2$$

**Step (f):** For two *identical* components where $A_1 = A_2 = A$:

$$A_p = 2A - A^2 = 1 - (1-A)^2$$

**Engineering Insight:** Parallel redundancy is *availability-strengthening* — it always yields $A_p \geq \max(A_1, A_2)$. This is the mathematical justification for deploying redundant servers, power supplies, and network links in datacenters.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A web server has an MTBF of 2000 hours and an MTTR of 4 hours.
**(a)** Calculate its steady-state availability.
**(b)** Calculate the expected annual downtime in minutes.
**(c)** If a second identical server is deployed in active-active parallel redundancy, what is the new system availability?

### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Apply the core availability formula:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 4} = \frac{2000}{2004}$$

$$A = 0.998004 \approx 99.80\%$$

**Part (b):** First compute unavailability:

$$U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.998004 = 0.001996$$

Convert to annual minutes (1 year = 365 × 24 × 60 = 525,600 minutes):

$$D_{\text{year}} = U \times 525600 = 0.001996 \times 525600$$

$$D_{\text{year}} = 1048.99 \text{ minutes} \approx 17.48 \text{ hours per year}$$

**Part (c):** For two identical servers in parallel:

$$A_p = 2A - A^2 = 2(0.998004) - (0.998004)^2$$

$$A_p = 1.996008 - 0.996012 = 0.999996$$

$$A_p \approx 99.9996\% \approx \text{Five Nines plus}$$

**Conclusion:** Adding one redundant server pushed the system from $\approx 99.8\%$ (Three Nines) to $\approx 99.9996\%$ — illustrating the dramatic impact of parallel redundancy on availability.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Configuration | Single Component ($A = 0.99$) | Two Series | Two Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $A$ | $A^2$ | $2A - A^2$ |
| Numerical Result | $0.9900$ | $0.9801$ | $0.9999$ |
| Annual Downtime (min) | $5256$ | $10454$ | $52.6$ |
| Availability Class | Two Nines | Below Two Nines | Four Nines |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show your algebraic expansion steps. KTU examiners award full marks (7/7) only when the substitution, expansion, and simplification are all explicitly visible. Do not skip the line $A_p = A_1 + A_2 - A_1 A_2$ from the boxed form.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Availability Service

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the Availability Service is architected as a layered defense, mapping the *control flow* from the end user down through the redundancy and recovery mechanisms that sustain uninterrupted service.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[Authorized End User]
    U2[Authorized Admin User]
    LB[Load Balancer and Traffic Distributor]
    FW[Firewall and DDoS Mitigation Layer]
    SVR1[Primary Application Server Node A]
    SVR2[Backup Application Server Node B]
    DB1[Primary Database Server]
    DB2[Replicated Standby Database Server]
    PS1[Primary Power Supply Unit]
    PS2[Redundant Power Supply Unit]
    NK1[Primary Network Link ISP1]
    NK2[Secondary Network Link ISP2]
    BCK[Offsite Encrypted Backup Vault]
    MON[24x7 Monitoring and SIEM Dashboard]

    U1 --> LB
    U2 --> LB
    LB --> FW
    FW --> SVR1
    FW --> SVR2
    SVR1 --> DB1
    SVR2 --> DB2
    DB1 -.replication.- DB2
    SVR1 -.heartbeat.- SVR2
    PS1 -.parallel.- PS2
    SVR1 --- PS1
    SVR2 --- PS2
    FW --- NK1
    FW --- NK2
    SVR1 --- BCK
    MON -.observes.- FW
    MON -.observes.- SVR1
    MON -.observes.- SVR2
    MON -.observes.- DB1
    MON -.observes.- DB2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows indicate *active request flow*. Dotted arrows represent *synchronization channels* (database replication, cluster heartbeat, monitoring telemetry). Dashed connections to `MON` indicate passive observation by the Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system.

## 4.2 Threat-to-Countermeasure Mapping Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram maps the principal **threats to availability** to their corresponding **engineering countermeasures**, illustrating the defense-in-depth philosophy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THR[Threats To Availability]
        T1[Denial of Service DoS Attack]
        T2[Distributed DoS DDoS Attack]
        T3[Hardware Component Failure]
        T4[Software Crash and Bug]
        T5[Natural Disaster or Power Loss]
        T6[Malware or Ransomware Outage]
    end

    subgraph CTR[Availability Countermeasures]
        C1[Rate Limiting and Traffic Filtering]
        C2[Scrubbing Centers and Anycast Routing]
        C3[Hardware Redundancy and Hot Spares]
        C4[Fault Tolerant Software and Watchdogs]
        C5[UPS Generators and Geo Redundancy]
        C6[Immutable Backups and Disaster Recovery]
    end

    T1 --> C1
    T2 --> C2
    T3 --> C3
    T4 --> C4
    T5 --> C5
    T6 --> C6
    C1 --> A[Assured Availability Service]
    C2 --> A
    C3 --> A
    C4 --> A
    C5 --> A
    C6 --> A
```

## 4.3 Availability Class Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Baseline Single Server]
    L1[Two Nines 99 percent]
    L2[Three Nines 99.9 percent]
    L3[Four Nines 99.99 percent]
    L4[Five Nines 99.999 percent]
    L5[Six Nines 99.9999 percent]

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

Each upward transition in this hierarchy requires exponentially greater engineering investment (redundancy tiers, geographic distribution, automated failover), which is why Five Nines is typically reserved for telecommunications-grade and financial settlement systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Availability Service in the context of information security. List any two threats to availability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Availability is a core security service in the **CIA Triad** that ensures that information, systems, and communication resources are accessible to authorized users whenever requested, without undue delay or interruption. It guarantees *timely and reliable access* to data and services.

Two major threats to availability are:
1. **Denial of Service (DoS) Attack** — An adversary floods a target system with overwhelming traffic, exhausting its computational or bandwidth resources and rendering it inaccessible to legitimate users.
2. **Hardware/Software Failure** — Unanticipated crashes, disk failures, or software bugs can cause unexpected downtime and service unavailability.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two threats = 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between MTBF and MTTR. How do they jointly determine system availability?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average operational time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. It is a measure of system **reliability**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to detect, diagnose, and repair a failed system, returning it to operational state. It is a measure of **serviceability**.

They jointly determine steady-state availability through the equation:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

A high MTBF and a low MTTR both contribute to maximizing $A$, pushing the system closer to $99.999\%$ (Five Nines) availability.

*(1 mark each for defining MTBF and MTTR, 1 mark for the joint equation = 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** on availability numerical problems by:
> (a) Forgetting to convert **annual downtime** into the *correct unit* (hours vs. minutes vs. seconds) as asked in the question.
> (b) Writing the **parallel system formula** as $A_1 \times A_2$ instead of $1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)$ — this is a fatal error that yields inverted answers.
> (c) Failing to show the **expansion step** $A_p = A_1 + A_2 - A_1 A_2$, which examiners explicitly require for full marks.
> (d) Confusing *series* and *parallel* when reading the problem statement; always draw a quick block diagram before substituting.

---

### Part B — Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A critical communication system uses three components in *series*, each with availability $A_1 = 0.98$, $A_2 = 0.95$, and $A_3 = 0.99$.
   **(i)** Compute the overall system availability.
   **(ii)** Calculate the expected annual downtime in hours.
   **(iii)** If a fourth redundant component with availability $A_4 = 0.98$ is added in **parallel** with the entire series chain, what is the new system availability? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of *active-active* and *active-passive* redundancy with suitable examples. How does each contribute to availability? **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Answer for Question A

**Part (a)(i) — Series availability (2 marks):**

$$A_s = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.99$$

$$A_s = 0.92169 \approx 0.9217$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Annual downtime (2 marks):**

$$U_s = 1 - 0.9217 = 0.0783$$

$$D_{\text{year}} = 0.0783 \times 8760 \text{ hours} = 686.0 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Parallel redundancy over the chain (3 marks):**

$$A_p = 1 - (1 - A_s)(1 - A_4) = 1 - (1 - 0.9217)(1 - 0.98)$$

$$A_p = 1 - (0.0783 \times 0.02) = 1 - 0.001566 = 0.998434$$

$$A_p \approx 99.84\%$$

**Part (b) — Redundancy types (7 marks):**

*Active-Active Redundancy:* In this configuration, *all* redundant components are simultaneously operational and share the workload. If one fails, the others seamlessly absorb its load. **Example:** Two web servers behind a load balancer, both serving live traffic. If Server A fails, the load balancer routes all traffic to Server B without service interruption. This provides both *high availability* (no downtime during failure) and *scalability* (load is distributed).

*Active-Passive (Standby) Redundancy:* Only the primary component handles the workload; the secondary remains idle (or receives a low-rate heartbeat). Upon failure of the primary, a *failover* mechanism activates the secondary. **Example:** A primary database server with a hot-standby replica receiving asynchronous updates. The standby takes over only when the primary fails. This typically provides a brief interruption (the failover time) but is cheaper to implement.

*Contribution to availability:* Both architectures significantly improve availability by eliminating single points of failure. Active-active is preferred for latency-sensitive applications; active-passive is preferred for stateful, data-intensive workloads where concurrent writes are problematic.

---

### Part B — Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A server has an MTBF of 5000 hours and an MTTR of 10 hours.
   **(i)** Calculate its steady-state availability.
   **(ii)** Compute the annual downtime in minutes.
   **(iii)** If two such identical servers are configured in active-active parallel, what is the new system availability and corresponding annual downtime in minutes? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the following availability mechanisms with one real-world example for each: (i) RAID, (ii) UPS, (iii) Load Balancing, (iv) Offsite Backup. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Answer for Question B

**Part (a)(i) — Steady-state availability (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 10} = \frac{5000}{5010}$$

$$A = 0.998004 \approx 99.80\%$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Annual downtime in minutes (2 marks):**

$$U = 1 - 0.998004 = 0.001996$$

$$D_{\text{year}} = 0.001996 \times 525600 = 1049.0 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Parallel redundancy (3 marks):**

$$A_p = 2A - A^2 = 2(0.998004) - (0.998004)^2$$

$$A_p = 1.996008 - 0.996012 = 0.999996$$

Annual downtime for the parallel configuration:

$$U_p = 1 - 0.999996 = 0.000004$$

$$D_{\text{year, parallel}} = 0.000004 \times 525600 = 2.10 \text{ minutes/year}$$

This is approximately the **Five Nines** class.

**Part (b) — Availability mechanisms (7 marks, ~1.75 marks each):**

**(i) RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks):** Distributes data across multiple physical disks with parity or mirroring so that disk failure does not cause data loss or service interruption. *Example:* RAID-1 (mirroring) duplicates data on two disks; if one fails, the other continues serving requests seamlessly.

**(ii) UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply):** A battery-backed power system that takes over within milliseconds when mains electricity fails, preventing abrupt server shutdowns. *Example:* A 5 kVA rack-mounted UPS in a datacenter bridging the gap until diesel generators spin up.

**(iii) Load Balancing:** Distributes incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers using algorithms like round-robin or least-connections, preventing any single server from becoming a bottleneck. *Example:* NGINX or HAProxy in front of a microservices cluster.

**(iv) Offsite Backup:** Periodic copying of critical data to a geographically distant, secure location (or cloud vault) to enable recovery from disasters like fire, flood, or ransomware. *Example:* Nightly encrypted snapshots of customer databases uploaded to AWS S3 Glacier in a different region.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CIA Triad:** Availability is the third pillar alongside Confidentiality and Integrity.
- **Core Equation:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — memorize and apply without hesitation.
- **Series System:** $A_s = \prod A_i$ — adding components *weakens* availability.
- **Parallel System:** $A_p = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ — adding components *strengthens* availability.
- **Identical Parallel Pair:** $A_p = 2A - A^2 = 1 - (1-A)^2$ — frequently tested.
- **Five Nines ($99.999\%$)** is the carrier-grade benchmark (5.26 min downtime/year).
- **Year Constants:** 1 year = 8760 hours = 525,600 minutes = 31,536,000 seconds.
- **Redundancy types:** Active-Active (load-shared) and Active-Passive (standby failover).
- **Key availability mechanisms:** Redundancy, RAID, UPS, Load Balancing, Offsite Backup, DDoS scrubbing, Failover clustering, Geographic distribution.
- **Exam Trap:** Never confuse *reliability* (probability of failure-free operation over a period) with *availability* (long-run fraction of uptime including repair). A system can be highly reliable (high MTBF) but have poor availability if MTTR is very high.
- **Always convert units consistently** — mixing hours and minutes in the same calculation is a guaranteed mark-loss.
- **Diagrams help:** Sketching a quick block diagram of series/parallel topology before solving earns at least 1–2 partial marks even if the arithmetic contains an error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model for Network Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Model for Network Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model for Network Security (Stallings' Classical Model)**
> A generalized, protocol-agnostic architectural framework that abstracts the essential participants, communication channels, trust relationships, and threat actors required to design, analyze, and implement secure communication across an open, distributed network. It formally specifies the security transformation, the secrecy mechanism (key distribution), and the adversary model as first-class engineering entities.

The model is **technology-neutral** — it does not depend on any specific cipher (AES, RSA, etc.) but rather defines the *role* each entity must play in any secure communication architecture (e.g., TLS, IPsec, SSH, WPA3).

## 1.2 Core Components of the Model

| Component | Standard Notation | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| Sender / Source | `Alice (A)` | Originates the plaintext message `M` |
| Receiver / Destination | `Bob (B)` | Consumes the recovered plaintext `M` |
| Information Channel | `C_unsecure` | Untrusted medium (e.g., the Internet) |
| Secret Key Generator | `K_gen` | Produces the secret `K` shared between A & B |
| Secure Channel | `C_secure` | Out-of-band channel for key distribution |
| Trusted Third Party | `TTP` | Arbitrates keys/certificates between A & B |
| Adversary / Opponent | `Oscar (O)` | Threat actor attempting to breach security goals |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's 2024 Scheme notation, the message `M` is also called **Plaintext**, the encrypted form `C` is **Ciphertext**, and the shared secret `K` is the **Key**. A **Security Transformation** `S` converts `M → C`, and a **Secrecy Transformation** is realized via the key `K`.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Locked Diary Courier"

Imagine Alice wants to send her personal diary to Bob through a public courier service she does not trust:

1. **Plaintext (M)** = the diary itself (readable, sensitive).
2. **Ciphertext (C)** = the diary locked inside a **tamper-proof steel box** with a unique combination lock.
3. **Key (K)** = the secret numeric combination known **only** to Alice and Bob.
4. **Security Transformation (Encryption, E)** = Alice places the diary in the box and locks it.
5. **Information Channel** = the courier truck (untrusted, anyone may intercept it).
6. **Decryption, D** = Bob, knowing the combination, opens the box and reads the diary.
7. **Adversary (Oscar)** = a thief who wants to (a) read the diary, (b) alter it, (c) pretend to be Alice, or (d) prevent Bob from ever getting it.
8. **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** = a notary who hands out the combinations through a separate, trusted channel (e.g., registered post).

Just as the diary problem requires **secrecy** (combination), **authenticity** (verify it really came from Alice), and **integrity** (no pages were swapped), the network security model captures these same needs in formal engineering terms.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners often award a 2-mark bonus if you explicitly identify *all four* canonical components — **Sender, Receiver, Trusted Third Party, and Adversary** — in your answer. Missing the TTP is the #1 cause of lost marks.

## 1.4 Why a "Model" is Needed

A model abstracts away implementation details so that:

* **Security goals** (CIA Triad: *Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability*) can be stated independently of the cipher.
* **Threats** can be categorized uniformly (passive vs. active).
* **Mechanisms** (encryption, digital signature, hash, access control) can be evaluated against the threat model.
* **Services** (authentication, non-repudiation, access control) can be mapped to mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> The **CIA Triad** is the foundational security goals triad evaluated across every KTU question on this topic. Memorize: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication + **N**on-repudiation + **A**vailability = the *5 pillars* in KTU's extended model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Logical Phases of the Model

### Phase 1 — Algorithm Design & Key Generation
1. A cryptographic algorithm `E` (e.g., AES-256) and its inverse `D` are *publicly chosen* from a vetted family of ciphers.
2. A secret key `K` is generated using a cryptographically secure pseudo-random number generator (CSPRNG).
3. Key space must be large enough to resist brute force — minimum **128-bit** security strength is the KTU-recommended baseline.

### Phase 2 — Key Distribution (Secrecy Step)
1. The key `K` is transmitted from a **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** to both Alice and Bob over a **secure channel** `C_secure`.
2. Examples: pre-shared keys (PSK), Diffie–Hellman exchange with authentication, PKI certificate delivery.

### Phase 3 — Secure Message Transfer (Security Step)
1. Alice computes ciphertext `C = E_K(M)` using the agreed cipher `E` and key `K`.
2. `C` is transmitted over the **untrusted information channel** `C_unsecure`.
3. Bob computes `M' = D_K(C)` and verifies `M' = M`.

### Phase 4 — Verification & Trust
1. Bob confirms the message is authentic (came from Alice) and unaltered.
2. Mechanisms: **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**, **Digital Signature**, **Hash Function**.

## 2.2 Two Equivalent Formalisations

**Stallings' Compact Form:**

$$
C = E(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D(C) \quad \text{where} \quad D(E(M)) = M
$$

**Keyed (Practical) Form:**

$$
C = E_K(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_K(C) = D_K(E_K(M))
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The keyed form is what KTU expects in Part B 14-mark questions. The unkeyed form is acceptable only in Part A (3-mark) definition questions.

## 2.3 Adversary Model — The "Oscar" Threat Taxonomy

| Adversary Class | Capability | Specific Attacks | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | Eavesdrops only; does not modify traffic | (i) Release of message contents, (ii) Traffic analysis | High |
| **Active** | Modifies / fabricates / replays traffic | (i) Masquerade, (ii) Replay, (iii) Modification, (iv) Denial of Service | Very High |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error is to list **only 2 attacks** under active adversary. The complete KTU-mandated list has **four**: Masquerade, Replay, Modification of messages, and Denial of Service. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark per missed attack.

## 2.4 Security Services (X.800 / KTU Canonical List)

| Service | Purpose | Primary Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Data Confidentiality | Protect content from disclosure | Symmetric / Asymmetric encryption |
| Data Integrity | Detect any alteration of content | Hash, MAC, Digital Signature |
| Authentication | Verify identity of sender | Digital Signature, MAC |
| Non-Repudiation | Prevent sender from denying origin | Asymmetric Digital Signature |
| Access Control | Restrict who can use resources | ACL, RBAC, Firewalls |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Mathematical Form | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| `M` | Plaintext message | $M \in \mathcal{M}$ | $\mathcal{M}$ = message space |
| `C` | Ciphertext | $C \in \mathcal{C}$ | $\mathcal{C}$ = ciphertext space |
| `K` | Secret key | $K \in \mathcal{K}$ | $\mathcal{K}$ = key space, size $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert \geq 2^{128}$ |
| `E` | Encryption algorithm | $E : \mathcal{M} \times \mathcal{K} \to \mathcal{C}$ | Publicly known |
| `D` | Decryption algorithm | $D : \mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{K} \to \mathcal{M}$ | Publicly known (symmetric) or private (asymmetric) |
| `H` | Hash function | $H(M) = h$, $h \in \{0,1\}^{n}$ | $n \geq 256$ bits for SHA-256 |
| `MAC` | Message Auth. Code | $MAC_K(M) = T$ | $T$ = tag, $\vert T \vert \geq 128$ bits |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU answer scripts, use the **set-theoretic** form $E : \mathcal{M} \times \mathcal{K} \to \mathcal{C}$ rather than just $C = E(M)$. It demonstrates mastery of the *abstract* model — which is exactly what Module 1 tests.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The model is implemented in:

* **TLS 1.3** (HTTPS): `C = AES-GCM_K(M)` + ECDHE key exchange via TTP (Certificate Authority).
* **IPsec / VPN**: ESP tunnel mode uses `E_K(M)` over an untrusted WAN.
* **WhatsApp Signal Protocol**: Implements all four model phases with double-ratchet `E` and authenticated key exchange.
* **Banking (RBI mandate 2018)**: End-to-end encryption must follow the Stallings model — encryption + integrity + non-repudiation.

> [!TIP]
> Linking the abstract model to a real protocol like **TLS 1.3** in your answer automatically gives the **Application-Level Insight** mark, which is a hidden +1 bonus in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Equation $D(E(M)) = M$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Derive the condition for a cipher to be correct."* The following derivation is the canonical answer.

**Given:**
* Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a finite set of plaintexts, $\vert \mathcal{M} \vert = N$.
* Let $\mathcal{K}$ be the set of all possible keys, $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = k$.
* Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the set of possible ciphertexts, $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = N$ (bijection assumed).

**Step 1 — Define the encryption map.**

For each key $K \in \mathcal{K}$, define:

$$
E_K : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{C}, \quad C = E_K(M)
$$

This mapping must be **deterministic** — the same $(M, K)$ always yields the same $C$.

**Step 2 — Define the decryption map.**

For each key $K$, the inverse map is:

$$
D_K : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{M}, \quad M' = D_K(C)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply decryption to a ciphertext produced by encryption.**

Substitute $C = E_K(M)$ into the decryption equation:

$$
M' = D_K(E_K(M))
$$

**Step 4 — Impose the correctness condition.**

For the cipher to be *correct*, the recovered plaintext must equal the original for every $(M, K)$:

$$
\forall\, M \in \mathcal{M},\; \forall\, K \in \mathcal{K} : \quad D_K(E_K(M)) = M
$$

**Step 5 — Express compactly using composition.**

$$
D_K \circ E_K = \text{Id}_{\mathcal{M}}
$$

where $\text{Id}_{\mathcal{M}}$ is the identity map on $\mathcal{M}$.

**Step 6 — Required bijectivity.**

For $D_K$ to exist as a (single-valued) function, $E_K$ **must be injective**:

$$
M_1 \neq M_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; E_K(M_1) \neq E_K(M_2)
$$

Since finite sets $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{C}$ both have cardinality $N$, injectivity implies **bijectivity**, so $E_K$ is a permutation of $\mathcal{M}$.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,D_K(E_K(M)) = M,\quad \forall M \in \mathcal{M}, \forall K \in \mathcal{K}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always state *both* the universal quantifiers ($\forall M, \forall K$) — KTU examiners specifically look for the quantifier rigor as a marker of understanding (worth +0.5 to +1 mark).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threats → Services → Mechanisms

| Adversary Attack | Security Goal Violated | Service Required | Mechanism Employed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Release of message contents | Confidentiality | Data Confidentiality | Symmetric encryption (AES) |
| Traffic analysis | Confidentiality (partial) | Confidentiality | Traffic padding, Onion routing |
| Masquerade | Authentication | Authentication | Digital Signature, MAC |
| Content modification | Integrity | Data Integrity | Hash (SHA-256), MAC |
| Sequence / Replay | Integrity + Authenticity | Authentication | Timestamps, Nonce, Sequence No. |
| Source repudiation | Non-Repudiation | Non-Repudiation | Asymmetric Digital Signature |
| Denial of Service | Availability | Availability | Firewalls, Rate limiting, IDS |

## 3.3 Key-Space Size Derivation (Brute-Force Resistance)

**Given:** A key space of size $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^n$ for an $n$-bit key, and an adversary capable of $10^{12}$ guesses per second (a modern GPU rig).

**Average number of trials to find the key:**

$$
E[\text{trials}] = \frac{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}{2} = 2^{n-1}
$$

**Average wall-clock time to break the cipher:**

$$
T_{\text{break}} = \frac{2^{n-1}}{r}, \quad r = 10^{12}\;\text{trials/sec}
$$

| Key Length $n$ | $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | $T_{\text{break}}$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 56 | $2^{56} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{16}$ | $\approx 18$ hours | **Broken** (DES) |
| 128 | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 1.7 \times 10^{19}$ years | **Secure** (AES-128) |
| 256 | $2^{256}$ | $\approx 1.5 \times 10^{58}$ years | **Future-proof** (AES-256) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU expects you to quote the **$2^{n-1}$ average** trials, not $2^n$ (worst case). Writing $2^n$ is a common mistake that costs 0.5 marks.

## 3.4 The Information-Theoretic Bound (Shannon's Argument)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield derivation for **Part B 14-mark** questions.

**Setup:** Suppose $\vert \mathcal{M} \vert = \vert \mathcal{C} \vert = N$ and the key $K$ is uniformly random over $\mathcal{K}$.

**Step 1 — For perfect secrecy, the ciphertext must leak zero information about the plaintext:**

$$
P(M = m \mid C = c) = P(M = m), \quad \forall\, m \in \mathcal{M},\; c \in \mathcal{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Use Bayes' theorem and the fact that $P(C=c \mid M=m) = P(K = E^{-1}_K(c, m))$:**

$$
P(C=c \mid M=m) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
P(M=m \mid C=c) = \frac{P(M=m) \cdot P(C=c \mid M=m)}{P(C=c)} = \frac{\frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}}{P(C=c)}
$$

**Step 4 — For the conditional to equal the prior $P(M=m) = 1/N$:**

$$
\frac{1}{N \cdot \vert \mathcal{K} \vert \cdot P(C=c)} = \frac{1}{N} \;\Longrightarrow\; P(C=c) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}
$$

**Step 5 — Since $P(C=c) \leq 1$ and sums to 1 over $c$:**

$$
\sum_{c \in \mathcal{C}} P(C=c) = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\vert \mathcal{C} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert} = 1
$$

**Shannon's Theorem Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\vert \mathcal{K} \vert \geq \vert \mathcal{M} \vert = \vert \mathcal{C} \vert\,}
$$

For *perfect secrecy*, the key must be **at least as long as the message** — the foundational result behind the **One-Time Pad (OTP)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stallings' Canonical Model for Network Security

The following block-level Mermaid diagram captures every component of the classical model, with explicit information flow between the sender, receiver, trusted third party, and adversary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    alice[Sender Alice]
    bob[Receiver Bob]
    ttp[Trusted Third Party TTP]
    oscar[Adversary Oscar]
    kgen[Key Generator]
    cinfo[Untrusted Information Channel]
    csec[Secure Channel]
    plaintext[Plaintext M]
    cipher[Encryption Algorithm E]
    key[Secret Key K]
    ciphertxt[Ciphertext C]
    decipher[Decryption Algorithm D]
    out[Recovered Plaintext M]

    kgen --> key
    key --> csec
    csec --> alice
    csec --> bob
    ttp -. mediates .-> kgen
    ttp -. mediates .-> csec

    plaintext --> alice
    alice --> cipher
    key --> cipher
    cipher --> ciphertxt
    ciphertxt --> cinfo
    cinfo --> decipher
    oscar -. intercepts .-> cinfo
    oscar -. injects .-> cinfo
    key --> decipher
    decipher --> bob
    bob --> out
```

**How to read the diagram (for KTU answer scripts):**

* The **solid arrows** show the *legitimate flow* of information (plaintext → ciphertext → recovered plaintext).
* The **dotted arrows** show *side-channels* (TTP mediation) and *adversarial actions* (interception/injection by Oscar).
* The **two channels** are visually distinct: `csec` (dashed/top route) carries the secret key; `cinfo` (bottom route) carries the encrypted message.

## 4.2 Security Services ↔ Mechanisms Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Goals [Security Goals]
        g1[Confidentiality]
        g2[Integrity]
        g3[Authentication]
        g4[Non Repudiation]
        g5[Availability]
    end

    subgraph Services [Security Services X800]
        s1[Data Confidentiality]
        s2[Data Integrity]
        s3[Peer Authentication]
        s4[Non Repudiation Service]
        s5[Access Control Service]
    end

    subgraph Mechanisms [Security Mechanisms]
        m1[Symmetric Encryption AES]
        m2[Asymmetric Encryption RSA ECC]
        m3[Hash SHA 256]
        m4[MAC HMAC]
        m5[Digital Signature]
        m6[Certificate X509]
        m7[Firewall IDS]
    end

    g1 --> s1 --> m1
    g1 --> s1 --> m2
    g2 --> s2 --> m3
    g2 --> s2 --> m4
    g3 --> s3 --> m4
    g3 --> s3 --> m5
    g4 --> s4 --> m5
    g4 --> s4 --> m6
    g5 --> s5 --> m7
```

## 4.3 Adversary Threat-Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[Adversary Oscar]
    subgraph Passive [Passive Attacks]
        p1[Release of Message Contents]
        p2[Traffic Analysis]
    end
    subgraph Active [Active Attacks]
        q1[Masquerade]
        q2[Replay]
        q3[Message Modification]
        q4[Denial of Service]
    end
    a --> Passive
    a --> Active
```

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU answer script, **redraw this Mermaid block diagram by hand** as a labelled box diagram. Examiners award up to **2 marks** for a clean, well-labelled block diagram of the model.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Model for Network Security. List its four main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Model for Network Security** is an abstract architectural framework that describes the participants, communication channels, and transformations required to achieve secure communication over an untrusted network. It was formalized by William Stallings and is the foundational model used in all modern security protocol design.

**The four main components are:**

1. **Sender & Receiver** (Alice and Bob) — the two legitimate endpoints that wish to exchange a confidential and authentic message.
2. **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** — a mutually trusted arbiter responsible for distributing secret keys (or certificates) over a separate, secure channel.
3. **Adversary (Oscar)** — the threat actor who attempts to compromise the security goals, classified as *passive* (eavesdropping) or *active* (modification, replay, masquerade, DoS).
4. **Secure Channel + Information Channel** — a separate out-of-band channel for key distribution, distinct from the untrusted main channel used for ciphertext transmission.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list *only three* components (sender, receiver, adversary) and forget the **TTP**. This costs 1 mark. Always include TTP explicitly.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between passive and active attacks in the model for network security. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Monitors / observes the channel without altering traffic | Modifies, fabricates, or disrupts the traffic |
| **Detectability** | Hard to detect (no change to traffic) | Easier to detect (anomalies in traffic) |
| **Threat to** | Confidentiality | Integrity, Authenticity, Availability |
| **Example** | Eavesdropping on an unsecured Wi-Fi to capture credit-card numbers | A man-in-the-middle injecting a forged transaction in online banking |

**Passive Example:** *Release of message contents* — an adversary intercepts unencrypted HTTP traffic to read the user's password.

**Active Example:** *Message modification* — an attacker alters the destination account number in a wire-transfer request mid-flight.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only "passive = read, active = write" is too brief for 3 marks. Always include the **detectability contrast** and a **real-world example** to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B requires the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks** and is split into two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the classical model for network security. Briefly describe the role of each entity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the four types of active attacks. State which security service is violated by each, and the mechanism used to counter it.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Draw the standard Stallings block diagram with the following elements, **clearly labelled**:

* Plaintext `M` on the far left
* Sender block feeding into Encryption block `E`
* Key `K` from the Key Generator entering both `E` and `D`
* Ciphertext `C` transmitted over the **Information Channel**
* Decryption block `D` on the receiver side
* Trusted Third Party at the top connected to Sender & Receiver via **Secure Channel**
* Adversary Oscar intercepting the Information Channel

**Step 2 — Role of each entity (4 marks, 0.5 each):**

* **Sender (Alice):** Originates the plaintext message and applies the encryption transformation $C = E_K(M)$.
* **Receiver (Bob):** Receives the ciphertext and recovers the plaintext via $M = D_K(C)$.
* **Trusted Third Party (TTP):** Distributes the secret key $K$ to both parties; can also arbitrate timestamps, nonces, or digital certificates for authentication.
* **Key Generator:** Produces the random key $K$ from a cryptographically secure random source.
* **Encryption Algorithm (E):** A public, invertible, parameterized transformation that produces the ciphertext.
* **Decryption Algorithm (D):** The inverse of E; recovers plaintext from ciphertext.
* **Secure Channel:** A trusted out-of-band medium (e.g., registered post, in-person exchange, TLS with mutual certificates) used solely for key distribution.
* **Information Channel:** The untrusted main medium (Internet, Wi-Fi, cellular) carrying ciphertext.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| # | Active Attack | Security Service Violated | Counter Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Masquerade** — attacker pretends to be a legitimate user | Authentication | Digital Signature, MAC, Biometric |
| 2 | **Replay** — re-sends a previously captured valid message | Authentication + Integrity | Nonce, Timestamps, Sequence Numbers |
| 3 | **Modification of Messages** — alters portion of a legal message | Integrity | Hash (SHA-256), MAC, Digital Signature |
| 4 | **Denial of Service (DoS)** — floods the system to block legitimate use | Availability | Firewalls, Rate Limiting, IDS, DDoS scrubbing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe only **two** active attacks. KTU mandates **four** — Masquerade, Replay, Modification, and Denial of Service. Missing any one costs **1.5 marks**.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the fundamental correctness condition for a symmetric cipher: $D_K(E_K(M)) = M$. State the bijectivity requirement on $E_K$ and justify why it is necessary.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An organization uses AES-128 to secure a message. The adversary can test $10^{10}$ keys per second. Calculate the **average time** required to break the cipher by exhaustive search. Comment on the security.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the maps (1.5 marks):**
Let $E_K : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{C}$ and $D_K : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{M}$ for every $K \in \mathcal{K}$.

**Step 2 — Apply decryption to encryption (1.5 marks):**
$M' = D_K(E_K(M))$.

**Step 3 — Impose correctness (2 marks):**
$\forall M \in \mathcal{M}, \forall K \in \mathcal{K} : D_K(E_K(M)) = M$.

**Step 4 — Show bijectivity is necessary (2 marks):**
For $D_K$ to be a function (single-valued), $E_K$ must be injective. Since $\vert \mathcal{M} \vert = \vert \mathcal{C} \vert$ (finite), injectivity $\Rightarrow$ bijectivity $\Rightarrow$ permutation of $\mathcal{M}$.

> [!TIP]
> Writing *both* the quantifier line and the permutation statement is worth the full 7 marks. Skipping the bijectivity proof costs 2 marks.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
Key length $n = 128$ bits; rate $r = 10^{10}$ keys/sec.

**Step 2 — Compute key-space size (1 mark):**
$\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{128} \approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}$.

**Step 3 — Average trials to break (1.5 marks):**
$E[\text{trials}] = \vert \mathcal{K} \vert / 2 = 2^{127} \approx 1.7014 \times 10^{38}$.

**Step 4 — Compute time (1.5 marks):**
$T_{\text{break}} = \dfrac{2^{127}}{10^{10}} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{28}$ seconds.

**Step 5 — Convert to years (1 mark):**
$T_{\text{break}} \approx \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{28}}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{20}$ **years**.

**Step 6 — Security comment (1 mark):**
This is astronomically longer than the age of the universe ($\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years). AES-128 is therefore computationally secure against brute-force attack.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students use $2^{128}$ (worst case) instead of $2^{127}$ (average). Examiners accept both, but the **average** is the KTU-canonical answer for "average time to break."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second Rapid Revision Checklist — print this out before the exam.**

- ✅ **Model for Network Security** = abstract framework with 4 entities: **Sender, Receiver, TTP, Adversary**.
- ✅ **Two channels**: *Secure channel* (for key) and *Information channel* (for ciphertext).
- ✅ **Three transformations**: Algorithm design, Key generation, Encryption/Decryption.
- ✅ **Core equation**: $D_K(E_K(M)) = M$ — requires $E_K$ to be a **bijection** (permutation).
- ✅ **Correctness quantifiers**: Must hold $\forall M \in \mathcal{M}$ **and** $\forall K \in \mathcal{K}$.
- ✅ **Passive attacks (2)**: Release of message contents, Traffic analysis.
- ✅ **Active attacks (4)**: Masquerade, Replay, Modification, Denial of Service.
- ✅ **5 Security Services (X.800)**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control.
- ✅ **5 Security Mechanisms**: Encryption (symmetric + asymmetric), Hash, MAC, Digital Signature, Certificate.
- ✅ **Shannon's Perfect Secrecy**: $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert \geq \vert \mathcal{M} \vert$ (key must be at least as long as message).
- ✅ **Average break-time formula**: $T_{\text{break}} = \dfrac{2^{n-1}}{r}$ for $n$-bit key, rate $r$ guesses/sec.
- ✅ **Minimum modern key size**: $\geq 128$ bits (AES-128 baseline).
- ✅ **Real-world mapping**: TLS 1.3, IPsec, Signal Protocol, banking VPNs all implement Stallings' model.
- ✅ **TTP** is the **most-forgotten component** in exam answers — never omit it.
- ✅ **Bijectivity proof** is the most-tested derivation — practice it as a 7-mark answer.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Model for Network Security. All the best for your KTU ESE! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symmetric Cipher Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Symmetric Cipher Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Symmetric Cipher Model** (also called *Conventional* or *Secret-Key Cryptosystem*) is a cryptographic framework in which **the same secret key is used for both encryption and decryption operations**, and the communicating parties must agree upon and share this key through a *secure out-of-band channel* prior to exchanging any confidential data.

Mathematically, the model is defined as a 5-tuple $(P, C, K, E, D)$ where:

- $P$ — finite set of **plaintext** messages
- $C$ — finite set of **ciphertext** messages
- $K$ — finite set of **secret keys** (the *keyspace*)
- $E$ — family of **encryption transformations** $E_k : P \to C$
- $D$ — family of **decryption transformations** $D_k : C \to P$

Such that for every key $k \in K$, the inverse property holds:
$$D_k(E_k(P)) = P$$

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Highlight
> The defining property of symmetric cryptography is **key symmetry** — both sender and receiver must possess **an identical copy** of the secret key, and anyone possessing this key can both encrypt AND decrypt. This single-key dependency is the model’s greatest strength and its central vulnerability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **steel safety deposit locker** in a bank:

1. You place your **jewellery** (plaintext) inside the locker.
2. The locker is sealed using **a single physical key** (secret key $K$) — this is the *encryption* step.
3. The sealed locker is delivered to your friend via a **secure courier** (the *untrusted channel*).
4. Your friend uses **an identical duplicate key** (the same key $K$) to open the locker — this is the *decryption* step.

Now think about the problems:
- **Key distribution problem**: How did your friend originally get the duplicate key? You must have met in person (out-of-band).
- **Single point of failure**: If anyone steals the key, the entire system collapses. Both sender and receiver are vulnerable equally.
- **Scalability problem**: With $n$ users, you need $n(n-1)/2$ different keys to allow everyone to communicate privately.

This "**one shared key for everything**" concept is the essence of symmetric ciphers like **DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, RC4, and ChaCha20**.

## 1.3 Core Components — One-Line Definitions

> [!NOTE] Five Pillars of the Symmetric Cipher Model
> 1. **Plaintext (P)** — Original, human-readable message.
> 2. **Encryption Algorithm (E)** — Performs substitutions and transformations on P.
> 3. **Secret Key (K)** — Independent input that randomizes the encryption output.
> 4. **Ciphertext (C)** — Scrambled, unintelligible output transmitted over the insecure channel.
> 5. **Decryption Algorithm (D)** — Inverse of E using the same key K.

## 1.4 Channel Taxonomy

- **Secure Channel (Out-of-Band)**: Used to transmit the shared secret key. Assumed *impractical and expensive*.
- **Insecure Channel (In-Band)**: Used to transmit the ciphertext. Vulnerable to eavesdropping, but useless without the key.
- **One-Way Function Assumption**: Without $K$, recovering $P$ from $C$ must be computationally **infeasible**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Flow of the Symmetric Cipher Model

The complete pipeline is structured into 7 sequential logic stages. Each stage is examined for its *purpose* and *cryptographic role*.

### Stage 1 — Plaintext Generation
- **What**: Source produces original intelligible message $P$.
- **Why**: Every secure session begins with raw data (e.g., email, financial transaction, audio stream).
- **Real-world engineering**: $P$ is encoded into a binary bit-stream using ASCII/Unicode before processing.

### Stage 2 — Key Establishment
- **What**: Both sender and receiver agree on $K \in K$ via a **secure out-of-band channel**.
- **Why**: Without a shared key, the model collapses mathematically.
- **Real-world engineering**: Delivered via Diffie–Hellman exchange (Module 3), RSA-encrypted key wrap, or physical courier.

### Stage 3 — Encryption Transformation
- **What**: Cipher algorithm applies $E_k$ to plaintext, generating $C = E_k(P)$.
- **Why**: Converts meaningful data into statistically random-looking bit pattern.
- **KTU High-Yield**: The output must satisfy the **avalanche effect** — flipping 1 bit in $P$ must change ~50% of bits in $C$.

### Stage 4 — Transmission Over Insecure Channel
- **What**: Ciphertext $C$ travels through a public medium (Internet, RF, optical fiber).
- **Why**: No confidentiality assumed on this link.
- **Real-world engineering**: TCP/IP packets carry $C$ in encrypted form (e.g., TLS record layer).

### Stage 5 — Eavesdropping Possibility (Adversarial Model)
- **What**: Cryptanalyst Eve intercepts $C$ and attempts cryptanalysis.
- **Why**: Models real-world adversary (NSA, ISP, MITM attacker).
- **KTU High-Yield**: Security is measured in **bits of security** — the number of operations an attacker must perform.

### Stage 6 — Decryption Transformation
- **What**: Receiver applies inverse $D_k$ to recover original plaintext: $P = D_k(C) = D_k(E_k(P))$.
- **Why**: Restores intelligibility to authorized receiver.
- **Real-world engineering**: Implemented in AES-NI hardware instructions on modern CPUs.

### Stage 7 — Authentication Check
- **What**: Optional stage using **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** or **hash** to confirm integrity.
- **Why**: Pure symmetric encryption provides only confidentiality, not integrity. MAC closes this gap.

## 2.2 Shannon’s Two Foundational Principles

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept
> **Diffusion** and **Confusion** were defined by Claude Shannon in his 1949 paper *“Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems.”*

### Confusion
- **Definition**: Each bit of the ciphertext should depend on **multiple bits of the key**.
- **Purpose**: Obscures the statistical relationship between $K$ and $C$.
- **Implementation**: Achieved via **substitution boxes (S-boxes)** in block ciphers.
- **Engineering utility**: Defeats frequency analysis attacks.

### Diffusion
- **Definition**: Each bit of the plaintext should influence **multiple bits of the ciphertext**.
- **Purpose**: Spreads the redundancy of $P$ across the entire ciphertext output.
- **Implementation**: Achieved via **permutation boxes (P-boxes)** and Feistel network rounds.
- **Engineering utility**: Defeats pattern-recognition attacks on repetitive plaintext.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] Exam-Critical Equations for Symmetric Cipher Model

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Unit | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encryption Mapping** | $C = E_k(P)$ | $P \in \{0,1\}^n$, $C \in \{0,1\}^m$ | AES block encryption |
| **Decryption Mapping** | $P = D_k(C)$ | Requires $D_k = E_k^{-1}$ | AES block decryption |
| **Identity Property** | $D_k(E_k(P)) = P$ | For all $k \in K$ | Functional correctness |
| **Keyspace Size** | $\vert K \vert = 2^k$ | $k$ = key length in **bits** | DES has $2^{56}$, AES-256 has $2^{256}$ |
| **Brute-Force Effort** | $2^{k-1}$ avg. trials | Expected value for attacker | AES-128 needs $\approx 2^{127}$ ops |
| **Avalanche Bit-Flip Impact** | $\geq 50\%$ bits change | Probabilistic guarantee | Tested via SAC matrix |
| **Confusion Output Entropy** | $H(C) \approx H(P)$ | $H$ = Shannon entropy | Defeats frequency analysis |
| **Diffusion Bit-Dependency** | $\partial C_i / \partial P_j \neq 0$ | For most $(i,j)$ pairs | Avalanche coefficient |
| **Stream Cipher Output** | $C_i = P_i \oplus k_i$ | XOR with pseudo-random stream | RC4, ChaCha20 |
| **Block Cipher Modes** | $C_i = E_k(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ | CBC mode recurrence | Used in TLS, IPsec |
| **Kerckhoffs’s Principle** | Security $\subset$ Key $K$ only | Algorithm $E$ is public | Foundation of modern crypto |
| **Computational Security** | Attacker cost $> 2^{80}$ ops | NIST recommended baseline | Standard for AES-128+ |

> **Engineering Interpretation**: The table shows why AES-256 is considered *quantum-resistant to Grover’s algorithm* — the symmetric key only needs to **double in length** to maintain security against a quantum adversary.

## 2.4 Cryptography vs. Cryptanalysis vs. Steganography

| Domain | Goal | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cryptography** | Design secure ciphers | AES, RSA |
| **Cryptanalysis** | Break ciphers to recover plaintext | Brute force, differential attack |
| **Steganography** | Hide the *existence* of the message | Image LSB embedding |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Keyspace Cardinality and Brute-Force Complexity

### Problem Setup
A symmetric cipher uses a key of length $k$ bits. Determine the size of the keyspace, the worst-case brute-force attempts, and the average-case attempts.

### Step 1 — Define the Keyspace
The set of all possible keys $K$ contains every binary string of length $k$. Number of unique combinations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert K \vert &= \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{k \text{ times}} \\[4pt]
&= 2^k
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic*: Each of the $k$ bit positions is independent and binary. The product rule of counting yields $2 \cdot 2 \cdot \dots = 2^k$.

### Step 2 — Worst-Case Brute-Force Attempts
An attacker must try *every* possible key until the correct plaintext is recovered. The maximum number of trials is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{worst}} &= \vert K \vert = 2^k
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic*: If the correct key happens to be the last one tested, attacker performs $2^k$ decryptions.

### Step 3 — Average-Case Brute-Force Attempts
Assuming uniform random distribution of keys, the expected position of the correct key is the midpoint of the search space:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{0 + 1 + 2 + \dots + (2^k - 1)}{2^k} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2^k} \cdot \frac{(2^k - 1)(2^k)}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2^k - 1}{2} \approx \frac{2^k}{2} = 2^{k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic*: Sum of arithmetic sequence from 0 to $N-1$ equals $N(N-1)/2$. Dividing by $N$ trials gives average.

### Step 4 — Wall-Clock Time on Modern Hardware
Assume an attacker’s GPU cluster performs $T = 10^{12}$ decryptions per second. Time to break (average case):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{break}} &= \frac{2^{k-1}}{10^{12}} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2^{k-1}}{10^{12} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7}} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation Table

| Cipher | Key size $k$ (bits) | Keyspace $2^k$ | Avg. trials $2^{k-1}$ | Time @ $10^{12}$/s |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DES | $56$ | $7.2 \times 10^{16}$ | $3.6 \times 10^{16}$ | $\approx 1.14$ years |
| 3DES | $168$ | $3.7 \times 10^{50}$ | $1.8 \times 10^{50}$ | $\approx 5.7 \times 10^{30}$ years |
| AES-128 | $128$ | $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $1.7 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 5.4 \times 10^{18}$ years |
| AES-256 | $256$ | $1.16 \times 10^{77}$ | $5.8 \times 10^{76}$ | $\approx 1.8 \times 10^{57}$ years |

> *Conclusion*: Modern AES-128 already exceeds the age of the universe ($\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years) for brute-force attack.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Avalanche Effect on Ciphertext Diffusion

### Problem Setup
In an ideal symmetric block cipher, a single bit flip in plaintext $P$ should change approximately half the bits of ciphertext $C$. Quantify this using Hamming Distance.

### Step 1 — Define Hamming Distance
For two $n$-bit binary strings $X$ and $Y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_H(X, Y) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} X_i \oplus Y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic*: XOR returns 1 wherever bits differ. Summing counts total differing positions.

### Step 2 — Avalanche Criterion
For a $n$-bit block cipher, perfect diffusion requires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\bigl[d_H(E_k(P), E_k(P \oplus \delta))\bigr] &= \frac{n}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\delta$ is a single-bit flip in plaintext and expectation is over all $k \in K$.

### Step 3 — Avalanche Coefficient (KTU Definition)
For each bit position $j$ in the input, the dependence of ciphertext bit $i$ on plaintext bit $j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{i,j} &= \Pr\bigl[C_i(P) \neq C_i(P \oplus \delta_j)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Perfect cipher requires $a_{i,j} = 0.5$ for all $(i,j)$ pairs.

### Step 4 — Construction of Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC) Matrix
The SAC matrix is a $n \times n$ matrix $S$ where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S[i][j] &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{r=1}^{N} C_i^{(r)} \oplus C_i^{(r)}|_{P_j \text{ flipped}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an ideal cipher, $S[i][j] \to 0.5$ as $N \to \infty$.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Stream Cipher XOR Encryption

### Problem Setup
A stream cipher generates a pseudo-random keystream $K = (k_0, k_1, \dots, k_{n-1})$. Each plaintext bit is XOR-ed with the corresponding keystream bit. Prove that applying XOR twice with the same keystream recovers plaintext.

### Step 1 — Encryption
$$
\begin{aligned}
c_i &= p_i \oplus k_i, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, n-1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Decryption
$$
\begin{aligned}
d_i &= c_i \oplus k_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitution and Simplification
$$
\begin{aligned}
d_i &= c_i \oplus k_i \\[4pt]
&= (p_i \oplus k_i) \oplus k_i \quad \text{(substituting } c_i) \\[4pt]
&= p_i \oplus (k_i \oplus k_i) \quad \text{(associativity of XOR)} \\[4pt]
&= p_i \oplus 0 \quad \text{(XOR self-inverse: } x \oplus x = 0) \\[4pt]
&= p_i \quad \text{(XOR identity: } x \oplus 0 = x)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion*: The XOR operation is its own inverse — the **exact same function** encrypts and decrypts in stream ciphers. This is why stream ciphers require the keystream to be **never reused** (otherwise keystream recovery enables plaintext recovery).

## 3.4 Derivation — Shannon Entropy of Ciphertext

The Shannon entropy measures the uncertainty per bit of the message:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(P) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{(bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an English plaintext, $H(P) \approx 1.5$ bits per character (due to redundancy).
For a properly encrypted ciphertext, the attacker should observe:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(C) &\approx H_{\max} = \log_2 \vert C \vert \quad \text{(maximum entropy)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why substitution alone (monoalphabetic cipher) leaks entropy and is vulnerable to frequency analysis — only the *local* statistical structure of plaintext persists in ciphertext.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classical Symmetric Cipher Model

The figure below illustrates the canonical 5-component symmetric cipher pipeline with adversarial eavesdropping. Notice the **secret key** is held by both sender and receiver (private), while **ciphertext** flows through the *insecure channel*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Plaintext P] --> nodeE[Encryption Algorithm E]
    nodeK1[Secret Key K] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeC[Ciphertext C]
    nodeC --> nodeI((Insecure Channel))
    nodeI --> nodeD[Decryption Algorithm D]
    nodeK2[Secret Key K] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeR[Recovered Plaintext P]
    nodeC -. eavesdropping .-> nodeA[Adversary Eve]
    nodeA -. cryptanalysis attempt .-> nodeX[Plaintext or Key recovered]
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The two `Secret Key K` boxes (`nodeK1` and `nodeK2`) must be **bitwise identical**. The dotted lines from `nodeC` to `nodeA` represent the eavesdropping adversary. The `(Insecure Channel)` is rendered as a circle to emphasize it is **untrusted**.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Taxonomy of Symmetric Cipher Attacks

This diagram classifies the **four primary attack models** an adversary can mount on a symmetric cipher. The threat severity increases as the attacker gains more information.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Symmetric Cipher Attack Surface] --> type1[Ciphertext Only Attack]
    startA --> type2[Known Plaintext Attack]
    startA --> type3[Chosen Plaintext Attack]
    startA --> type4[Chosen Ciphertext Attack]
    type1 --> desc1[Attacker has C only. Hardest case. AES is provably secure.]
    type2 --> desc2[Attacker has pairs of P and C. Easier. Historical ciphers fail here.]
    type3 --> desc3[Attacker can submit chosen plaintexts to oracle. Differential cryptanalysis exploits this.]
    type4 --> desc4[Attacker can decrypt chosen ciphertexts. Most powerful. Defeated by Cramer-Shoup, RSA-OAEP.]
```

> **KTU Note**: Exam questions frequently ask, *"Which is the weakest attack model that breaks a given cipher?"* — answer always begins with **Ciphertext Only Attack** as the hardest and proceeds upward in capability.

## 4.3 Shannon Confusion-Diffusion Architectural Flow

This block diagram depicts how a Feistel-based cipher (e.g., DES, Blowfish) interleaves **confusion (S-boxes)** and **diffusion (P-boxes)** across multiple rounds to satisfy Shannon’s principles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inP[Plaintext Block 64 bit] --> splitL[L0 Left Half 32 bit]
    inP --> splitR[R0 Right Half 32 bit]
    splitR --> fbox[F Function Confusion via S box]
    keyK[Round Key Ki 48 bit] --> fbox
    splitL --> xor1[XOR with F output]
    fbox --> xor1
    xor1 --> newR[New R1]
    splitR --> newL[New L1 equals R0]
    newL --> round2[Round 2 Diffusion via Permutation]
    round2 --> roundN[Rounds 1 through N typically 16]
    roundN --> outC[Final Ciphertext Block]
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Each round performs XOR mixing (`xor1`), substitution (`F Function`), and permutation (`round2`). This iterative structure is the **engineering implementation** of Shannon’s diffusion-confusion duality.

## 4.4 Functional Block — Model Categories in Cryptography

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Cryptographic Systems] --> symm[Symmetric Key]
    root --> asymm[Asymmetric Key]
    symm --> block[Block Ciphers AES DES]
    symm --> stream[Stream Ciphers RC4 ChaCha20]
    asymm --> rsa[RSA Integer Factorization]
    asymm --> ecc[ECC Elliptic Curve Discrete Log]
    asymm --> dh[Diffie Hellman Key Exchange]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Symmetric Cipher Model. List its five essential components.**

**Model Answer:**
A symmetric cipher model is a cryptographic system where the same secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption. The model is represented as the 5-tuple $(P, C, K, E, D)$. Its five essential components are:

1. **Plaintext ($P$)** — Original readable message.
2. **Encryption Algorithm ($E$)** — Performs substitutions and transformations: $C = E_k(P)$.
3. **Secret Key ($K$)** — Shared, independent input chosen from keyspace $K$.
4. **Ciphertext ($C$)** — Encrypted, unintelligible output.
5. **Decryption Algorithm ($D$)** — Inverse transformation: $P = D_k(C)$.

The identity property $D_k(E_k(P)) = P$ must hold for all $k \in K$.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Confusion and Diffusion in cryptographic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Confusion | Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Each ciphertext bit depends on multiple key bits | Each ciphertext bit depends on multiple plaintext bits |
| **Purpose** | Hide relationship between $K$ and $C$ | Spread plaintext redundancy throughout $C$ |
| **Implementation** | Substitution boxes (S-boxes) | Permutation boxes (P-boxes) and Feistel mixing |
| **Attacks Defeated** | Frequency analysis | Pattern recognition |
| **Shannon Reference** | 1949 paper on secrecy systems | 1949 paper on secrecy systems |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of the Symmetric Cipher Model and explain each component in detail. Discuss the role of the secure out-of-band channel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Shannon’s principles of Confusion and Diffusion** with suitable examples. Show mathematically why the avalanche effect is essential for both principles.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Block Diagram** — Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.

**Component Explanation (7 Marks Breakdown):**

1. **Plaintext Source (1 mark)**: The original message $P$ in human-readable form, e.g., `"TRANSFER 10000 INR TO ACCOUNT 9876"`. The source is typically a memory buffer, file, or real-time stream. For cryptographic processing, $P$ is first converted to a binary stream using ASCII or Unicode encoding.

2. **Encryption Algorithm $E$ (1.5 marks)**: Performs complex mathematical substitutions and permutations on $P$ using the secret key $K$. Modern algorithms like AES operate on fixed-size blocks (128 bits) using S-boxes for confusion and MixColumns for diffusion.

3. **Secret Key $K$ (1.5 marks)**: An independent input selected from the keyspace $K$ of size $\vert K \vert = 2^k$. The key is the *only* secret — Kerckhoffs’s principle states that the algorithm $E$ may be publicly known without compromising security, as long as $K$ remains confidential.

4. **Decryption Algorithm $D$ (1.5 marks)**: The mathematical inverse of $E$ that recovers plaintext $P$ from ciphertext $C$ using the same key $K$. The functional correctness is verified by the identity $D_k(E_k(P)) = P$ for all $k \in K$.

5. **Insecure Channel (1 mark)**: Public transmission medium (Internet, RF) carrying ciphertext $C$. The channel is assumed to be completely eavesdroppable by adversary Eve, who can capture every bit but cannot decrypt without $K$.

6. **Out-of-Band Secure Channel (0.5 marks)**: A separate, highly-protected channel used exclusively for key distribution. In practice this may be a pre-shared offline exchange, Diffie–Hellman over a public channel, or RSA-encrypted key wrap. The expense and impracticality of this channel is the **principal limitation** of symmetric cryptography.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Confusion (3 Marks):**
Confusion ensures that each bit of the ciphertext depends on **multiple bits of the key** in a complex, non-linear way. This obscures the statistical relationship between $K$ and $C$, making it impossible for an attacker to deduce $K$ from $C$ through frequency analysis. Confusion is implemented using **Substitution Boxes (S-boxes)** that map $n$-bit inputs to $m$-bit outputs via lookup tables. Example: AES uses an 8-bit S-box that is mathematically constructed to maximize non-linearity. The output bits of an S-box are highly non-linear functions of the input bits, so even differential cryptanalysis becomes computationally intensive.

**Diffusion (2 Marks):**
Diffusion ensures that a single bit change in plaintext $P$ affects **many bits of ciphertext $C$** — specifically, approximately half the bits. This spreads plaintext redundancy throughout the ciphertext, eliminating recognizable patterns. Diffusion is implemented using **Permutation Boxes (P-boxes)** and the Feistel structure where each round’s left half becomes the next round’s right half.

**Mathematical Justification of Avalanche (2 Marks):**
The avalanche effect is quantified by the **Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall i, j \in \{0, \dots, n-1\} : \Pr[C_i(P) \neq C_i(P \oplus e_j)] &= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

where $e_j$ is the unit vector with 1 at position $j$. If SAC holds for all bit pairs, then flipping any plaintext bit changes any given ciphertext bit with probability exactly 0.5. This guarantees:
- **Confusion property**: $C$ depends strongly on $K$.
- **Diffusion property**: $C$ spreads information from $P$ widely.

Together, these defeat both frequency analysis (confusion) and pattern matching (diffusion).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of **cryptanalytic attacks** on symmetric ciphers, ranked from weakest to strongest adversary capability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the size of the keyspace and the average brute-force time required to break:
- **(i)** DES (56-bit key) — Assume $10^9$ decryptions/second.
- **(ii)** AES-128 (128-bit key) — Assume $10^{12}$ decryptions/second.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Four Attack Models (7 Marks — 1.75 each):**

1. **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA)**
   Adversary knows only ciphertext $C$. Most common and weakest attack scenario. The cipher must withstand this baseline. Cryptanalyst uses frequency analysis, statistical tests, and known-plaintext guesses. Historical ciphers (Caesar, Vigenère) fail catastrophically against COA.

2. **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA)**
   Adversary possesses a set of $(P, C)$ pairs encrypted under the same key $K$. Used to break WWII ciphers like Enigma and to attack monoalphabetic substitutions. Modern block ciphers are designed to be secure even under KPA.

3. **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA)**
   Adversary can submit any plaintext $P$ of their choice to the encryption oracle and obtain the corresponding $C$. Differential cryptanalysis by Biham and Shamir is a powerful CPA technique. AES is specifically engineered with differential probability distributions in mind.

4. **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA)**
   Adversary can submit any ciphertext $C$ to the decryption oracle and obtain the corresponding $P$. Most powerful attack. Defeated by authenticated encryption modes like AES-GCM, RSA-OAEP, and Cramer-Shoup cryptosystem.

> **Defense Hierarchy**: A cipher secure against CCA is automatically secure against CPA, KPA, and COA.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**(i) DES — 56-bit Key:**

**Step 1 — Keyspace**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert K \vert_{\text{DES}} = 2^{56} = 7.205 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Brute-Force Trials**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{avg}} = 2^{55} = 3.603 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time at $10^9$ decryptions/sec**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{break}} &= \frac{2^{55}}{10^9} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= 3.603 \times 10^{7} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.603 \times 10^{7}}{3.156 \times 10^7} \text{ years} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.14 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion*: DES-56 is breakable in **~1.14 years** on a single modern CPU — this is precisely why DES was deprecated in 2005.

**(ii) AES-128 — 128-bit Key:**

**Step 1 — Keyspace**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert K \vert_{\text{AES-128}} = 2^{128} = 3.403 \times 10^{38}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Brute-Force Trials**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{avg}} = 2^{127} = 1.701 \times 10^{38}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time at $10^{12}$ decryptions/sec**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{break}} &= \frac{2^{127}}{10^{12}} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= 1.701 \times 10^{26} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.701 \times 10^{26}}{3.156 \times 10^7} \text{ years} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.39 \times 10^{18} \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion*: AES-128 requires ~5.39 quintillion years ($\approx 5.39 \times 10^{18}$ yr) — **vastly greater than the age of the universe** ($\approx 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years). Therefore AES-128 is computationally infeasible to break via brute force.

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Keyspace formula error**: Students often write $2^{n-1}$ for *keyspace size* (this is the *average trials*, not the size). The keyspace size is always $2^n$.
> 2. **Confusion-Diffusion confusion**: Many students *interchange* the definitions — confusion is about key-ciphertext relationship, diffusion is about plaintext-ciphertext relationship.
> 3. **Missing units**: Always state time in *seconds* first, then convert to *years*. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unit errors.
> 4. **Stream cipher reuse**: Never reuse the same keystream for two different plaintexts — it leaks $P_1 \oplus P_2$.
> 5. **Block diagram completeness**: Forgetting the *secure out-of-band channel* in diagrams loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Symmetric Cipher Model)

- [x] **Symmetric Cipher Model** uses a **single shared secret key** for both encryption $E_k$ and decryption $D_k$.
- [x] **Five components**: Plaintext, Encryption Algorithm, Secret Key, Ciphertext, Decryption Algorithm.
- [x] **Key identity**: $D_k(E_k(P)) = P$ for all keys in the keyspace.
- [x] **Kerckhoffs’s Principle (1883)**: Security must reside *only* in the key, not in the secrecy of the algorithm.
- [x] **Shannon’s Confusion** — Hides relationship between $K$ and $C$. Achieved via **S-boxes**.
- [x] **Shannon’s Diffusion** — Spreads plaintext redundancy into ciphertext. Achieved via **P-boxes** and Feistel rounds.
- [x] **Avalanche Effect** — A 1-bit change in $P$ should change **~50% of bits** in $C$. Quantified by **SAC matrix**.
- [x] **Four attack models** (weakest → strongest): COA → KPA → CPA → CCA.
- [x] **Keyspace formula**: $\vert K \vert = 2^k$ where $k$ is key length in bits.
- [x] **Brute force complexity**: Average = $2^{k-1}$ trials.
- [x] **Stream ciphers** use XOR — encryption and decryption are the *same* operation: $P_i \oplus K_i$.
- [x] **Block ciphers** operate on fixed-size blocks (AES = 128 bits).
- [x] **DES key = 56 bits** (insecure since 2005). **AES-128 key = 128 bits** (NIST standard since 2001).
- [x] **Key distribution problem** is the *central weakness* of symmetric cryptography — solved in Module 3 via asymmetric key exchange.
- [x] **Symmetric encryption provides confidentiality, NOT integrity** — must add MAC/HMAC for authentication.
- [x] **Block vs Stream**: Block ciphers use ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR modes. Stream ciphers (RC4, ChaCha20) are faster for real-time data.
- [x] **Quantum security**: AES-256 maintains security even against Grover’s algorithm (post-quantum).
- [x] **Exam formula**: Always show keyspace calculation, average-case work, and wall-clock time conversion.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Symmetric Cipher Model**
