topics.title: Feistel Decryption Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feistel Decryption Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Feistel Decryption Algorithm** is the inverse transformation of the Feistel encryption process, which recovers the original plaintext block from the ciphertext block by applying the *same structural round function* but using the **round subkeys in strictly reverse order** (i.e., $K_n, K_{n-1}, K_{n-2}, \ldots, K_1$). A defining and elegant property of the Feistel cipher is that **decryption is structurally identical to encryption** — the only change is the order in which the round keys are fed into the function $F$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Feistel structure is the foundational design pattern of modern block ciphers such as **DES (Data Encryption Standard)**, **Blowfish**, **Twofish**, and **CAST-128**. The reversibility of the Feistel network — even with a *non-invertible* round function $F$ — is what makes it one of the most important theorems in classical symmetric-key cryptography.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

Imagine a **two-track escalator** going up and a parallel escalator going down in a multi-storey building.

- The **up-escalator** carries a sealed letter (plaintext) to the top floor. At each floor (round), a different security guard ($K_1, K_2, K_3$) modifies the document using their own unique stamp. When the letter reaches the top, it is *fully scrambled* (ciphertext).
- The **down-escalator** runs the *exact same machinery* in reverse. To unscramble, we don't need a different "un-stamper." We simply send the scrambled document back down, but this time the guards operate in the **opposite order**: $K_3$ first, then $K_2$, then $K_1$.
- Because the scrambling operation was just a reversible rearrangement (XOR) layered on top of an arbitrary function, the inverse walk-down with reversed keys perfectly restores the original letter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even if the function $F$ is mathematically *one-way* and *non-invertible*, the XOR operation $\oplus$ at the heart of Feistel ensures the entire cipher remains invertible as long as we run the rounds in reverse with reversed subkeys.

## 1.3 Core Components of the Feistel Network

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $PT$ | Plaintext block of length $2w$ bits |
| $CT$ | Ciphertext block of length $2w$ bits |
| $LE_i, RE_i$ | Left and Right halves at the *end* of round $i$ |
| $LD_i, RD_i$ | Left and Right halves during decryption at round $i$ |
| $F$ | Round function (can be **non-invertible**) |
| $K_i$ | Subkey (round key) for round $i$, $1 \le i \le n$ |
| $n$ | Total number of rounds |
| $\oplus$ | Bitwise XOR operation |
| $K_n \rightarrow K_1$ | Subkeys used in reverse order during decryption |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Encryption Recap (Foundation for Decryption)

A Feistel cipher splits the plaintext block $PT$ of length $2w$ bits into two equal halves: a Left half $LE_0$ of $w$ bits and a Right half $RE_0$ of $w$ bits. It then performs $n$ rounds of the following transformation:

$$
LE_{i+1} = RE_i
$$

$$
RE_{i+1} = LE_i \oplus F(RE_i, K_i)
$$

After the final round $n$, the ciphertext is the concatenation of the two halves. **Crucially**, in most Feistel constructions (e.g., DES), a *final swap* is performed: $CT = (RE_n \,\|\, LE_n)$ so that decryption has a clean structure.

## 2.2 The Decryption Algorithm — Structural Inversion

The decryption algorithm takes the ciphertext $CT = (RE_n \,\|\, LE_n)$ and feeds it as the initial state for decryption: $LD_0 = RE_n$ and $RD_0 = LE_n$ (notice the swap!). It then runs $n$ rounds using the subkeys in **reverse order** $K_n, K_{n-1}, \ldots, K_1$:

$$
LD_{i+1} = RD_i
$$

$$
RD_{i+1} = LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i})
$$

where $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$. After $n$ rounds, the output is $(LD_n, RD_n) = (LE_0, RE_0)$, which is then re-swapped to produce the plaintext $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the initial swap?** Because the encryption algorithm performs a final swap of $LE_n$ and $RE_n$ before emitting the ciphertext, decryption must "undo" that swap by *starting* with the swapped halves. This is what makes the encryption and decryption algorithms perfectly mirror images of one another.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Relation** | **Description** | **Used In** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $LE_{i+1} = RE_i$ | Left half propagation in encryption | Encryption |
| $RE_{i+1} = LE_i \oplus F(RE_i, K_i)$ | Right half XOR with round function | Encryption |
| $LD_0 = RE_n, \; RD_0 = LE_n$ | Initial state for decryption (swap) | Decryption |
| $LD_{i+1} = RD_i$ | Left half propagation in decryption | Decryption |
| $RD_{i+1} = LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i})$ | Right half inversion in decryption | Decryption |
| $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n)$ | Final swap to recover plaintext | Decryption |
| $a \oplus a = 0$ | XOR self-inverse property | Inversion proof |
| $a \oplus 0 = a$ | XOR identity property | Inversion proof |
| $a \oplus b = b \oplus a$ | XOR commutativity | Inversion proof |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Feistel decryption algorithm is the **operational core** of:

- **DES (Data Encryption Standard)** — 16-round Feistel network with 56-bit key.
- **Triple DES (3DES)** — Cascades three DES operations for increased security.
- **Blowfish** — 16-round Feistel with variable key length (32–448 bits).
- **Twofish** — A 16-round Feistel finalist of the AES competition.
- **CAST-128** — Used in PGP / GPG email encryption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** Feistel-based ciphers dominate the landscape of *software-implementable* symmetric cryptography. Their reversible-by-design property allows hardware and firmware engineers to implement *one unified circuit* for both encryption and decryption, drastically reducing silicon area, code size, and verification complexity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Proof of Correctness of Decryption

The correctness proof shows that feeding the ciphertext through the decryption rounds with the subkeys in reverse order recovers the original plaintext. We begin with the encryption equations:

$$
LE_{i+1} = RE_i \quad \text{...(Eq. E1)}
$$

$$
RE_{i+1} = LE_i \oplus F(RE_i, K_i) \quad \text{...(Eq. E2)}
$$

### Step 1 — Express $LE_i$ and $RE_i$ in terms of their successors

From Eq. E1 (encryption), the next round's left half equals the current right half. So:

$$
LE_i = RE_{i-1}
$$

Substituting this into Eq. E2:

$$
RE_{i+1} = RE_{i-1} \oplus F(RE_i, K_i)
$$

Rearranging to isolate the term that contains the round function $F$:

$$
F(RE_i, K_i) = RE_{i+1} \oplus RE_{i-1}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We are isolating $F(\cdot, K_i)$ because during decryption, $F$ will be re-evaluated with the reversed subkey. We want to show that the same $F$-value appears in both encryption and decryption rounds.

### Step 2 — Set up the decryption recurrence

For decryption, the algorithm receives $LD_0 = RE_n$ and $RD_0 = LE_n$ and runs:

$$
LD_{i+1} = RD_i \quad \text{...(Eq. D1)}
$$

$$
RD_{i+1} = LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i}) \quad \text{...(Eq. D2)}
$$

By the same algebraic manipulation, from Eq. D1:

$$
LD_i = RD_{i-1}
$$

Substituting into Eq. D2:

$$
RD_{i+1} = RD_{i-1} \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i}) \quad \text{...(Eq. D3)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the *mirror image* of the encryption relation we derived in Step 1. The structural form is identical, except the subkey index is reversed.

### Step 3 — Show equivalence by direct comparison

Compare the encryption-side result:

$$
F(RE_i, K_i) = RE_{i+1} \oplus RE_{i-1}
$$

with the decryption-side recurrence:

$$
RD_{i+1} = RD_{i-1} \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i})
$$

By the **induction hypothesis** that $RD_i = RE_{n-i}$ (the decryption right half at round $i$ equals the encryption right half at round $n-i$), we substitute:

$$
LD_i = RD_{i-1} = RE_{n-(i-1)} = RE_{n-i+1}
$$

$$
F(RD_i, K_{n-i}) = F(RE_{n-i}, K_{n-i})
$$

Plugging this into Eq. D3:

$$
RD_{i+1} = RE_{n-i+1} \oplus F(RE_{n-i}, K_{n-i})
$$

But from Eq. E2 evaluated at round $j = n-i$, we know:

$$
RE_{n-i+1} = RE_{n-i-1} \oplus F(RE_{n-i}, K_{n-i})
$$

So $F(RE_{n-i}, K_{n-i}) = RE_{n-i+1} \oplus RE_{n-i-1}$, giving:

$$
RD_{i+1} = RE_{n-i+1} \oplus (RE_{n-i+1} \oplus RE_{n-i-1}) = RE_{n-i-1}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The two identical $RE_{n-i+1}$ terms **cancel by the XOR self-inverse property** ($x \oplus x = 0$), leaving only $RE_{n-i-1}$. This confirms that $RD_{i+1} = RE_{n-i-1}$, which is the inductive step we needed.

### Step 4 — Base case and conclusion

**Base case** ($i = n$): $RD_n = RE_0 = RE_{n-n} = RE_0$. ✓

By induction, $RD_i = RE_{n-i}$ for all $i$. In particular, at $i = n$:

$$
LD_n = RD_{n-1} = RE_{n-(n-1)} = RE_1
$$

$$
RD_n = RE_0
$$

After the final swap, plaintext $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n) = (RE_0 \,\|\, RE_1)$... 

Wait — we must retrieve the *original* $LE_0$. Recall $LE_0 = RE_{-1}$ in the shifted view, but more directly: the ciphertext was emitted as $(RE_n \,\|\, LE_n)$ after the encryption's final swap. The decryption begins with $LD_0 = RE_n, RD_0 = LE_n$, runs $n$ rounds, and ends with $LD_n = LE_0, RD_n = RE_0$. The final swap yields $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n) = (RE_0 \,\|\, LE_0)$, which is exactly the original plaintext. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (4-bit Halves, 3 Rounds)

Let $w = 4$, $n = 3$, and let the round function be a trivial identity $F(x, K) = x \oplus K$.

| Round $i$ | Subkey $K_i$ (4 bits) | $LE_i$ | $RE_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | — | $0110$ | $1100$ |
| 1 | $K_1 = 1010$ | $1100$ | $0110 \oplus (1100 \oplus 1010) = 0110 \oplus 0110 = 0000$ |
| 2 | $K_2 = 0101$ | $0000$ | $1100 \oplus (0000 \oplus 0101) = 1100 \oplus 0101 = 1001$ |
| 3 | $K_3 = 1111$ | $1001$ | $0000 \oplus (1001 \oplus 1111) = 0000 \oplus 0110 = 0110$ |

**Ciphertext (after final swap):** $CT = (RE_3 \,\|\, LE_3) = 0110 \,\|\, 1001$

### Decryption (subkeys reversed: $K_3, K_2, K_1$)

| Round $i$ | Subkey $K_{n-i}$ | $LD_i$ | $RD_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | — | $0110$ (=$RE_3$) | $1001$ (=$LE_3$) |
| 1 | $K_3 = 1111$ | $1001$ | $0110 \oplus (1001 \oplus 1111) = 0110 \oplus 0110 = 0000$ |
| 2 | $K_2 = 0101$ | $0000$ | $1001 \oplus (0000 \oplus 0101) = 1001 \oplus 0101 = 1100$ |
| 3 | $K_1 = 1010$ | $1100$ | $0000 \oplus (1100 \oplus 1010) = 0000 \oplus 0110 = 0110$ |

**Plaintext (after final swap):** $PT = (RD_3 \,\|\, LD_3) = 0110 \,\|\, 1100$ ✓

The original plaintext $(LE_0 = 0110, RE_0 = 1100)$ is perfectly recovered.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Feistel Decryption Round (Single-Round Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a single round of the Feistel **decryption** algorithm, showing how the subkey is fed *in reverse order* compared to encryption.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpLD["LD_i Left Half w bits"] --> xor1
    inpRD["RD_i Right Half w bits"] --> assignLD
    inpRD --> funcF
    subK["Subkey K_n_minus_i"] --> funcF
    funcF["Round Function F"] --> xor1
    xor1["XOR Gate"] --> outRD["RD_i_plus_1 Right Half w bits"]
    assignLD[/"LD_i_plus_1 = RD_i"/] --> outLD["LD_i_plus_1 Left Half w bits"]
    style xor1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style funcF fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style subK fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The XOR gate performs $LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i})$. The arrow from $RD_i$ to the assignment block indicates the *unmodified copy* that becomes the next round's left half $LD_{i+1}$.

## 4.2 Full $n$-Round Feistel Decryption Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ct["Ciphertext Block CT of length 2w bits"] --> split
    split["Initial Swap Decompose into LD_0 and RD_0"] --> r1
    r1["Round 1 with Subkey K_n"] --> r2
    r2["Round 2 with Subkey K_n_minus_1"] --> r3
    r3["Round i with Subkey K_n_minus_i_plus_1"] --> rN
    rN["Round n with Subkey K_1"] --> finalSwap
    finalSwap["Final Swap Concatenate RD_n and LD_n"] --> pt["Recovered Plaintext PT"]
    style ct fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style pt fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style split fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px
    style finalSwap fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px
    style r1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    style r2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    style r3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    style rN fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Key Schedule Reversal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Kmaster["Master Key K of length k bits"] --> ksGen
    ksGen["Key Schedule Generator Same algorithm in reverse direction"] --> revK
    revK["Reversed Subkey Sequence K_n, K_n_minus_1, K_n_minus_2 down to K_1"] --> round1
    round1["Feistel Round 1 with K_n"] --> round2
    round2["Feistel Round 2 with K_n_minus_1"] --> roundN
    roundN["Feistel Round n with K_1"] --> plaintext
    plaintext["Plaintext PT recovered"]
    style Kmaster fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style ksGen fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style revK fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,stroke-width:2px
    style plaintext fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Mapping:** This block diagram is used in lieu of a physical circuit schematic. In a real DES decryption chip, the *same key schedule hardware* is reused, but the shift register is initialized to operate in the opposite direction (or the subkey ROM is read backwards).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Feistel Decryption Algorithm. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **Feistel Decryption Algorithm** is the inverse process used to recover the original plaintext from a ciphertext that was produced by a Feistel cipher. It is *structurally identical* to the Feistel encryption algorithm with the **only** difference being that the round subkeys are applied in **reverse order** — i.e., $K_n$ is used in round 1, $K_{n-1}$ in round 2, ..., and $K_1$ in round $n$. The ciphertext is first split into two halves which are swapped before entering round 1, and a final swap is applied at the end to obtain the plaintext. The round function $F$ can be *non-invertible* because invertibility of the entire cipher is guaranteed by the XOR operation and the reversed key schedule. **(3 marks)**

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Tip:** 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for "reverse key order", 1 mark for "non-invertible $F$ allowed" / structural identity.

---

### Question 2: Why does Feistel decryption require swapping the halves at the beginning and end? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

In a standard Feistel cipher, the **encryption algorithm** performs a final swap of the two halves before emitting the ciphertext, i.e., $CT = (RE_n \,\|\, LE_n)$ instead of $(LE_n \,\|\, RE_n)$. This swap is done so that the encryption and decryption algorithms have *identical structural form*. To compensate, the **decryption algorithm** must start by un-swapping the input halves: $LD_0 = RE_n$ and $RD_0 = LE_n$. After running $n$ rounds with reversed subkeys, the output $(LD_n, RD_n)$ must again be swapped to recover the original order: $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n)$. The two swaps — one at the start and one at the end — are therefore essential to make the decryption algorithm a perfect mirror image of encryption. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Feistel Decryption — Design and Proof [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a): Explain the Feistel decryption algorithm with a neat block diagram. Show that decryption is the inverse of encryption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Encryption Recap (2 marks):**

The Feistel encryption processes a $2w$-bit plaintext block split into $LE_0$ and $RE_0$ through $n$ rounds:

$$
LE_{i+1} = RE_i
$$

$$
RE_{i+1} = LE_i \oplus F(RE_i, K_i), \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1
$$

The final ciphertext is $CT = (RE_n \,\|\, LE_n)$ (after the final swap).

**Step 2 — Decryption Algorithm (2 marks):**

For decryption, set $LD_0 = RE_n$ and $RD_0 = LE_n$ (the ciphertext swap is undone). Run $n$ rounds with the subkeys applied in reverse order $K_n, K_{n-1}, \ldots, K_1$:

$$
LD_{i+1} = RD_i
$$

$$
RD_{i+1} = LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i}), \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1
$$

The final plaintext is $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n)$ after a final swap.

**Step 3 — Block Diagram (1 mark):** A single decryption round has the same structure as an encryption round, with inputs $LD_i, RD_i$, function $F$ applied to $RD_i$ with subkey $K_{n-i}$, and XOR with $LD_i$.

**Step 4 — Proof of Inversion (2 marks):** By induction, we show $LD_i = RE_{n-i}$ and $RD_i = LE_{n-i}$ for all $i$. *Base case* $i=0$: $LD_0 = RE_n$, $RD_0 = LE_n$ by definition. *Inductive step*: Assume true for $i$, then:

$$
LD_{i+1} = RD_i = LE_{n-i} = RE_{n-(i+1)} = RE_{n-i-1} \quad \checkmark
$$

For the right half:

$$
RD_{i+1} = LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i}) = RE_{n-i} \oplus F(LE_{n-i}, K_{n-i})
$$

But from encryption: $LE_{n-i} = RE_{n-i-1}$, and $RE_{n-i+1} = RE_{n-i-1} \oplus F(RE_{n-i}, K_{n-i})$, so:

$$
RD_{i+1} = RE_{n-i} \oplus (RE_{n-i+1} \oplus RE_{n-i-1})
$$

After expanding and using $RE_{n-i+1} = LE_{n-i+1} \oplus F(RE_{n-i+1}, K_{n-i+1})$... [continuing via the standard inversion lemma] we obtain $RD_{i+1} = LE_{n-i-1}$. This completes the induction, and at $i = n$ we recover $LD_n = LE_0$, $RD_n = RE_0$.

#### Part (b): Discuss the design parameters of the Feistel cipher that affect its security and efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block Size ($2w$):** Larger block sizes (e.g., 128 bits in modern ciphers) provide greater security and higher diffusion. A 64-bit block (DES) is now considered small. **(1 mark)**

2. **Key Size ($k$):** Larger key sizes increase the brute-force search space. DES uses 56 effective bits (vulnerable today), AES uses 128/192/256 bits. **(1 mark)**

3. **Number of Rounds ($n$):** More rounds increase security but reduce throughput. DES uses 16 rounds; modern ciphers use 10–20 rounds. **(1 mark)**

4. **Subkey Generation Algorithm:** Must generate sufficiently different round keys. Weak key schedules can lead to related-key attacks. **(1 mark)**

5. **Round Function $F$:** Should provide high *confusion* and *diffusion* (Shannon's principles). It need *not* be invertible — this is a major advantage of Feistel over SPN (Substitution-Permutation Network) ciphers. **(1.5 marks)**

6. **Software vs Hardware Speed Trade-offs:** Feistel structure is fast in software (only XOR, table lookups, and permutations). It is also compact in hardware since encryption and decryption share the same circuit. **(1 mark)**

7. **Ease of Analysis:** The Feistel structure has been extensively studied; proofs of security can be derived under idealized assumptions (e.g., Luby-Rackoff theorem: 3 rounds suffice for PRP security against chosen-plaintext attacks, 4 rounds for chosen-ciphertext). **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Worked Decryption Example [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a): Consider a Feistel cipher with block size 8 bits, $n = 2$ rounds, and the following parameters: $LE_0 = 1010$, $RE_0 = 0110$, $K_1 = 0011$, $K_2 = 1100$, and the round function $F(x, K) = (x \lll 1) \oplus K$, where $\lll 1$ denotes a 1-bit left rotation. Perform the encryption to obtain the ciphertext. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Round 1 ($i = 0$, subkey $K_1 = 0011$):**

Step 1: $F(RE_0, K_1) = F(0110, 0011)$.

Rotate $0110$ left by 1: $0110 \lll 1 = 1100$.

XOR with $K_1$: $1100 \oplus 0011 = 1111$. So $F(RE_0, K_1) = 1111$.

Step 2: $LE_1 = RE_0 = 0110$.

Step 3: $RE_1 = LE_0 \oplus F(RE_0, K_1) = 1010 \oplus 1111 = 0101$.

**Round 2 ($i = 1$, subkey $K_2 = 1100$):**

Step 1: $F(RE_1, K_2) = F(0101, 1100)$.

Rotate $0101$ left by 1: $0101 \lll 1 = 1010$.

XOR with $K_2$: $1010 \oplus 1100 = 0110$. So $F(RE_1, K_2) = 0110$.

Step 2: $LE_2 = RE_1 = 0101$.

Step 3: $RE_2 = LE_1 \oplus F(RE_1, K_2) = 0110 \oplus 0110 = 0000$.

**Final Swap and Ciphertext:**

$$
CT = (RE_2 \,\|\, LE_2) = 0000 \,\|\, 0101 = 00000101
$$

#### Part (b): Now perform decryption on the ciphertext $CT = 00000101$ using the subkeys in reverse order ($K_2, K_1$) and verify that you recover the original plaintext $(LE_0 = 1010, RE_0 = 0110)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Initial State (after un-swapping the ciphertext):**

$LD_0 = RE_2 = 0000$, $RD_0 = LE_2 = 0101$.

**Decryption Round 1 ($i = 0$, subkey $K_2 = 1100$):**

Step 1: $F(RD_0, K_2) = F(0101, 1100)$.

Rotate $0101$ left by 1: $0101 \lll 1 = 1010$.

XOR with $K_2$: $1010 \oplus 1100 = 0110$. So $F(RD_0, K_2) = 0110$.

Step 2: $LD_1 = RD_0 = 0101$.

Step 3: $RD_1 = LD_0 \oplus F(RD_0, K_2) = 0000 \oplus 0110 = 0110$.

**Decryption Round 2 ($i = 1$, subkey $K_1 = 0011$):**

Step 1: $F(RD_1, K_1) = F(0110, 0011)$.

Rotate $0110$ left by 1: $0110 \lll 1 = 1100$.

XOR with $K_1$: $1100 \oplus 0011 = 1111$. So $F(RD_1, K_1) = 1111$.

Step 2: $LD_2 = RD_1 = 0110$.

Step 3: $RD_2 = LD_1 \oplus F(RD_1, K_1) = 0101 \oplus 1111 = 1010$.

**Final Swap and Recovered Plaintext:**

$$
PT = (RD_2 \,\|\, LD_2) = 1010 \,\|\, 0110 = 10100110
$$

**Verification:** The recovered plaintext $PT = 10100110$ corresponds to $(LE_0, RE_0) = (1010, 0110)$, which is exactly the original plaintext. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial swap (1–2 marks lost):** Many students begin decryption with $LD_0 = LE_n$, $RD_0 = RE_n$. This is wrong — the swap at the start of decryption is *mandatory* to undo the encryption's final swap.
> 2. **Using subkeys in forward order (3–5 marks lost):** The single most common error. Subkeys MUST be used in reverse: $K_n$ first, $K_1$ last.
> 3. **Forgetting the final swap (1 mark lost):** After round $n$, the halves must be swapped to recover the original plaintext order.
> 4. **Confusing the XOR order in the round equation (2 marks lost):** It is $LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i})$, *not* $F(LD_i, K_{n-i}) \oplus RD_i$.
> 5. **Ignoring the bit-rotation in the round function (1–2 marks lost):** When the round function involves shifts/rotations, students often drop that step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- **Feistel Decryption = Feistel Encryption with reversed subkey order** — this is the central, board-favorite fact.
- **Initial swap** at the start of decryption: $LD_0 = RE_n$, $RD_0 = LE_n$.
- **Final swap** at the end of decryption: $PT = (RD_n \,\|\, LD_n)$.
- **Decryption round equation:** $RD_{i+1} = LD_i \oplus F(RD_i, K_{n-i})$.
- **The round function $F$ need not be invertible** — this is a *feature*, not a bug, of Feistel networks.
- **XOR properties to memorize for proofs:** self-inverse ($a \oplus a = 0$), identity ($a \oplus 0 = a$), and commutativity ($a \oplus b = b \oplus a$).
- **Luby-Rackoff Theorem (bonus):** 3-round Feistel is a secure PRP against chosen-plaintext attacks; 4 rounds for chosen-ciphertext security.
- **Real-world examples:** DES (16 rounds, 56-bit key), 3DES, Blowfish, Twofish, CAST-128.
- **Common valuation traps:** forgetting the initial swap, using subkeys in forward order, omitting the final swap, miswriting the XOR argument order.
- **Hardware advantage:** A single Feistel circuit handles both encryption and decryption — only the key schedule direction changes.
- **Block size matters:** 64-bit blocks (DES) are now considered small; modern Feistel variants use 128-bit blocks.
- **Induction proof structure:** Show $LD_i = RE_{n-i}$ and $RD_i = LE_{n-i}$ for all $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** is a symmetric-key, block cipher that was adopted by the U.S. National Bureau of Standards (now **NIST**) in 1977 as **FIPS Publication 46** for the encryption of unclassified computer data. DES operates on **64-bit plaintext blocks** under a **64-bit key**, of which **8 bits are used for parity checking** (one parity bit per byte), leaving only **56 effective key bits** for the cryptographic process. The algorithm performs **16 identical rounds** of substitution and permutation based on the classical **Feistel Network** architecture, followed by a final swap and an inverse initial permutation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Data Encryption Standard is the foundational symmetric block cipher that every KTU student of Secure Communication MUST know in detail. Questions frequently appear on Feistel structure, S-boxes, key schedule, and round function.

### Standardised Parameters (Recall Block)

| Parameter | Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Size** | **64 bits** | Fixed for all DES operations |
| **Key Length (Logical)** | **64 bits** | Raw key input |
| **Effective Key Length** | **56 bits** | 8 bits are parity (ignored) |
| **Number of Rounds** | **16 rounds** | Identical Feistel rounds |
| **Subkey Size** | **48 bits** | Generated per round |
| **Algorithm Type** | **Symmetric Block Cipher** | Same key for enc/dec |
| **Structure** | **Feistel Network** | Half-block processed per round |
| **Standard Document** | **FIPS 46-3 (1977/1999)** | NIST Federal Standard |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The 16-Lock Factory"

Imagine a 64-character secret message that you need to hide. You place it in a long box, then put it through a **16-station assembly line**. At each station:

- The box is **split into two halves**: the Left half (L) and Right half (R).
- A **worker (F-function)** at that station mixes the Right half with a **unique secret recipe (48-bit subkey)** obtained from a 16-page **master recipe book (the 56-bit key)**.
- The two halves are then **swapped** and sent to the next station.

After 16 stations, the box has been scrambled so thoroughly that, without the exact same master recipe book and the exact same 16 recipes in the exact same order, the original message **cannot** be recovered. The brilliant trick of Feistel is that the same assembly line, run **in reverse order with the same recipes**, perfectly unscrambles the box — this is why DES can use **one algorithm for both encryption and decryption**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Key Point:** DES is a *product cipher* — it combines *substitution* (S-boxes) and *permutation* (P-boxes) alternately to achieve what cryptographers call **confusion** and **diffusion**, the two pillars of Shannon's cryptographic design principle.

## 1.3 Historical Context (Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **History Timeline for KTU Answers:**
> - **1973:** NIST issues public request for a national cipher.
> - **1974:** IBM submits **Lucifer** cipher (designed by Horst Feistel's team).
> - **1976:** NSA modifies the key length (128→56 bits) and tweaks the **S-boxes** (controversially).
> - **1977:** DES adopted as **FIPS 46**.
> - **1997:** DES cracked publicly via brute-force (`DESCHALL` project, ~96 days).
> - **1999:** Cracked in **22 hours 15 minutes** (EFF's `Deep Crack`, $250,000).
> - **2005:** NIST officially **withdraws** DES; replaced by **AES (Rijndael)**.
> - **Modern role:** Survives in **3DES (Triple DES)** and legacy financial systems (e.g., ATM PINs).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The High-Level DES Pipeline

DES encryption follows a deterministic pipeline. Every step is bit-level and bit-permuting. The top-level flow is:

$$\text{Plaintext (64)} \xrightarrow{IP} L_0 \parallel R_0 \xrightarrow{16 \text{ Feistel rounds}} L_{16} \parallel R_{16} \xrightarrow{Swap} R_{16} \parallel L_{16} \xrightarrow{IP^{-1}} \text{Ciphertext (64)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **swap before $IP^{-1}$ is mandatory** because in the last Feistel round the halves are *not* swapped; the explicit swap restores the correct ordering for the inverse permutation.

### 2.1.1 The Initial Permutation (IP)

The IP is a fixed, key-independent bit-reordering of the 64 input bits. It has no cryptographic security value — its only purpose was to **simplify hardware loading** on 1970s-era byte-oriented computers. Output bits are taken from the input in a scrambled order defined by the IP table.

### 2.1.2 The Feistel Round (Core Engine)

Each of the 16 rounds takes a 64-bit input $(L_{i-1}, R_{i-1})$ and produces:

$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

where:
- $\oplus$ is the **bitwise XOR** operation.
- $F(\cdot)$ is the **round function** (a non-linear, key-dependent substitution).
- $K_i$ is the **48-bit subkey** for round $i$ (for $i = 1, 2, \dots, 16$).

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Round Function $F(R, K_i)$

The F-function is the cryptographic heart of DES. It takes a **32-bit half-block $R$** and a **48-bit subkey $K_i$** and produces a **32-bit output** through 4 sub-steps:

### Step A — Expansion (E-box)
The 32-bit $R$ is **expanded to 48 bits** by duplicating 16 of its bits (each appears in 2 overlapping 6-bit groups). This balances the bit-widths for XOR and creates the **avalanche** effect.

$$E : \{0,1\}^{32} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{48}$$

The expansion is fixed and key-independent.

### Step B — Subkey Mixing (XOR)
$$A = E(R) \oplus K_i$$

This step introduces **key dependency**. Without $K_i$, the cipher would be a fixed permutation and trivially invertible.

### Step C — S-box Substitution (8 S-boxes)
The 48-bit $A$ is split into eight **6-bit chunks** $A = a_1 \parallel a_2 \parallel \dots \parallel a_8$. Each chunk $a_j$ is fed into a corresponding **substitution box $S_j$** (for $j = 1, 2, \dots, 8$), each producing **4 output bits**:

- The **outer two bits** $a_{j,1} a_{j,6}$ of the 6-bit chunk select a **row** $r$ in $[0, 3]$.
- The **inner four bits** $a_{j,2} a_{j,3} a_{j,4} a_{j,5}$ select a **column** $c$ in $[0, 15]$.
- The 4-bit output is the entry $S_j[r][c]$.

$$S_j : \{0,1\}^{6} \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{4}, \quad S_j = \{0,1\}^{4 \times 16}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S-boxes are the ONLY non-linear component of DES.** They provide **confusion** (Shannon). All other operations are linear. Without non-linearity, the cipher is vulnerable to *linear cryptanalysis* (Matsui, 1993).

The 8 outputs (each 4 bits) are concatenated to form a **32-bit** block $B$.

### Step D — P-box Permutation
The 32-bit $B$ is permuted bit-by-bit using a fixed **P-box (Straight Permutation)** table, producing the 32-bit output of F.

$$F(R, K_i) = P(S_1 \dots S_8 (E(R) \oplus K_i))$$

> [!NOTE]
> The P-box spreads the influence of each S-box output bit across all 8 S-boxes of the **next round** — this provides **diffusion** (Shannon). Together with S-box confusion, this is the cryptographic core of DES.

## 2.3 The Key Schedule (Subkey Generation)

The 64-bit user-supplied key is first passed through **PC-1 (Permuted Choice 1)**, which discards the 8 parity bits and outputs a **56-bit permuted key**. This is split into two 28-bit halves $C_0$ and $D_0$.

For each round $i$:

$$C_i = \text{LSB}^{n_i}(C_{i-1}), \quad D_i = \text{LSB}^{n_i}(D_{i-1})$$

where $\text{LSB}^n$ denotes a **circular left shift by $n$ bit positions**. The shift schedule is: $n = 1$ for rounds $1, 2, 9, 16$; $n = 2$ for all other rounds.

The 56-bit $(C_i \parallel D_i)$ is then passed through **PC-2 (Permuted Choice 2)**, which selects 48 of the 56 bits to form the round subkey $K_i$.

$$K_i = PC_2(C_i \parallel D_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, 16$$

## 2.4 DES Decryption

Decryption uses **the exact same algorithm** as encryption, but with the subkeys applied in **reverse order**:

$$K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_2, K_1$$

This is the beauty of the **Feistel structure** — encryption and decryption are symmetric.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table. Every line is fair game in a KTU Part A or Part B question.

| Concept | Mathematical Statement | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Feistel Round** | $L_i = R_{i-1}; \quad R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$ | Core recurrence |
| **Round Function** | $F(R, K) = P(S_1 \dots S_8(E(R) \oplus K))$ | Non-linear core |
| **Expansion** | $E : 32 \to 48$ bits | 16 bits duplicated |
| **S-boxes** | $S_j : 6 \to 4$ bits, $j = 1 \dots 8$ | Only non-linearity |
| **P-box** | $P : 32 \to 32$ bits (permutation) | Provides diffusion |
| **Block size** | $n = 64$ bits | |
| **Key size** | $k = 56$ effective bits | 8 parity bits discarded |
| **Rounds** | $r = 16$ | |
| **Subkey size** | $K_i = 48$ bits | From PC-2 |
| **Complement Property** | $E_K(P) = \overline{E_{\overline{K}}(\overline{P})}$ | Halves brute-force search |
| **Brute-force Complexity** | $2^{56} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{16}$ keys | Insecure today |
| **Weak Keys** | $E_K(E_K(P)) = P$ | 4 weak keys exist |
| **Semi-Weak Keys** | $E_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(P)) = P$ | 6 pairs (12 keys) |
| **Possible Weak Keys** | $E_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(P)) = E_{K_3}(E_{K_4}(P))$ | 48 keys in 12 quadruples |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write the complement property as $E_K(P) = E_{\overline{K}}(\overline{P})$ — this is **wrong**. The correct statement is the more nuanced one above. Marks are lost in Part A for this.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of DES (Historical) |
|---|---|
| **Banking** | Early ATM PIN encryption, magnetic-stripe data |
| **Government** | US Federal unclassified communications (pre-2005) |
| **Network Protocols** | Legacy SSL/TLS, Kerberos v4 |
| **Disk Encryption** | CBC-mode DES for early disk/file encryption |
| **Password Hashing** | Unix `crypt(3)` (DES-based) |
| **Smart Cards** | EMV chip card authentication (now 3DES / AES) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Feistel Decryption Identity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is the most-asked 14-mark concept** on Feistel ciphers. Memorise it line-by-line.

Let $L_i, R_i$ denote the outputs of round $i$ during **encryption** with subkey $K_i$.

From the Feistel recurrence:

$$L_i = R_{i-1}$$

$$R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1}, K_i)$$

**Goal:** Show that feeding ciphertext through the same Feistel structure with subkeys in reverse order recovers the plaintext.

### Step 1 — Begin decryption from round-16 outputs

During encryption, after round 16 we apply the explicit swap, so the ciphertext registers hold:

$$L_{16}^{cipher} = R_{16}^{enc}, \quad R_{16}^{cipher} = L_{16}^{enc}$$

For clarity, in decryption let us re-label the input to the first decryption round as $(L_0', R_0') = (R_{16}^{cipher}, L_{16}^{cipher}) = (R_{16}^{enc}, L_{16}^{enc})$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Feistel round using $K_{16}$

$$L_1' = R_0' = L_{16}^{enc}$$

$$R_1' = L_0' \oplus F(R_0', K_{16}) = R_{16}^{enc} \oplus F(L_{16}^{enc}, K_{16})$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the encryption identity

From the encryption recurrence for round 16:

$$R_{16}^{enc} = L_{15}^{enc} \oplus F(L_{16}^{enc}, K_{16})$$

Therefore:

$$R_1' = [L_{15}^{enc} \oplus F(L_{16}^{enc}, K_{16})] \oplus F(L_{16}^{enc}, K_{16}) = L_{15}^{enc}$$

because $X \oplus Y \oplus Y = X$ (XOR is self-inverse).

### Step 4 — Inductive Step

After the $j$-th decryption round (using $K_{16-j+1}$), the registers hold:

$$L_j' = L_{16-j}^{enc}, \quad R_j' = R_{16-j}^{enc}$$

The induction step is identical: substitute the encryption identity for the next round, cancel the duplicated F-term with XOR, and recover the previous encryption state.

### Step 5 — Final State

After 16 decryption rounds (using $K_{16}, K_{15}, \dots, K_1$ in that order):

$$L_{16}' = L_0^{enc}, \quad R_{16}' = R_0^{enc}$$

The final swap places $(L_0^{enc}, R_0^{enc})$ into the correct order, and applying $IP^{-1}$ yields the original plaintext.

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The proof works because $F$ is **not required to be invertible**. The XOR cancellation in Step 3 is what makes Feistel ciphers so elegant — even a "bad" F-function is acceptable, as long as it is key-dependent and non-linear.

## 3.2 Worked Example: One Round of DES with Trivial 8-bit Mini-DES

To make the F-function concrete, consider a **toy 8-bit Feistel block cipher** that mimics DES (often called **mini-DES** in KTU textbooks). Assume:
- Block size = 8 bits (4-bit L, 4-bit R)
- Subkey $K_i$ = 4 bits
- $F(R, K) = $ rotation of $(R \oplus K)$ by 1 bit to the left

**Plaintext:** $L_0 = 1010, \ R_0 = 0110$ (i.e., 0xA6)
**Subkey for round 1:** $K_1 = 0011$

### Step 1 — Compute the F-function

$$R_0 \oplus K_1 = 0110 \oplus 0011 = 0101$$

Rotate left by 1:

$$F(R_0, K_1) = 1010$$

### Step 2 — Apply Feistel recurrence

$$L_1 = R_0 = 0110$$

$$R_1 = L_0 \oplus F(R_0, K_1) = 1010 \oplus 1010 = 0000$$

### Step 3 — Round 2 with $K_2 = 1100$

$$R_1 \oplus K_2 = 0000 \oplus 1100 = 1100$$

Rotate left by 1: $F(R_1, K_2) = 1001$

$$L_2 = R_1 = 0000$$

$$R_2 = L_1 \oplus F(R_1, K_2) = 0110 \oplus 1001 = 1111$$

### Step 4 — Final state after 2 rounds

Ciphertext half-blocks: $L_2 = 0000, \ R_2 = 1111$ → Ciphertext $= 0000\,1111$.

### Step 5 — Verify by decrypting with reversed subkeys

Start with $L_0' = 1111, \ R_0' = 0000$, use $K_2$ first:

$$F(R_0', K_2) = F(0000, 1100) = 1001$$

$$L_1' = 0000, \quad R_1' = 1111 \oplus 1001 = 0110$$

Now use $K_1$:

$$F(R_1', K_1) = F(0110, 0011) = 1010$$

$$L_2' = 0110, \quad R_2' = 0000 \oplus 1010 = 1010$$

Final swap: $(R_2', L_2') = (1010, 0110)$ → $10100110$ = original plaintext. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams:** If a numerical DES problem is given, always show the XOR step explicitly, label each intermediate, and state the final bit pattern in hexadecimal. Examiners reward tidy, labelled work.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DES vs AES vs 3DES

| Parameter | DES | 3DES (Triple DES) | AES |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Block size** | 64 bits | 64 bits | 128 bits |
| **Key size** | 56 bits | 112 or 168 bits | 128/192/256 bits |
| **Rounds** | 16 | 48 (3 × 16) | 10/12/14 |
| **Structure** | Feistel | Feistel | SPN |
| **S-box count** | 8 (6→4) | 8 (×3) | 1 (16-byte, algebraic) |
| **Effective security** | $\approx 2^{56}$ | $\approx 2^{112}$ | $\geq 2^{128}$ |
| **Speed** | Fast (legacy) | Slow (3× DES) | Very fast |
| **Status** | Withdrawn (2005) | Legacy NIST approved | Current standard |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level DES Encryption Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plaintext 64 bits] --> IP[Initial Permutation IP]
    IP --> LR0[L0 parallel R0]
    LR0 --> R1[Feistel Round 1 K1]
    R1 --> R2[Feistel Round 2 K2]
    R2 --> R3[Feistel Round 3 K3]
    R3 --> R4[Feistel Round 4 K4]
    R4 --> R5[Feistel Round 5 K5]
    R5 --> R6[Feistel Round 6 K6]
    R6 --> R7[Feistel Round 7 K7]
    R7 --> R8[Feistel Round 8 K8]
    R8 --> R9[Feistel Round 9 K9]
    R9 --> R10[Feistel Round 10 K10]
    R10 --> R11[Feistel Round 11 K11]
    R11 --> R12[Feistel Round 12 K12]
    R12 --> R13[Feistel Round 13 K13]
    R13 --> R14[Feistel Round 14 K14]
    R14 --> R15[Feistel Round 15 K15]
    R15 --> R16[Feistel Round 16 K16]
    R16 --> SW[Explicit Swap]
    SW --> IPI[Inverse Permutation IPinv]
    IPI --> C[Ciphertext 64 bits]
```

## 4.2 Single Feistel Round (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Lprev[L i minus 1 32 bits] --> XOR1[XOR]
    Rprev[R i minus 1 32 bits] --> EXP[E Box 32 to 48]
    Rprev --> LInew[L i equals R i minus 1]
    Ki[K i 48 bits] --> XOR1
    EXP --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> SBX[S Boxes 8 of 6 to 4]
    SBX --> PBX[P Box 32 bit permutation]
    PBX --> Fout[F Output 32 bits]
    Fout --> XOR2[XOR with L i minus 1]
    XOR2 --> RInew[R i 32 bits]
    Lprev --> XOR2
```

## 4.3 DES Key Schedule (Subkey Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[64 bit Key] --> PC1[PC1 drops 8 parity bits]
    PC1 --> C0D0[C0 28 bits and D0 28 bits]
    C0D0 --> LS1[Left Shift by 1]
    LS1 --> PC2a[PC2 selects 48 bits]
    PC2a --> K1[K1 48 bits]
    LS1 --> LS2[Left Shift by 1]
    LS2 --> PC2b[PC2 selects 48 bits]
    PC2b --> K2[K2 48 bits]
    LS2 --> LS3[Continue for all 16 rounds]
    LS3 --> K16[K16 48 bits]
```

## 4.4 The Round Function F — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Rin[R 32 bits] --> EB[E Expansion 32 to 48]
    EB --> XORK[XOR with K i]
    SubK[K i 48 bits] --> XORK
    XORK --> A48[48 bit intermediate A]
    A48 --> S1[S1 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S2[S2 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S3[S3 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S4[S4 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S5[S5 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S6[S6 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S7[S7 6 to 4]
    A48 --> S8[S8 6 to 4]
    S1 --> B32[32 bit intermediate B]
    S2 --> B32
    S3 --> B32
    S4 --> B32
    S5 --> B32
    S6 --> B32
    S7 --> B32
    S8 --> B32
    B32 --> PB[P Permutation 32 to 32]
    PB --> Fout[F R K i 32 bits]
```

## 4.5 Decryption Architecture (Same Algorithm, Reversed Subkeys)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Ciphertext 64 bits] --> IP2[Initial Permutation IP]
    IP2 --> Ld0[Ld0 parallel Rd0]
    Ld0 --> DR1[Feistel Round with K16]
    DR1 --> DR2[Feistel Round with K15]
    DR2 --> DR3[Feistel Round with K14]
    DR3 --> DRdots[Continue with K13 to K2]
    DRdots --> DR16[Feistel Round with K1]
    DR16 --> SWD[Explicit Swap]
    SWD --> IPID[Inverse Permutation IPinv]
    IPID --> PP[Recovered Plaintext 64 bits]
```
