### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for last-minute review):**

- ✅ **SubBytes is the FIRST step of every AES round** (including the final round).
- ✅ The S-Box is a **16 × 16 = 256-entry table** with 8-bit entries.
- ✅ **Lookup Rule:** For byte `{xy}`, `x` = row (upper nibble), `y` = column (lower nibble).
- ✅ **Construction Order:** (1) Multiplicative Inverse in GF(2⁸) → (2) Affine Transform over GF(2). **Never reverse this order.**
- ✅ **Irreducible Polynomial:** `m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1` (0x11B).
- ✅ **Affine Constant:** `c = 0x63 = 01100011₂`.
- ✅ **Affine Formula:** `b'_i = b_i ⊕ b_{(i+4) mod 8} ⊕ b_{(i+5) mod 8} ⊕ b_{(i+6) mod 8} ⊕ b_{(i+7) mod 8} ⊕ c_i`.
- ✅ **Edge Cases to Memorize:**
  - `SBox[0x00] = 0x63` (zero maps to non-zero)
  - `SBox[0x01] = 0x7C` (one does NOT map to one)
  - `SBox[0xFF] = 0x16`
  - `SBox[0x53] = 0xED` (NIST FIPS-197 standard test vector)
- ✅ **SubBytes provides NON-LINEARITY** — the only non-linear step in AES. This is its sole cryptographic purpose.
- ✅ **InvSubBytes** uses the **Inverse S-Box** (a *different* table, not the reverse of the original S-Box).
- ✅ **Bijectivity:** The S-Box is a permutation of all 256 byte values; no collisions exist.
- ✅ **Non-linearity Value:** 112 (out of max 120) — extremely close to optimal.
- ✅ **Position in AES Round:** SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey (omitting MixColumns in the final round).
- ✅ **No Fixed Points:** For all `b ≠ 0`, `SBox[b] ≠ b`. This is by design.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Formula Flashcard:**
> SubBytes = (GF(2⁸) Multiplicative Inverse) ∘ (Affine Transformation with c=0x63)
> "Inverse first, Affine second. Always. Forever. No exceptions."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shift Row Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shift Row Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ShiftRows Transformation** is a byte-level transposition operation used in the **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** block cipher, operating on the internal **State matrix** of dimensions **4 × 4** bytes. It is a linear diffusion primitive that cyclically rotates (left-shifts) the bytes of the last three rows of the State by fixed offsets **0, 1, 2, and 3** positions respectively, while the first row remains unchanged.

The State matrix $S$ before the transformation is:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3} \end{bmatrix}
$$

After applying ShiftRows, the transformed State $S'$ becomes:

$$
S' = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} & s_{1,0} \\ s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} & s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} \\ s_{3,3} & s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ShiftRows is one of the **three non-key-dependent layers** in AES that provide *diffusion*. It is part of the round transformation applied for **Nr − 1** rounds (where $N_r = 10, 12, 14$ for AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 respectively).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **4-row, 4-column classroom** of students holding numbered cards. The teacher (AES algorithm) gives the instruction:

- **Row 0** (front bench): *"Stay in your seats."* → **No shift**
- **Row 1** (second bench): *"Everyone move one seat to the left. The leftmost person wraps around to the rightmost seat."* → **Shift left by 1**
- **Row 2** (third bench): *"Everyone move two seats to the left."* → **Shift left by 2**
- **Row 3** (last bench): *"Everyone move three seats to the left."* → **Shift left by 3**

This circular shuffling is exactly what ShiftRows does to bytes inside the AES State matrix. It scatters byte positions across columns so that after the next layer (MixColumns), even a **single changed input bit** influences a large portion of the encrypted output — this is the cryptographic property of **diffusion**.

## 1.3 Key Parameters at a Glance

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Block Size** | **128 bits** (always) |
| **State Matrix Size** | **4 × 4 bytes** |
| **Row 0 Shift Offset** | **0** (no shift) |
| **Row 1 Shift Offset** | **1 byte (left)** |
| **Row 2 Shift Offset** | **2 bytes (left)** |
| **Row 3 Shift Offset** | **3 bytes (left)** |
| **Operation Type** | **Byte-level transposition (permutation)** |
| **Key-Dependence** | **No** (key-independent) |
| **Inverse Operation** | **InvShiftRows — right shifts of 0, 1, 2, 3** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic — Why ShiftRows Exists

ShiftRows is designed with three explicit cryptographic goals:

1. **Column Mixing across Rounds:** Because the bytes within each column are now drawn from different original columns, the subsequent **MixColumns** step operates on a *mixed* set of inputs, spreading the influence of any one byte across the entire output.
2. **Resistance to Trivial Attacks:** Without shifting, columns would be processed independently by MixColumns, allowing attackers to mount *column-by-column* algebraic attacks.
3. **Ensuring Avalanche Effect:** Combined with SubBytes and MixColumns, ShiftRows guarantees that changing **one bit** in the plaintext or key ultimately changes roughly **half the ciphertext bits** after a few rounds (typically by round 2 or 3).

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Shift

For a row index $i$ (where $i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$), the new column index $j'$ of the byte originally at column $j$ is:

$$
j' = (j + \text{shift}_i) \mod 4
$$

where $\text{shift}_i$ is the row-specific offset:

$$
\text{shift}_i = i \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, 3
$$

So the byte $s_{i,j}$ is moved to position $s_{i, (j+i) \bmod 4}$.

## 2.3 InvShiftRows (The Decryption Counterpart)

During AES decryption, the inverse transformation is applied: each row is cyclically shifted to the **right** by the same offsets. Equivalently, the byte at position $s_{i, j}$ originated from $s_{i, (j - i) \bmod 4}$.

$$
\text{InvShiftRows:} \quad s_{i,j} = s'_{i, (j - i) \bmod 4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** ShiftRows and InvShiftRows are **self-inverse only when paired properly**. ShiftRows applied twice is NOT the identity because shifts are by 1, 2, 3 — applying them again yields shifts of 2, 4, 6, which mod 4 become 2, 0, 2 (not identity). The correct inverse is *right-shift* by the same amount.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| State dimensions | $4 \times 4$ bytes | Fixed for all AES variants |
| Total bytes in state | $\mathbf{16}$ bytes = **128 bits** | One AES block |
| Row $i$ shift count | $C_i = i$ for $i \in \{0,1,2,3\}$ | Left cyclic shift |
| Byte position mapping | $s_{i,j} \rightarrow s_{i, (j+i) \bmod 4}$ | Forward ShiftRows |
| Inverse mapping | $s_{i,j} = s'_{i, (j-i) \bmod 4}$ | For InvShiftRows |
| Total rounds $N_r$ | **10, 12, 14** | For 128, 192, 256-bit keys |
| ShiftRows applied | $N_r$ times | Every round (including last for AES-128? No — only in main rounds) |
| Computational cost | **Negligible** (just byte moves) | 0 XOR, 0 S-box lookups |
| Cryptographic role | **Diffusion** (Shannon) | Spreads byte influence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The diffusion property provided by ShiftRows (and the wider AES cipher) is the same reason why financial transactions on HTTPS, secure file storage on cloud platforms, and military-grade VPNs are *fast enough* for real-time use yet *hard enough* to break with current classical computing power. Modern CPUs (Intel/AMD) include **AES-NI hardware instructions** that implement ShiftRows, SubBytes, MixColumns, and AddRoundKey directly in silicon for trillions of operations per second.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Applying ShiftRows to a Given State

**Problem:** Given the State matrix $S$ below (in hexadecimal), apply the ShiftRows transformation and write the output State $S'$.

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} \text{0A} & \text{12} & \text{1C} & \text{26} \\ \text{34} & \text{3E} & \text{48} & \text{52} \\ \text{5C} & \text{66} & \text{70} & \text{7A} \\ \text{84} & \text{8E} & \text{98} & \text{A2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify each row and its shift offset

From the formula $C_i = i$:

- Row 0: shift = **0** bytes (no change)
- Row 1: shift = **1** byte (left)
- Row 2: shift = **2** bytes (left)
- Row 3: shift = **3** bytes (left)

### Step 2 — Apply no shift to Row 0

Row 0 unchanged: $[\text{0A}, \text{12}, \text{1C}, \text{26}]$

### Step 3 — Apply shift of 1 to Row 1

Original Row 1: $[\text{34}, \text{3E}, \text{48}, \text{52}]$

Left-shift by 1: take bytes from positions $(0, 1, 2, 3) \rightarrow (1, 2, 3, 0)$:

New Row 1: $[\text{3E}, \text{48}, \text{52}, \text{34}]$

### Step 4 — Apply shift of 2 to Row 2

Original Row 2: $[\text{5C}, \text{66}, \text{70}, \text{7A}]$

Left-shift by 2: take bytes from positions $(0, 1, 2, 3) \rightarrow (2, 3, 0, 1)$:

New Row 2: $[\text{70}, \text{7A}, \text{5C}, \text{66}]$

### Step 5 — Apply shift of 3 to Row 3

Original Row 3: $[\text{84}, \text{8E}, \text{98}, \text{A2}]$

Left-shift by 3: take bytes from positions $(0, 1, 2, 3) \rightarrow (3, 0, 1, 2)$:

New Row 3: $[\text{A2}, \text{84}, \text{8E}, \text{98}]$

### Step 6 — Assemble the Final Output State

$$
S' = \begin{bmatrix} \text{0A} & \text{12} & \text{1C} & \text{26} \\ \text{3E} & \text{48} & \text{52} & \text{34} \\ \text{70} & \text{7A} & \text{5C} & \text{66} \\ \text{A2} & \text{84} & \text{8E} & \text{98} \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mapping a Single Byte's New Position

**Problem:** Determine the new position of the byte at position $(r, c) = (2, 1)$ after applying ShiftRows.

### Step 1 — Identify row index

Row $r = 2$, so the shift amount is $C_r = 2$.

### Step 2 — Apply the position formula

$$
c' = (c + C_r) \bmod 4 = (1 + 2) \bmod 4 = 3 \bmod 4 = 3
$$

### Step 3 — State the answer

The byte originally at $(2, 1)$ moves to position $(2, 3)$.

**Verification with general formula:** $s_{i, (j+i) \bmod 4} \Rightarrow s_{2, (1+2)\bmod 4} = s_{2, 3}$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Applying InvShiftRows

**Problem:** Given the State $S'$ from Example 1, recover the original State $S$ using InvShiftRows.

### Step 1 — Apply right-shift of 0 to Row 0

Row 0 unchanged: $[\text{0A}, \text{12}, \text{1C}, \text{26}]$

### Step 2 — Apply right-shift of 1 to Row 1

Current Row 1: $[\text{3E}, \text{48}, \text{52}, \text{34}]$

Right-shift by 1: positions $(0,1,2,3) \rightarrow (3,0,1,2)$:

New Row 1: $[\text{34}, \text{3E}, \text{48}, \text{52}]$

### Step 3 — Apply right-shift of 2 to Row 2

Current Row 2: $[\text{70}, \text{7A}, \text{5C}, \text{66}]$

Right-shift by 2: positions $(0,1,2,3) \rightarrow (2,3,0,1)$:

New Row 2: $[\text{5C}, \text{66}, \text{70}, \text{7A}]$

### Step 4 — Apply right-shift of 3 to Row 3

Current Row 3: $[\text{A2}, \text{84}, \text{8E}, \text{98}]$

Right-shift by 3: positions $(0,1,2,3) \rightarrow (1,2,3,0)$:

New Row 3: $[\text{84}, \text{8E}, \text{98}, \text{A2}]$

### Step 5 — Confirm recovery

$$
S_{\text{recovered}} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{0A} & \text{12} & \text{1C} & \text{26} \\ \text{34} & \text{3E} & \text{48} & \text{52} \\ \text{5C} & \text{66} & \text{70} & \text{7A} \\ \text{84} & \text{8E} & \text{98} & \text{A2} \end{bmatrix} = S \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.4 Verification of the Diffusion Property

Let us consider what happens if we flip the **last bit** of byte $s_{0,0}$ (e.g., change $\text{0A}$ to $\text{0B}$). After ShiftRows, the byte $\text{0B}$ is still in position $(0, 0)$ because Row 0 is not shifted. However, the *columns* seen by MixColumns are now:

- Column 0: $[\text{0B}, \text{3E}, \text{70}, \text{A2}]^T$ — *mixing changed and unchanged bytes*
- Column 1: $[\text{12}, \text{48}, \text{7A}, \text{84}]^T$
- Column 2: $[\text{1C}, \text{52}, \text{5C}, \text{8E}]^T$
- Column 3: $[\text{26}, \text{34}, \text{66}, \text{98}]^T$

Column 0 now contains a "poisoned" byte, which after MixColumns will spread the bit-flip to **all four bytes** of that column. Across multiple rounds, the influence propagates to the entire 128-bit block — this is the **avalanche effect**, the central security guarantee of AES.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Position of ShiftRows within the AES Round

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["128-bit Plaintext Input"] --> B["Initial AddRoundKey"]
    B --> C["AES Round 1 to Round Nr-1"]
    C --> D["SubBytes Step"]
    D --> E["ShiftRows Step - Current Topic"]
    E --> F["MixColumns Step"]
    F --> G["AddRoundKey with Round Key"]
    G --> H{"Is this the last round?"}
    H -->|No| C
    H -->|Yes| I["Final Round - SubBytes"]
    I --> J["Final Round - ShiftRows"]
    J --> K["Final Round - AddRoundKey"]
    K --> L["128-bit Ciphertext Output"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Mechanics of ShiftRows

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Input State Matrix 4x4"] --> R0["Row 0 - No Shift"]
    S --> R1["Row 1 - Left Shift by 1"]
    S --> R2["Row 2 - Left Shift by 2"]
    S --> R3["Row 3 - Left Shift by 3"]
    R0 --> M0["Byte at position 0"]
    R0 --> M1["Byte at position 1"]
    R0 --> M2["Byte at position 2"]
    R0 --> M3["Byte at position 3"]
    R1 --> N0["Byte from original col 1"]
    R1 --> N1["Byte from original col 2"]
    R1 --> N2["Byte from original col 3"]
    R1 --> N3["Byte from original col 0"]
    R2 --> P0["Byte from original col 2"]
    R2 --> P1["Byte from original col 3"]
    R2 --> P2["Byte from original col 0"]
    R2 --> P3["Byte from original col 1"]
    R3 --> Q0["Byte from original col 3"]
    R3 --> Q1["Byte from original col 0"]
    R3 --> Q2["Byte from original col 1"]
    R3 --> Q3["Byte from original col 2"]
    M0 --> OUT["Output State Matrix 4x4"]
    M1 --> OUT
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    N0 --> OUT
    N1 --> OUT
    N2 --> OUT
    N3 --> OUT
    P0 --> OUT
    P1 --> OUT
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    Q0 --> OUT
    Q1 --> OUT
    Q2 --> OUT
    Q3 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematic — Row-by-Row Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input State"]
        I0["Row 0: A B C D"]
        I1["Row 1: E F G H"]
        I2["Row 2: I J K L"]
        I3["Row 3: M N O P"]
    end
    subgraph SHIFT["ShiftRows Applied"]
        S0["Row 0 unchanged: A B C D"]
        S1["Row 1 shifted left by 1: F G H E"]
        S2["Row 2 shifted left by 2: K L I J"]
        S3["Row 3 shifted left by 3: P M N O"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output State"]
        O0["Row 0: A B C D"]
        O1["Row 1: F G H E"]
        O2["Row 2: K L I J"]
        O3["Row 3: P M N O"]
    end
    I0 --> S0 --> O0
    I1 --> S1 --> O1
    I2 --> S2 --> O2
    I3 --> S3 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Data-Flow Diagram — ShiftRows in Cryptographic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SubBytes Output 4x4 State"] --> B["ShiftRows"]
    B --> C["MixColumns"]
    C --> D["AddRoundKey"]
    D --> E["Next Round Input or Final Output"]
    F["Cryptographic Property: Diffusion"] --> B
    G["Key-Dependence: None"] --> B
    H["Inverse: InvShiftRows"] --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the ShiftRows transformation in AES. How many rows are shifted and by what offsets?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **ShiftRows** is a transposition operation performed on the 4×4 byte **State** matrix in each round of AES (except the key-schedule-free final layer does include it; ShiftRows runs in every round including the last). The first row is **not shifted**. The second, third, and fourth rows are cyclically shifted to the **left by 1, 2, and 3 byte positions** respectively. This operation provides **diffusion**, ensuring byte positions are redistributed across columns so that MixColumns mixes data from different original columns, strengthening the cipher's resistance to cryptanalysis.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Distinguish between ShiftRows and SubBytes in AES with respect to their cryptographic role and computational nature.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | SubBytes | ShiftRows |
|---|---|---|
| **Cryptographic Role** | Provides **confusion** (non-linear S-box substitution) | Provides **diffusion** (linear byte permutation) |
| **Operation Type** | Non-linear byte substitution using a fixed 256-entry S-box | Linear cyclic rotation of bytes within rows |
| **Key-Dependence** | Key-independent | Key-independent |
| **Computation Cost** | Requires S-box lookup (1 memory access per byte = 16 per round) | Negligible — just byte reordering |
| **Effect on Bytes** | Changes byte *values* | Changes byte *positions* only |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Comprehensive Derivation (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular Exam]**

**(a)** Explain the ShiftRows transformation in AES with the help of a State diagram. Clearly show the byte movement for each row. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the ShiftRows transformation to the following State matrix and show the resulting State. Also explain the cryptographic significance of this step. **(7 marks)**

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} \text{D4} & \text{E0} & \text{B8} & \text{1E} \\ \text{BF} & \text{B4} & \text{41} & \text{27} \\ \text{5D} & \text{52} & \text{11} & \text{98} \\ \text{30} & \text{AE} & \text{F1} & \text{E5} \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**

ShiftRows is a byte-wise transposition operation performed on the AES State matrix (4 rows × 4 columns) as part of the round transformation. It cyclically shifts the second, third, and fourth rows to the left by 1, 2, and 3 bytes respectively; the first row remains unchanged.

**Diagram explanation (3 marks):**

Given State $S$:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3} \end{bmatrix}
$$

After ShiftRows:

$$
S' = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} & s_{1,0} \\ s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} & s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} \\ s_{3,3} & s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step-by-step logic (2 marks):**

- Row 0: shift = 0, no change.
- Row 1: byte at column $j$ moves to column $(j+1) \bmod 4$.
- Row 2: byte at column $j$ moves to column $(j+2) \bmod 4$.
- Row 3: byte at column $j$ moves to column $(j+3) \bmod 4$.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify rows (1 mark):**

- Row 0: $[\text{D4}, \text{E0}, \text{B8}, \text{1E}]$
- Row 1: $[\text{BF}, \text{B4}, \text{41}, \text{27}]$
- Row 2: $[\text{5D}, \text{52}, \text{11}, \text{98}]$
- Row 3: $[\text{30}, \text{AE}, \text{F1}, \text{E5}]$

**Step 2 — Apply shifts (3 marks):**

- Row 0 unchanged: $[\text{D4}, \text{E0}, \text{B8}, \text{1E}]$
- Row 1 shifted left by 1: $[\text{B4}, \text{41}, \text{27}, \text{BF}]$
- Row 2 shifted left by 2: $[\text{11}, \text{98}, \text{5D}, \text{52}]$
- Row 3 shifted left by 3: $[\text{E5}, \text{30}, \text{AE}, \text{F1}]$

**Step 3 — Write final State (1 mark):**

$$
S' = \begin{bmatrix} \text{D4} & \text{E0} & \text{B8} & \text{1E} \\ \text{B4} & \text{41} & \text{27} & \text{BF} \\ \text{11} & \text{98} & \text{5D} & \text{52} \\ \text{E5} & \text{30} & \text{AE} & \text{F1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Cryptographic significance (2 marks):**

ShiftRows provides **diffusion** in AES, a property outlined by **Claude Shannon**. It ensures that bytes from different original columns are brought into the same column before MixColumns operates. This guarantees the **avalanche effect**: a single-bit change in the input eventually diffuses across the entire 128-bit ciphertext after a few rounds, making linear and differential cryptanalysis computationally infeasible. It is key-independent, so it does not weaken against known-plaintext attacks.

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain how the **InvShiftRows** operation recovers the original State. Derive the inverse position formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the State $T$ below (post-ShiftRows output), apply InvShiftRows and verify that the original State is recovered. Also state the difference between ShiftRows and a full column permutation. **(7 marks)**

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \text{87} & \text{F2} & \text{4D} & \text{97} \\ \text{6E} & \text{46} & \text{A5} & \text{EC} \\ \text{46} & \text{52} & \text{9F} & \text{14} \\ \text{0A} & \text{0B} & \text{0C} & \text{0D} \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Inverse definition (2 marks):**

InvShiftRows is the inverse of the ShiftRows transformation used during AES decryption. It performs cyclic **right-shifts** of the last three rows by offsets 1, 2, and 3 respectively (Row 0 remains unchanged).

**Derivation of inverse formula (3 marks):**

In ShiftRows, the forward mapping is:

$$
c' = (c + i) \bmod 4
$$

To invert, we solve for the original column $c$ given the new column $c'$:

$$
c' - i \equiv c \pmod 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = (c' - i) \bmod 4
$$

Equivalently:

$$
s_{i, j}^{\text{original}} = s'_{i, (j - i) \bmod 4}
$$

**Diagrammatic illustration (2 marks):**

For a row $i$, the byte currently at column $j$ originally came from column $(j - i) \bmod 4$:

- Row 0: $c' = c$ (no change)
- Row 1: $c' = (c - 1) \bmod 4$ (right-shift by 1)
- Row 2: $c' = (c - 2) \bmod 4$ (right-shift by 2)
- Row 3: $c' = (c - 3) \bmod 4$ (right-shift by 3)

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify rows of $T$ (1 mark):**

- Row 0: $[\text{87}, \text{F2}, \text{4D}, \text{97}]$
- Row 1: $[\text{6E}, \text{46}, \text{A5}, \text{EC}]$
- Row 2: $[\text{46}, \text{52}, \text{9F}, \text{14}]$
- Row 3: $[\text{0A}, \text{0B}, \text{0C}, \text{0D}]$

**Step 2 — Apply right shifts (3 marks):**

- Row 0 unchanged: $[\text{87}, \text{F2}, \text{4D}, \text{97}]$
- Row 1 right-shifted by 1: positions $(0,1,2,3) \rightarrow (3,0,1,2)$: $[\text{EC}, \text{6E}, \text{46}, \text{A5}]$
- Row 2 right-shifted by 2: positions $(0,1,2,3) \rightarrow (2,3,0,1)$: $[\text{9F}, \text{14}, \text{46}, \text{52}]$
- Row 3 right-shifted by 3: positions $(0,1,2,3) \rightarrow (1,2,3,0)$: $[\text{0B}, \text{0C}, \text{0D}, \text{0A}]$

**Step 3 — Write recovered State (1 mark):**

$$
S_{\text{recovered}} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{87} & \text{F2} & \text{4D} & \text{97} \\ \text{EC} & \text{6E} & \text{46} & \text{A5} \\ \text{9F} & \text{14} & \text{46} & \text{52} \\ \text{0B} & \text{0C} & \text{0D} & \text{0A} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Difference between ShiftRows and column permutation (2 marks):**

| Aspect | ShiftRows | Full Column Permutation |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Operates on each row independently | Rearranges entire columns as a whole |
| **Granularity** | Byte-level (within rows) | Column-level (entire columns) |
| **Effect on MixColumns** | Pre-mixes bytes inside a column from multiple original columns | MixColumns sees bytes from a single relocated column |
| **Diffusion Strength** | Strong (favors AES design) | Weak (defeats column-independence benefit) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write that Row 0 is shifted by 0 *and* apply a shift to it. *Correction:* Row 0 has an explicit shift of **0** — it is *not* skipped due to a special case; the formula $\text{shift}_i = i$ simply yields 0.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *left* shift with *right* shift. The AES standard uses **left** shift for encryption (ShiftRows) and **right** shift for decryption (InvShiftRows). Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Writing the destination formula as $(j - i) \bmod 4$ for **forward** ShiftRows. That is the inverse formula. Forward ShiftRows uses $(j + i) \bmod 4$.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that ShiftRows is *linear* and *key-independent*. Examiners frequently award 1 mark for explicitly stating this property.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Drawing the State matrix with row index on the vertical axis but writing byte addresses as $(i, j)$ where students swap $i$ and $j$. KTU's standard convention is $(row, column)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ShiftRows Transformation**

- ✅ **Definition:** Cyclic left shift of bytes in rows 1, 2, 3 by offsets 1, 2, 3 respectively; row 0 unchanged.
- ✅ **State Size:** Always **4 × 4 bytes** (128 bits).
- ✅ **Position Formula:** $s_{i,j} \to s_{i, (j+i) \bmod 4}$.
- ✅ **Inverse (InvShiftRows):** Right shifts of 1, 2, 3 bytes for rows 1, 2, 3.
- ✅ **Key-Dependence:** **None** — ShiftRows is a fixed permutation.
- ✅ **Cryptographic Role:** Provides **diffusion** (Shannon's principle).
- ✅ **Type of Operation:** **Linear, byte-level permutation** (no arithmetic).
- ✅ **Computational Cost:** **Zero XOR, zero S-box lookups** — just memory shuffles.
- ✅ **Why Not Full Permutation:** Avoids breaking column-wise independence that MixColumns relies on.
- ✅ **Avalanche Contribution:** Within 2–3 rounds, one input bit change influences the entire 128-bit block.
- ✅ **Hardware Support:** Modern CPUs implement ShiftRows via AES-NI instructions in a single cycle.
- ✅ **Inverse is NOT the Same Operation:** Right-shift by $i$ is not equal to left-shift by $i$ (except when $i=0$ or $i=2$).
- ✅ **Memory Tip:** Offsets $0, 1, 2, 3$ = row indices $0, 1, 2, 3$ — they line up directly. **"Row $i$ shifts by $i$."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mix Column Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mix Column Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (FIPS-197 / KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**MixColumns** is the third internal transformation of a single round of the **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** block cipher. Formally, it is a **linear diffusion operator** that operates independently on each of the four columns of the AES 4×4 State matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (FIPS 197, Section 5.1.3):** MixColumns is a linear transformation that operates on the State column-by-column. Each column is treated as a four-term polynomial over **GF(2⁸)** and is multiplied (modulo x⁴ + 1) with a fixed polynomial **a(x) = {03}x³ + {01}x² + {01}x + {02}**.

In matrix notation, for column *c* of the State:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
s'_{0,c} \\
s'_{1,c} \\
s'_{2,c} \\
s'_{3,c}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\
01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\
01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\
03 & 01 & 01 & 02
\end{bmatrix}
\otimes
\begin{bmatrix}
s_{0,c} \\
s_{1,c} \\
s_{2,c} \\
s_{3,c}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

All multiplications (`⊗`) and additions (`⊕`) are performed in the **Galois Field GF(2⁸)** with the irreducible polynomial **m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1** (written in hex as `0x11B`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shuffling Mixer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine four sealed envelopes lying in a column on a desk. Each envelope contains one byte. A "mixer" comes along, opens *all four* envelopes simultaneously, takes a weighted blend of their contents using a strict rulebook, and re-seals the results into the same column. After mixing, **no single output byte comes from just one input byte** — every output byte is a *shared mixture* of all four inputs. This is the heart of **diffusion**: changes in any one input ripple across every output.

| Property | Plain-English Meaning | Cryptographic Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Column-wise** | Operates on 4 bytes at a time | Locality of diffusion |
| **Linear in GF(2⁸)** | XORs and modular shifts only | Easily analysable, but combined with S-Box it becomes non-linear overall |
| **Invertible** | A unique inverse matrix exists | Round decryption is possible (InvMixColumns) |
| **MDS Matrix** | Maximum Distance Separable | Guarantees 5 active S-Boxes after 2 rounds |

### 1.3 Position within the AES Round

MixColumns sits **between** SubBytes (non-linear confusion) and ShiftRows (positional diffusion). It is the *algebraic* engine that connects these two, ensuring that any change to a single State byte is smeared across the entire column after this step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The final round of AES **omits MixColumns** (it uses only SubBytes, ShiftRows, and AddRoundKey). This is a frequently tested MCQ/short-answer point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Three Layers

**Layer 1 — Byte Arithmetic (GF(2⁸))**

Every byte is an 8-bit polynomial over GF(2). Addition is bitwise XOR. Multiplication is polynomial multiplication followed by reduction modulo `m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1`.

The two constants used by MixColumns — `{02}` and `{03}` — have special compact multiplication rules:

| Operation | Rule | Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **× `{02}` (xtime)** | Shift left by 1; if MSB was 1, XOR result with `{1B}` | `b << 1`; if `b & 0x80` then `result ^= 0x1B` |
| **× `{03}`** | `(× 02) ⊕ (× 01)` — i.e., xtime then XOR original | `xtime(b) ⊕ b` |

The value `{1B}` comes from reducing the overflow term `x⁸` using `m(x)`: `x⁸ ≡ x⁴ + x³ + x + 1 = 0x1B`.

**Layer 2 — Column Polynomial (GF(2⁸)[x] / (x⁴ + 1))**

Each column `[s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃]ᵀ` is interpreted as the polynomial:

$$
s(x) = s_3 x^3 + s_2 x^2 + s_1 x + s_0
$$

MixColumns computes:

$$
s'(x) = a(x) \cdot s(x) \pmod{x^4 + 1}
$$

where `a(x) = 03x³ + 01x² + 01x + 02`. The modulus `x⁴ + 1` is chosen so that the transformation is cyclic on the 4-byte column (since `x⁴ ≡ 1`).

**Layer 3 — Why This Matrix?**

The matrix is an **MDS (Maximum Distance Separable) matrix** over GF(2⁸). This means:
- The branch number is **5** — any 2-round differential or linear trail involves ≥ 5 active S-Boxes.
- This provides a provable lower bound on the cipher's resistance to differential and linear cryptanalysis.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| `m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1` | AES irreducible polynomial | Field reduction reference |
| `a(x) = {03}x³ + {01}x² + {01}x + {02}` | Fixed MixColumns polynomial | Direct transformation |
| `xtime(b) = (b << 1) ⊕ (0x1B if b ≥ 0x80 else 0)` | Multiplication by `{02}` | Core sub-routine |
| `{03}·b = xtime(b) ⊕ b` | Multiplication by `{03}` | Core sub-routine |
| `x⁴ + 1` | Column modulus | Polynomial reduction |
| **MDS matrix** (the 4×4 table above) | Diffusion operator | Direct state update |
| **Branch number = 5** | Minimum active S-Boxes per 2 rounds | Cryptanalysis lower bound |
| `InvMixColumns matrix` | Multiplied by `{0B, 0D, 09, 0E}` | Decryption round |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MixColumns (and its MDS-style cousins) is used in every modern block cipher that demands provable diffusion guarantees — AES, SM4, Twofish, and the lightweight ciphers of IoT (ASCON, GIFT). It is the standard "go-to" design for SPN networks.

### 2.3 Why MixColumns is Invertible

Because `gcd(a(x), x⁴ + 1) = 1` in GF(2⁸)[x], the polynomial `a(x)` has a multiplicative inverse, namely `a⁻¹(x) = {0B}x³ + {0D}x² + {09}x + {0E}`. This guarantees **InvMixColumns** exists for decryption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compact Multiplication Rules

**Step 1 — Representation.** Let `b = b₇x⁷ + b₆x⁶ + ... + b₀` be a byte in GF(2⁸). Multiplication by `x` shifts coefficients up by one degree:

$$
x \cdot b = b_7 x^8 + b_6 x^7 + b_5 x^6 + b_4 x^5 + b_3 x^4 + b_2 x^3 + b_1 x^2 + b_0 x
$$

**Step 2 — Reduce the overflow.** The term `b₇ x⁸` is outside GF(2⁸). Using `m(x)`, we replace `x⁸ ≡ x⁴ + x³ + x + 1`:

$$
x \cdot b = b_7(x^4 + x^3 + x + 1) + b_6 x^7 + \ldots + b_0 x
$$

**Step 3 — Factor back into a byte.** Grouping coefficients of `x⁰ ... x⁷` produces an 8-bit value. The constant 0x1B = 00011011 = x⁴ + x³ + x + 1 is exactly what gets XORed when `b₇ = 1`.

Therefore:
$$
\text{xtime}(b) =
\begin{cases}
b \ll 1 & \text{if } b_7 = 0 \\
(b \ll 1) \oplus \mathtt{0x1B} & \text{if } b_7 = 1
\end{cases}
$$

For `× 03`, observe that `03 = 02 ⊕ 01`, so:

$$
\{03\} \cdot b = \{02\} \cdot b \oplus \{01\} \cdot b = \text{xtime}(b) \oplus b
$$

This is the **key shortcut** that makes MixColumns efficient in hardware.

### 3.2 Worked Example — A Complete Column (FIPS-197 Spec Example)

Let us take the **first column of the State** just after the SubBytes step in the first round of AES (taken from the FIPS 197 example, Appendix B):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s_0 \\ s_1 \\ s_2 \\ s_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \mathtt{0x87} \\ \mathtt{0x6E} \\ \mathtt{0x46} \\ \mathtt{0xA6} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Compute the four required products in GF(2⁸):**

**Product A: `{02}·0x87`**
- `0x87 = 1000 0111₂`, MSB = 1.
- Shift left: `0x10E`, drop the overflow 1: `0x0E`.
- XOR with `0x1B`: `0x0E ⊕ 0x1B = 0x15`.
- Result: **`0x15`**.

**Product B: `{03}·0x6E`**
- First, `0x6E = 0110 1110₂`, MSB = 0.
- Shift left: `0xDC` (no reduction needed).
- Then XOR with original: `0xDC ⊕ 0x6E = 0xB2`.
- Result: **`0xB2`**.

**Product C: `{02}·0x46`**
- `0x46 = 0100 0110₂`, MSB = 0.
- Shift left: `0x8C` (no reduction).
- Result: **`0x8C`**.

**Product D: `{02}·0xA6`**
- `0xA6 = 1010 0110₂`, MSB = 1.
- Shift left: `0x14C`, drop the overflow 1: `0x4C`.
- XOR with `0x1B`: `0x4C ⊕ 0x1B = 0x57`.
- Result: **`0x57`**.

**Product E: `{03}·0x46`**
- `0x8C ⊕ 0x46 = 0xCA`.
- Result: **`0xCA`**.

**Product F: `{03}·0xA6`**
- `0x57 ⊕ 0xA6 = 0xF1`.
- Result: **`0xF1`**.

**Product G: `{03}·0x87`**
- `0x15 ⊕ 0x87 = 0x92`.
- Result: **`0x92`**.

**Now assemble each output byte using the matrix rows:**

$$
s'_0 = \{02\}\cdot 0x87 \oplus \{03\}\cdot 0x6E \oplus \{01\}\cdot 0x46 \oplus \{01\}\cdot 0xA6
$$

$$
s'_0 = \mathtt{0x15} \oplus \mathtt{0xB2} \oplus \mathtt{0x46} \oplus \mathtt{0xA6}
$$

Computing bitwise:
- `0x15 ⊕ 0xB2 = 0xA7`
- `0xA7 ⊕ 0x46 = 0xE1`
- `0xE1 ⊕ 0xA6 = 0x47`

$$
\boxed{s'_0 = \mathtt{0x47}}
$$

$$
s'_1 = \{01\}\cdot 0x87 \oplus \{02\}\cdot 0x6E \oplus \{03\}\cdot 0x46 \oplus \{01\}\cdot 0xA6
$$

$$
s'_1 = \mathtt{0x87} \oplus \mathtt{0xDC} \oplus \mathtt{0xCA} \oplus \mathtt{0xA6}
$$

- `0x87 ⊕ 0xDC = 0x5B`
- `0x5B ⊕ 0xCA = 0x91`
- `0x91 ⊕ 0xA6 = 0x37`

$$
\boxed{s'_1 = \mathtt{0x37}}
$$

$$
s'_2 = \{01\}\cdot 0x87 \oplus \{01\}\cdot 0x6E \oplus \{02\}\cdot 0x46 \oplus \{03\}\cdot 0xA6
$$

$$
s'_2 = \mathtt{0x87} \oplus \mathtt{0x6E} \oplus \mathtt{0x8C} \oplus \mathtt{0xF1}
$$

- `0x87 ⊕ 0x6E = 0xE9`
- `0xE9 ⊕ 0x8C = 0x65`
- `0x65 ⊕ 0xF1 = 0x94`

$$
\boxed{s'_2 = \mathtt{0x94}}
$$

$$
s'_3 = \{03\}\cdot 0x87 \oplus \{01\}\cdot 0x6E \oplus \{01\}\cdot 0x46 \oplus \{02\}\cdot 0xA6
$$

$$
s'_3 = \mathtt{0x92} \oplus \mathtt{0x6E} \oplus \mathtt{0x46} \oplus \mathtt{0x57}
$$

- `0x92 ⊕ 0x6E = 0xFC`
- `0xFC ⊕ 0x46 = 0xBA`
- `0xBA ⊕ 0x57 = 0xED`

$$
\boxed{s'_3 = \mathtt{0xED}}
$$

**Final transformed column:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s'_0 \\ s'_1 \\ s'_2 \\ s'_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \mathtt{0x47} \\ \mathtt{0x37} \\ \mathtt{0x94} \\ \mathtt{0xED} \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matches the FIPS-197 reference output (Appendix B, after the MixColumns step of round 1).

### 3.3 Verification — Why Each Output Depends on All Four Inputs

Notice that the bit `0x47` in `s'₀` was constructed by XORing contributions from **all four** input bytes (`0x87`, `0x6E`, `0x46`, `0xA6`). Flipping *any single bit* of any of the four inputs will, with overwhelming probability, flip *multiple* bits in all four outputs. This is **diffusion** in action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Where MixColumns Lives in AES

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[128-bit Plaintext Block] --> B[AddRoundKey 0]
    B --> C{Round i = 1 to Nr-1}
    C --> D[SubBytes]
    D --> E[ShiftRows]
    E --> F["MixColumns"]
    F --> G[AddRoundKey i]
    G --> H{i less than Nr-1}
    H -->|Yes| C
    H -->|No| I[SubBytes]
    I --> J[ShiftRows]
    J --> K[AddRoundKey Nr]
    K --> L[128-bit Ciphertext Block]
    F -.->|Note: Final round omits this step| J
```

### 4.2 Internal Dataflow of MixColumns (One Column)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Column s_c"]
        s0["s0"]
        s1["s1"]
        s2["s2"]
        s3["s3"]
    end

    subgraph X2["xtime Module x02"]
        x20["02 s0"]
        x21["02 s1"]
        x22["02 s2"]
        x23["02 s3"]
    end

    subgraph X3["x03 = x02 XOR x01"]
        x30["03 s1"]
        x31["03 s2"]
        x32["03 s3"]
        x33["03 s0"]
    end

    subgraph XOR["4-way XOR Combiner"]
        xor0["XOR all four"]
        xor1["XOR all four"]
        xor2["XOR all four"]
        xor3["XOR all four"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Column s prime c"]
        out0["s0 prime"]
        out1["s1 prime"]
        out2["s2 prime"]
        out3["s3 prime"]
    end

    s0 --> x20
    s1 --> x21
    s2 --> x22
    s3 --> x23

    s1 --> x30
    s2 --> x31
    s3 --> x32
    s0 --> x33

    x20 --> xor0
    x30 --> xor0
    s2 --> xor0
    s3 --> xor0
    xor0 --> out0

    s0 --> xor1
    x21 --> xor1
    x31 --> xor1
    s3 --> xor1
    xor1 --> out1

    s0 --> xor2
    s1 --> xor2
    x22 --> xor2
    x32 --> xor2
    xor2 --> out2

    x33 --> xor3
    s1 --> xor3
    s2 --> xor3
    x23 --> xor3
    xor3 --> out3
```

### 4.3 State View — Column-wise Processing of the 4×4 AES State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATE["AES State Matrix 4x4"]
        direction LR
        C0["Column 0"] --> C1["Column 1"] --> C2["Column 2"] --> C3["Column 3"]
    end

    STATE -->|Parallel| MC["MixColumns Engine"]
    MC -->|Parallel| NEW["Transformed State"]

    subgraph DETAIL["Inside MC Engine"]
        direction TB
        D0["Col 0 transformed in parallel"]
        D1["Col 1 transformed in parallel"]
        D2["Col 2 transformed in parallel"]
        D3["Col 3 transformed in parallel"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> All four columns are processed **independently and in parallel** — a key reason AES is fast in both hardware and software. There is **no cross-column data flow** inside MixColumns; cross-byte mixing happens only at the *AES round level* when combined with ShiftRows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**  
> **Q: Define the MixColumns transformation in AES. Why is it called a "linear" transformation?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
MixColumns is the third operation in each AES round (except the last). It operates on each column of the State matrix by treating it as a four-term polynomial `s(x) = s₃x³ + s₂x² + s₁x + s₀` over GF(2⁸) and multiplying it (mod `x⁴ + 1`) by the fixed polynomial `a(x) = {03}x³ + {01}x² + {01}x + {02}`. Equivalently, it is matrix multiplication by a 4×4 MDS matrix over GF(2⁸). It is "linear" because every output byte is a linear combination (XOR of scaled inputs) of the input bytes — no non-linear S-Box lookup is involved. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**  
> **Q: List the irreducible polynomial of AES and the modulus used in MixColumns. What is `xtime` and why is `0x1B` used?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Irreducible polynomial:** `m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1` (i.e., `0x11B`).
- **Column modulus:** `x⁴ + 1` (so that the polynomial multiplication is cyclic over 4 bytes).
- **xtime(b):** Multiplication of byte `b` by `{02}` in GF(2⁸). Algorithm: shift `b` left by 1 bit; if the original MSB was 1, XOR the result with `0x1B`.
- **Why `0x1B`:** When the MSB is 1, the shifted value contains an `x⁸` term which must be reduced using `m(x)`. The reduction yields `x⁸ ≡ x⁴ + x³ + x + 1 = 0x1B`, which is XORed in. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Style ESE]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the MixColumns transformation of AES. Show the matrix used, the field of computation, and explain why it provides diffusion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the input column `[0xC2, 0x96, 0x47, 0x91]ᵀ`, compute the output of MixColumns step-by-step. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

MixColumns is a **linear diffusion layer** in AES. Each of the four columns of the State is processed independently.

**Field:** All arithmetic is in the **Galois Field GF(2⁸)** with irreducible polynomial `m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1`.

**Matrix representation:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
s'_0 \\ s'_1 \\ s'_2 \\ s'_3
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\
01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\
01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\
03 & 01 & 01 & 02
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
s_0 \\ s_1 \\ s_2 \\ s_3
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Polynomial view:** `s'(x) = a(x)·s(x) mod (x⁴+1)` where `a(x) = 03x³ + 01x² + 01x + 02`.

**Why diffusion:** Every output byte is a *linear combination* (XORs and GF(2⁸) products) of **all four** input bytes in that column. Hence, flipping one bit in any input byte will flip, on average, half the bits in each of the four output bytes. The matrix is also **MDS (Maximum Distance Separable)** with branch number 5, providing a provable guarantee that after two AES rounds, ≥ 5 S-Boxes are active — strengthening resistance to differential/linear cryptanalysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Input column: `[s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃] = [0xC2, 0x96, 0x47, 0x91]`.

**Step 1 — Compute the GF(2⁸) products.**

- `02 · 0xC2`: `0xC2 = 1100 0010₂`, MSB = 1. Shift = `0x184`, reduce: `0x84 ⊕ 0x1B = 0x9F`.
- `02 · 0x96`: `0x96 = 1001 0110₂`, MSB = 1. Shift = `0x12C`, reduce: `0x2C ⊕ 0x1B = 0x37`.
- `02 · 0x47`: `0x47 = 0100 0111₂`, MSB = 0. Shift = `0x8E`.
- `02 · 0x91`: `0x91 = 1001 0001₂`, MSB = 1. Shift = `0x122`, reduce: `0x22 ⊕ 0x1B = 0x39`.

- `03 · 0xC2 = 0x9F ⊕ 0xC2 = 0x5D`.
- `03 · 0x96 = 0x37 ⊕ 0x96 = 0xA1`.
- `03 · 0x47 = 0x8E ⊕ 0x47 = 0xC9`.
- `03 · 0x91 = 0x39 ⊕ 0x91 = 0xA8`.

**Step 2 — Apply the matrix rows.**

$$
s'_0 = 0x9F \oplus 0xA1 \oplus 0x47 \oplus 0x91
$$

- `0x9F ⊕ 0xA1 = 0x3E`
- `0x3E ⊕ 0x47 = 0x79`
- `0x79 ⊕ 0x91 = 0xE8` → **s'₀ = 0xE8**

$$
s'_1 = 0xC2 \oplus 0x37 \oplus 0xC9 \oplus 0x91
$$

- `0xC2 ⊕ 0x37 = 0xF5`
- `0xF5 ⊕ 0xC9 = 0x3C`
- `0x3C ⊕ 0x91 = 0xAD` → **s'₁ = 0xAD**

$$
s'_2 = 0xC2 \oplus 0x96 \oplus 0x8E \oplus 0xA8
$$

- `0xC2 ⊕ 0x96 = 0x54`
- `0x54 ⊕ 0x8E = 0xDA`
- `0xDA ⊕ 0xA8 = 0x72` → **s'₂ = 0x72**

$$
s'_3 = 0x5D \oplus 0x96 \oplus 0x47 \oplus 0x39
$$

- `0x5D ⊕ 0x96 = 0xCB`
- `0xCB ⊕ 0x47 = 0x8C`
- `0x8C ⊕ 0x39 = 0xB5` → **s'₃ = 0xB5**

**Final output column:** `[0xE8, 0xAD, 0x72, 0xB5]ᵀ` **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of the polynomial `a(x) = 03x³ + 01x² + 01x + 02` in MixColumns. How is invertibility guaranteed? Write the InvMixColumns matrix. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Perform the MixColumns transformation on the column `[0xD4, 0xBF, 0x5D, 0x30]ᵀ`. Show every xtime operation and XOR. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The polynomial `a(x) = 03x³ + 01x² + 01x + 02` is the **fixed multiplier** in the MixColumns operation. Each State column is interpreted as a polynomial `s(x)` of degree ≤ 3, and the output column is `a(x)·s(x) mod (x⁴+1)`. The coefficients `{02, 03, 01, 01}` of `a(x)` correspond to the entries of the MDS diffusion matrix, ensuring the **branch number is 5**.

**Invertibility:** In GF(2⁸)[x], `a(x)` is invertible if and only if `gcd(a(x), x⁴+1) = 1`. Since `x⁴+1` factors as `(x+1)⁴` over GF(2) and `a(1) = 03+01+01+02 = 03 ≠ 0` in GF(2⁸), they are coprime. The inverse polynomial is computed via the extended Euclidean algorithm, giving:

$$
a^{-1}(x) = \mathtt{0B}\,x^3 + \mathtt{0D}\,x^2 + \mathtt{09}\,x + \mathtt{0E}
$$

**InvMixColumns matrix:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0E & 0B & 0D & 09 \\
09 & 0E & 0B & 0D \\
0D & 09 & 0E & 0B \\
0B & 0D & 09 & 0E
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix is used in the decryption rounds (with the inverse ShiftRows order and inverse S-Box) to reverse MixColumns. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Input: `[0xD4, 0xBF, 0x5D, 0x30]ᵀ`.

**Compute GF(2⁸) products:**

- `02 · 0xD4`: `0xD4 = 1101 0100₂`, MSB = 1. Shift = `0x1A8`, reduce: `0xA8 ⊕ 0x1B = 0xB3`.
- `02 · 0xBF`: `0xBF = 1011 1111₂`, MSB = 1. Shift = `0x17E`, reduce: `0x7E ⊕ 0x1B = 0x65`.
- `02 · 0x5D`: `0x5D = 0101 1101₂`, MSB = 0. Shift = `0xBA`.
- `02 · 0x30`: `0x30 = 0011 0000₂`, MSB = 0. Shift = `0x60`.

- `03 · 0xD4 = 0xB3 ⊕ 0xD4 = 0x67`.
- `03 · 0xBF = 0x65 ⊕ 0xBF = 0xDA`.
- `03 · 0x5D = 0xBA ⊕ 0x5D = 0xE7`.
- `03 · 0x30 = 0x60 ⊕ 0x30 = 0x50`.

**Apply matrix rows:**

$$
s'_0 = 0xB3 \oplus 0xDA \oplus 0x5D \oplus 0x30
$$

- `0xB3 ⊕ 0xDA = 0x69`
- `0x69 ⊕ 0x5D = 0x34`
- `0x34 ⊕ 0x30 = 0x04` → **s'₀ = 0x04**

$$
s'_1 = 0xD4 \oplus 0x65 \oplus 0xE7 \oplus 0x30
$$

- `0xD4 ⊕ 0x65 = 0xB1`
- `0xB1 ⊕ 0xE7 = 0x56`
- `0x56 ⊕ 0x30 = 0x66` → **s'₁ = 0x66**

$$
s'_2 = 0xD4 \oplus 0xBF \oplus 0xBA \oplus 0x50
$$

- `0xD4 ⊕ 0xBF = 0x6B`
- `0x6B ⊕ 0xBA = 0xD1`
- `0xD1 ⊕ 0x50 = 0x81` → **s'₂ = 0x81**

$$
s'_3 = 0x67 \oplus 0xBF \oplus 0x5D \oplus 0x60
$$

- `0x67 ⊕ 0xBF = 0xD8`
- `0xD8 ⊕ 0x5D = 0x85`
- `0x85 ⊕ 0x60 = 0xE5` → **s'₃ = 0xE5`

**Final output column:** `[0x04, 0x66, 0x81, 0xE5]ᵀ` **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing field:** Marks are lost for writing "mod 256" or using normal integer arithmetic. Always explicitly state **GF(2⁸)** with the polynomial `x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1`.
> 2. **Forgetting the reduction:** When shifting a byte whose MSB is 1, students often omit the XOR with `0x1B`. The xtime function is *defined* to include this reduction; skipping it is a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 3. **Mixing up row/column ordering:** The State column must be read `[s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃]` top-to-bottom (row 0 first). Many textbooks present it as `[s₀..s₃]` left-to-right — both are valid if the matrix is drawn consistently. Stay consistent!
> 4. **Final round:** Forgetting that the **last round omits MixColumns** is a very common short-answer blunder — KTU examiners love testing this.
> 5. **Polynomial vs. matrix form:** When asked to "show the polynomial a(x)," don't just draw the matrix. Both forms are required for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MixColumns** is the **linear diffusion step** of AES, sitting between SubBytes and AddRoundKey.
- ✅ It operates **column-by-column** on the 4×4 State, treating each column as a 4-term polynomial over **GF(2⁸)**.
- ✅ The fixed multiplier is **`a(x) = 03x³ + 01x² + 01x + 02`**, reduced modulo **`x⁴ + 1`**.
- ✅ The matrix is a **4×4 MDS matrix** with entries from `{01, 02, 03}` — only three distinct constants are needed.
- ✅ Multiplication by `{02}` is the **xtime** operation: shift-left and conditionally XOR with `0x1B`.
- ✅ Multiplication by `{03}` is computed as `xtime(b) ⊕ b` (since `03 = 02 ⊕ 01`).
- ✅ The field's **irreducible polynomial** is `m(x) = x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1` (`0x11B`).
- ✅ The inverse polynomial is `a⁻¹(x) = 0Bx³ + 0Dx² + 09x + 0E` — used in **InvMixColumns**.
- ✅ The **final round of AES omits MixColumns** — only SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey.
- ✅ The matrix has **branch number 5**, giving a provable lower bound of 5 active S-Boxes per 2 rounds — a cornerstone of AES's security proof against differential and linear cryptanalysis.
- ✅ All four columns of the State are processed **independently and in parallel**, making MixColumns highly amenable to hardware pipelining and SIMD acceleration.
- ✅ The constant `0x1B` arises directly from reducing the `x⁸` overflow using `m(x)`.
- ✅ Output of MixColumns feeds into **AddRoundKey** (XOR with the round key) to complete the round.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Cryptography: RSA and Key Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Public Key Cryptography: RSA and Key Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Public Key Cryptography — Formal Definition

**Public Key Cryptography (PKC)**, also called **Asymmetric Cryptography**, is a cryptographic paradigm in which a pair of mathematically related but non-identical keys — a **Public Key** $K_{pub}$ (freely distributed) and a **Private Key** $K_{priv}$ (held secret by the owner) — is used to perform encryption, decryption, and digital signature operations. The fundamental security premise rests on the **computational infeasibility** of deriving $K_{priv}$ from $K_{pub}$, even though both keys are algorithmically linked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT756 — Module 3):** Public Key Cryptography introduces RSA, the Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange, and the broader Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) framework governing key generation, distribution, certification, and revocation.

| Property | Symmetric Key Cryptography | Public Key Cryptography |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Keys | **1 shared secret key** | **2 keys per entity (1 public + 1 private)** |
| Key Distribution | Hard problem | Solved via public directory |
| Speed | **Fast (bulk data)** | **Slow (~1000× slower)** |
| Key Count for $n$ users | $n(n-1)/2$ keys | $2n$ keys |
| Primary Use | Confidentiality | Confidentiality + Authenticity + Non-repudiation |
| Example Algorithms | AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | **RSA**, ECC, ElGamal, DSA |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mailbox" Model

Imagine a city with thousands of **transparent mailboxes** locked in a public square. Anyone can *drop* a letter into any mailbox (encryption with the public key), but **only the owner with the unique private key** can open the lid and read it (decryption). The reverse also works: a person can sign a letter with a unique private stamp (digital signature) and everyone can verify the stamp's authenticity using the public key published in a directory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two keys instead of one?** A single shared key requires an impossibly secure pre-established channel between every pair of users. Public key cryptography eliminates this bottleneck by allowing strangers to communicate securely **without prior secret exchange** — a revolutionary concept formalized by **Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in 1976** ("New Directions in Cryptography").

---

### 1.3 RSA — The Cornerstone Algorithm

**RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977)** is the most widely deployed public key cryptosystem. Its security relies on the **practical intractability of factoring the product of two large prime numbers** — a problem known as the **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core RSA Premise:** Multiplying two large primes is trivial, but recovering those primes from their product is computationally infeasible for numbers beyond **2048 bits** with classical computers.

### 1.4 Key Management — Definition

**Key Management** refers to the entire lifecycle of cryptographic keys: **generation, distribution, storage, use, rotation, archival, and destruction**. For public key systems, it is realized through a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** comprising Certification Authorities (CAs), Registration Authorities (RAs), digital certificates (X.509), Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs), and Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) responders.

---

### 1.5 KTU Course Outcome Mapping

| CO Code | Course Outcome Description | Topic Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** | Apply public key cryptographic algorithms to solve security problems | RSA, Diffie–Hellman, ECC |
| **CO4** | Analyze key management and trust models in distributed systems | PKI, X.509, Key Distribution |
| **CO5** | Evaluate cryptographic protocols for confidentiality, integrity, and non-repudiation | Digital signatures, certificates |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RSA — Underlying Mathematical Foundations

RSA's correctness depends on three pillars of **Number Theory**:

1. **Modular Arithmetic:** Operations performed within a finite integer ring $\mathbb{Z}_n$.
2. **Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$:** Counts positive integers $\leq n$ that are coprime to $n$.
3. **Euler's Theorem:** $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ when $\gcd(a,n)=1$.

---

### 2.2 RSA Algorithm — Three Operational Phases

#### **Phase 1: Key Generation (by Receiver Bob)**

**Step 1 — Prime Selection:**
Choose two large, distinct primes $p$ and $q$ of approximately equal bit-length (e.g., **1024 bits each** for a 2048-bit modulus). The security of RSA scales with the size of $n$.

**Step 2 — Modulus Computation:**
Compute the public modulus $n = p \cdot q$.

**Step 3 — Euler's Totient:**
Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ since $p$ and $q$ are prime.

**Step 4 — Public Exponent Selection:**
Choose a public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Standard choice: $e = 65537 = 2^{16}+1$ (Fermat prime $F_4$).

**Step 5 — Private Exponent Derivation:**
Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$, satisfying:
$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pairs Published:**
> - **Public Key:** $\{e, n\}$ — distributed openly.
> - **Private Key:** $\{d, n\}$ — kept secret by the owner.

#### **Phase 2: Encryption (by Sender Alice)**

Alice converts plaintext message $M$ (an integer with $0 \le M < n$) to ciphertext $C$:
$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

#### **Phase 3: Decryption (by Receiver Bob)**

Bob recovers plaintext from ciphertext:
$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

**Proof of Correctness (Euler's Theorem application):**
$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{ed} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RSA Public Modulus** | $n = p \cdot q$ | Bit-length defines security level |
| 2 | **Euler's Totient** | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Required for key generation |
| 3 | **Encryption** | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Public key operation |
| 4 | **Decryption** | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Private key operation |
| 5 | **Key Pair Invariant** | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Foundation of RSA |
| 6 | **Euler's Theorem** | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | Proves decryption correctness |
| 7 | **Digital Signature** | $S \equiv M^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Sign with private key |
| 8 | **Signature Verification** | $M \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Verify with public key |
| 9 | **Fermat's Little Theorem** (prime $p$) | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Special case when $\phi(p) = p-1$ |
| 10 | **Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret** | $K = (g^{b})^{a} \equiv (g^{a})^{b} \pmod{p}$ | Key agreement without exchange |
| 11 | **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ via $d_p, d_q$ | Speeds up decryption **~4×** |
| 12 | **RSA Key Size Recommendation** | $\geq 2048$ bits (NIST 2024) | Protects against GNFS attacks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Real-world RSA implementations use **OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding)** or **PSS (Probabilistic Signature Scheme)** to defeat chosen-ciphertext and signature forgery attacks. Textbook RSA without padding is **semantically insecure**.

---

### 2.4 RSA for Digital Signatures

RSA can be *inverted* in usage: the **private key signs**, and the **public key verifies**. This provides **Authentication**, **Integrity**, and **Non-Repudiation**.

| Operation | Key Used | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sign** | Private $d$ | $S = M^{d} \bmod n$ | Sender proves authorship |
| **Verify** | Public $e$ | $M' = S^{e} \bmod n$ | Receiver checks integrity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hashing in practice:** Signing is applied to a hash $H(M)$ (e.g., SHA-256), not the message directly, because raw RSA cannot sign messages larger than $n$.

---

### 2.5 Key Management — PKI Architecture

**Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** is the framework that binds public keys to real-world identities via trusted third parties. The components are:

1. **Certification Authority (CA):** Issues and signs digital certificates (e.g., VeriSign, Let's Encrypt).
2. **Registration Authority (RA):** Verifies identity before CA issues certificate.
3. **Digital Certificate (X.509 v3):** Binds subject's identity to a public key, signed by CA.
4. **Certificate Revocation List (CRL):** Lists serial numbers of revoked certificates.
5. **OCSP Responder:** Real-time certificate status checker.
6. **Key Repository / Directory:** Public key distribution point (e.g., LDAP).
7. **Key Escrow / Recovery Agent:** Trusted third-party backup of private keys.

#### **X.509 Certificate Standard Structure**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Version | v1, v2, or v3 |
| Serial Number | Unique CA-assigned ID |
| Signature Algorithm | e.g., `sha256WithRSAEncryption` |
| Issuer | Distinguished Name of CA |
| Validity Period | `notBefore` and `notAfter` |
| Subject | Owner's Distinguished Name |
| Subject Public Key Info | Algorithm + public key bit-string |
| Extensions (v3) | Key usage, SAN, CRL distribution points |
| CA Digital Signature | Hash signed with CA's private key |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | RSA / PKI Application |
|---|---|
| **HTTPS / TLS Handshake** | RSA / ECDSA authenticates server via X.509 certificates |
| **Email Security (PGP, S/MIME)** | RSA encrypts session keys + signs messages |
| **Code Signing** | Software publishers sign executables with RSA keys |
| **JWT / OAuth Tokens** | RS256 algorithm uses RSA signatures |
| **Blockchain / Bitcoin** | ECDSA (ECC analog) signs transactions |
| **Smart Cards / ePassports** | ICAO PKI uses RSA-2048 / ECC for BAC/PACE protocols |
| **SSH Authentication** | RSA host keys verify server identity |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — RSA End-to-End

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical 14-Mark Question):** Alice wishes to send the message $M = 7$ to Bob using RSA. Bob has chosen primes $p = 11$ and $q = 13$, and public exponent $e = 7$. Perform full key generation, encryption, and decryption.

---

#### **Step 1 — Compute the Modulus $n$**

$$n = p \cdot q = 11 \times 13 = 143$$

> The modulus $n = 143$ is the public arithmetic universe for all RSA operations.

---

#### **Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$**

Since $p$ and $q$ are prime:
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = (11-1)(13-1) = 10 \times 12 = 120$$

> $\phi(143) = 120$ counts integers in $[1, 143]$ coprime to 143.

---

#### **Step 3 — Verify Public Exponent $e = 7$**

We need $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = \gcd(7, 120) = 1$ ✓

> Since 7 is prime and does not divide 120, $e = 7$ is valid.

---

#### **Step 4 — Derive Private Exponent $d$**

We require:
$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} \implies 7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$$

**Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

$$120 = 7 \times 17 + 1$$
$$7 = 1 \times 7 + 0$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 120 - 7 \times 17$$

Therefore:
$$1 = -17 \times 7 + 1 \times 120 \implies d \equiv -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$$

**Verification:** $7 \times 103 = 721 = 6 \times 120 + 1 = 720 + 1$ ✓

> Hence $d = 103$.

---

#### **Step 5 — Publish Public Key, Retain Private Key**

$$\boxed{K_{pub} = \{e = 7, n = 143\}}$$
$$\boxed{K_{priv} = \{d = 103, n = 143\}}$$

---

#### **Step 6 — Encryption: Alice Computes Ciphertext $C$**

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} \implies C \equiv 7^{7} \pmod{143}$$

**Compute $7^{7}$ stepwise using successive squaring (modular exponentiation):**

$$7^{1} = 7 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^{2} = 49 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^{4} = 49^{2} = 2401 = 16 \times 143 + 113 \implies 7^{4} \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^{7} = 7^{4} \cdot 7^{2} \cdot 7^{1} = 113 \cdot 49 \cdot 7 \pmod{143}$$

Compute:
$$113 \times 49 = 5537 = 38 \times 143 + 123 \implies 113 \times 49 \equiv 123 \pmod{143}$$
$$123 \times 7 = 861 = 6 \times 143 + 3 \implies 123 \times 7 \equiv 3 \pmod{143}$$

> Therefore, $C = 3$.

---

#### **Step 7 — Decryption: Bob Recovers Plaintext $M$**

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \implies M \equiv 3^{103} \pmod{143}$$

**Compute $3^{103} \pmod{143}$ via binary expansion of 103:**

$$103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 2^{6} + 2^{5} + 2^{2} + 2^{1} + 2^{0}$$

Successive squares of 3 modulo 143:

$$3^{1} \equiv 3 \pmod{143}$$
$$3^{2} \equiv 9 \pmod{143}$$
$$3^{4} \equiv 81 \pmod{143}$$
$$3^{8} \equiv 6561 = 45 \times 143 + 126 \implies 3^{8} \equiv 126 \pmod{143}$$
$$3^{16} \equiv 126^{2} = 15876 = 111 \times 143 + 3 \implies 3^{16} \equiv 3 \pmod{143}$$

> Interesting: $3^{16} \equiv 3 \pmod{143}$, indicating the multiplicative order of 3 modulo 143 divides 15.

$$3^{32} \equiv 3^{2} \equiv 9 \pmod{143}$$
$$3^{64} \equiv 3^{4} \equiv 81 \pmod{143}$$

Now reconstruct $3^{103}$:

$$3^{103} = 3^{64} \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^{4} \cdot 3^{2} \cdot 3^{1}$$
$$= 81 \cdot 9 \cdot 81 \cdot 9 \cdot 3 \pmod{143}$$

Stepwise:
$$81 \cdot 9 = 729 = 5 \times 143 + 14 \implies \equiv 14 \pmod{143}$$
$$14 \cdot 81 = 1134 = 7 \times 143 + 133 \implies \equiv 133 \pmod{143}$$
$$133 \cdot 9 = 1197 = 8 \times 143 + 53 \implies \equiv 53 \pmod{143}$$
$$53 \cdot 3 = 159 = 1 \times 143 + 16 \implies \equiv 16 \pmod{143}$$

**Hmm, let me recheck** using the Fermat-Euler shortcut: $3^{120} \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$ (since $\gcd(3,143)=1$). So $3^{103}$ can be computed via $3^{103} = 3^{-17} \pmod{143}$ since $103 \equiv -17 \pmod{120}$.

Compute $3^{17} \pmod{143}$ to find the inverse:

$$3^{16} \equiv 3 \pmod{143} \text{ (shown above)}$$
$$3^{17} = 3^{16} \cdot 3 = 3 \cdot 3 = 9 \pmod{143}$$

So $3^{103} \equiv (3^{17})^{-1} \equiv 9^{-1} \pmod{143}$.

Find $9^{-1} \pmod{143}$: Solve $9x \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$.

$$143 = 15 \times 9 + 8$$
$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1 \implies 1 = 9 - 8 = 9 - (143 - 15 \times 9) = 16 \times 9 - 143$$

So $9^{-1} \equiv 16 \pmod{143}$, i.e., $3^{103} \equiv 16 \pmod{143}$.

> **Wait — that doesn't match $M=7$.** Let me recheck Step 6.

Recheck $7^7 \pmod{143}$:
$$7^2 = 49, \quad 7^3 = 343 = 2 \times 143 + 57, \text{ so } 7^3 \equiv 57$$
$$7^6 = (7^3)^2 = 57^2 = 3249 = 22 \times 143 + 123, \text{ so } 7^6 \equiv 123$$
$$7^7 = 7^6 \cdot 7 = 123 \times 7 = 861 = 6 \times 143 + 3, \text{ so } 7^7 \equiv 3$$

Confirmed: $C = 3$.

Recheck $3^{103} \pmod{143}$ using the **Fermat shortcut** $3^{120} \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$:

$$3^{103} = 3^{120-17} = 3^{120} \cdot 3^{-17} \equiv 3^{-17} \equiv (3^{17})^{-1} \pmod{143}$$

$$3^{17} = 3^{16} \cdot 3 \equiv 3 \cdot 3 = 9 \pmod{143}$$

$$3^{103} \equiv 9^{-1} \equiv 16 \pmod{143}$$

Hmm. But we expected $M=7$. The error is in $3^{16} \equiv 3 \pmod{143}$ — let me re-verify:

$$3^8 \equiv 126 \pmod{143}$$
$$3^{16} = (3^8)^2 = 126^2 = 15876$$
$$15876 / 143 = 111.02...; \quad 111 \times 143 = 15873; \quad 15876 - 15873 = 3$$

So $3^{16} \equiv 3 \pmod{143}$ is correct.

$$3^{17} = 3^{16} \cdot 3 \equiv 3 \cdot 3 = 9 \pmod{143}$$

$$3^{103} = 3^{120-17} \equiv (3^{120}) \cdot (3^{17})^{-1} \equiv 1 \cdot 9^{-1} \equiv 16 \pmod{143}$$

So decryption gives $M = 16$, not 7. This means I made an error somewhere. Let me check by direct computation using CRT.

**Direct verification via Chinese Remainder Theorem:**

Modulo $p = 11$:
$$M \equiv C^{d} \equiv 3^{103} \pmod{11}$$
By Fermat: $3^{10} \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$, so $3^{103} = 3^{10 \cdot 10 + 3} \equiv 3^{3} \equiv 27 \equiv 5 \pmod{11}$

Modulo $q = 13$:
$$M \equiv 3^{103} \pmod{13}$$
$3^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$, so $3^{103} = 3^{8 \cdot 12 + 7} \equiv 3^{7} \pmod{13}$
$$3^2 = 9, \quad 3^4 = 81 = 6 \times 13 + 3 \equiv 3, \quad 3^7 = 3^4 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 3 = 3 \cdot 9 \cdot 3 = 81 \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$$

So $M \equiv 5 \pmod{11}$ and $M \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$.

**Apply CRT:** Find $M$ such that $M = 11k + 5$ and $M \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$.

$$11k + 5 \equiv 3 \pmod{13} \implies 11k \equiv -2 \pmod{13} \implies 11k \equiv 11 \pmod{13}$$
$$\implies k \equiv 1 \pmod{13} \implies k = 1 \implies M = 11(1) + 5 = 16$$

So $M = 16$? But $M$ should be 7! There must be a computational error in encryption.

Let me recompute $7^7 \pmod{143}$ using a different method:
$$7^1 = 7$$
$$7^2 = 49$$
$$7^3 = 7 \cdot 49 = 343; \quad 343 - 2(143) = 343 - 286 = 57 \implies 7^3 \equiv 57 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^4 = 7 \cdot 57 = 399; \quad 399 - 2(143) = 399 - 286 = 113 \implies 7^4 \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^5 = 7 \cdot 113 = 791; \quad 791 - 5(143) = 791 - 715 = 76 \implies 7^5 \equiv 76 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^6 = 7 \cdot 76 = 532; \quad 532 - 3(143) = 532 - 429 = 103 \implies 7^6 \equiv 103 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^7 = 7 \cdot 103 = 721; \quad 721 - 5(143) = 721 - 715 = 6 \implies 7^7 \equiv 6 \pmod{143}$$

I had an arithmetic error before! $7^7 \equiv 6 \pmod{143}$, not 3.

So $C = 6$.

Now re-decrypt: $M \equiv 6^{103} \pmod{143}$.

Modulo 11: $6^{10} \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$, so $6^{103} = 6^{10 \cdot 10 + 3} \equiv 6^3 = 216 \equiv 216 - 19(11) = 216 - 209 = 7 \pmod{11}$

Modulo 13: $6^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$, so $6^{103} = 6^{8 \cdot 12 + 7} \equiv 6^7 \pmod{13}$
$$6^2 = 36 \equiv 10 \pmod{13}$$
$$6^4 = 100 \equiv 9 \pmod{13}$$
$$6^7 = 6^4 \cdot 6^2 \cdot 6 = 9 \cdot 10 \cdot 6 = 540 \equiv 540 - 41(13) = 540 - 533 = 7 \pmod{13}$$

So $M \equiv 7 \pmod{11}$ and $M \equiv 7 \pmod{13}$. By CRT: $M = 7$. ✓

> **Final verified result:** $C = 6$, and decryption recovers $M = 7$.

This example illustrates a **real and common student error** in modular exponentiation — always verify intermediate steps.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — RSA Security Levels vs Key Sizes

| RSA Key Size ($n$ bits) | Equivalent Symmetric Security | Status (NIST 2024) | Recommended Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1024 | ~80 bits | **Deprecated** | Legacy systems only |
| 2048 | ~112 bits | **Acceptable** | TLS, code signing, S/MIME |
| 3072 | ~128 bits | **Recommended** | Long-term sensitive data |
| 4096 | ~150 bits | **High assurance** | Root CA certificates, classified |
| 7680 | ~170 bits | Excessive | Niche high-security use |
| 15360 | ~190 bits | Excessive | Academic/paranoid tier |

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Euler's Totient for $n = pq$

For distinct primes $p, q$:

Since $\phi$ is multiplicative over coprime factors, and for any prime $p$, $\phi(p) = p-1$:

$$\phi(n) = \phi(p \cdot q) = \phi(p) \cdot \phi(q) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

> This formula is **mandatory knowledge** for every RSA problem in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Extended Euclidean Algorithm — Generic Template

To compute $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$, the systematic procedure is:

1. Apply Euclidean algorithm to find $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = g$.
2. Back-substitute to express $g$ as a linear combination: $g = x \cdot e + y \cdot \phi(n)$.
3. Since $g = 1$, we have $1 = x \cdot e + y \cdot \phi(n) \implies x \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
4. Therefore $d = x \bmod \phi(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to reduce $d$ to the range $[1, \phi(n)-1]$ after back-substitution. Always take $d \bmod \phi(n)$ as the final step.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RSA Encryption/Decryption Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER["SENDER (Alice)"]
        M1["Plaintext M (integer)"]
        ENC["Encryption Module: C = M^e mod n"]
    end

    PUB["Public Channel: Transmit C"]

    subgraph RECEIVER["RECEIVER (Bob)"]
        DEC["Decryption Module: M = C^d mod n"]
        M2["Recovered Plaintext M"]
    end

    subgraph KEYS["KEY INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        KP["K_pub = {e, n} - Public"]
        KV["K_priv = {d, n} - Private"]
    end

    M1 --> ENC
    KP --> ENC
    ENC --> PUB
    PUB --> DEC
    KV --> DEC
    DEC --> M2
```

### 4.2 RSA Key Generation Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Choose large primes p, q"] --> B["Compute n = p x q"]
    B --> C["Compute phi(n) = p-1 x q-1"]
    C --> D{"Choose e: gcd(e, phi(n)) = 1"}
    D -->|Invalid| E["Pick another e"]
    E --> D
    D -->|Valid| F["Compute d = e^-1 mod phi(n)"]
    F --> G["Publish K_pub = e, n"]
    F --> H["Secure Store K_priv = d, n"]
    G --> I["End: Keys Ready"]
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 RSA Digital Signature Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIGNER["SIGNER (Alice)"]
        MSG1["Message M"]
        HASH1["Hash: H = SHA256(M)"]
        SIGGEN["Sign Module: S = H^d mod n"]
    end

    TRANS["Transmit (M, S)"]

    subgraph VERIFIER["VERIFIER (Bob)"]
        HASH2["Hash: H_prime = SHA256(M)"]
        SIGVER["Verify Module: H_check = S^e mod n"]
        CMP{"H_check == H_prime?"}
        OK["Signature VALID"]
        NO["Signature INVALID"]
    end

    MSG1 --> HASH1 --> SIGGEN --> TRANS
    TRANS --> HASH2
    TRANS --> SIGVER
    HASH2 --> CMP
    SIGVER --> CMP
    CMP -->|Yes| OK
    CMP -->|No| NO
```

### 4.4 PKI Hierarchical Trust Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Root CA\n(Self-Signed Cert)"]
    INT1["Intermediate CA 1\n(Policy CA)"]
    INT2["Intermediate CA 2\n(Policy CA)"]
    ENT["Enterprise Sub-CA"]
    SRV["Server Certificate\n(SSL/TLS)"]
    CLI["Client Certificate\n(Authentication)"]
    EMAIL["Email Certificate\n(S/MIME)"]

    ROOT --> INT1
    ROOT --> INT2
    INT1 --> ENT
    INT1 --> SRV
    INT2 --> CLI
    INT2 --> EMAIL
    ENT --> SRV
```

### 4.5 RSA Attack Surface — Conceptual Threat Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RSA["RSA Cryptosystem"] --> A1["Factor n = pq\n(GNFS, Shor's Algorithm)"]
    RSA --> A2["Chosen Ciphertext Attack\n(Bleichenbacher 1998)"]
    RSA --> A3["Low-Entropy Padding\n(Textbook RSA)"]
    RSA --> A4["Small e Attack\n(e=3, Coppersmith)"]
    RSA --> A5["Side-Channel: Timing\n(Power Analysis)"]
    RSA --> A6["Common Modulus Attack\n(same n, different e)"]
    RSA --> A7["Broadcast Attack\n(Hastad)"]

    A1 --> D1["Defence: 2048+ bit n"]
    A2 --> D2["Defence: OAEP padding"]
    A3 --> D3["Defence: PKCS#1 v2.2"]
    A4 --> D4["Defence: e=65537 + padding"]
    A5 --> D5["Defence: Constant-time impl"]
    A6 --> D6["Defence: Unique n per user"]
    A7 --> D7["Defence: Unique padding"]
```
