The protocol's security relies on the trust chain: $$ T(\text{DA} \rightarrow \text{ID}_A = PU_a), \quad A \xrightarrow{\text{sec}} \text{DA} \xrightarrow{\text{sec}} B $$ For any secure session: $$ \text{Confidentiality} = E(PU_b, \text{Message}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Only B with } PR_b \text{ can recover } M $$ --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept / Term | Mathematical / Symbolic Form | Description | Trust Requirement | |---|---|---|---| | Participant Identity | $\text{ID}_X$ | Unique name for participant X | Globally unique | | Public Key of X | $PU_x$ | Asymmetric key (public half) | Bound to $\text{ID}_X$ | | Private Key of X | $PR_x$ | Asymmetric key (private half) | Never disclosed | | Directory Entry | $(\text{ID}_X, PU_x, T_{\text{valid}})$ | Tuple stored by DA | Authoritative | | Registration Step | $A \xrightarrow{\text{sec}} DA: \text{ID}_A \Vert PU_a$ | One-time key deposit | Mutual authentication | | Lookup Step | $A \xrightarrow{\text{sec}} DA: \text{Request}(\text{ID}_B) \rightarrow PU_b$ | On-demand retrieval | DA authenticates to A | | Confidentiality Primitive | $C = E(PU_b, M)$ | Encrypt with recipient's public key | $PU_b$ must be authentic | | Signature Primitive | $\sigma = S(PR_a, H(M))$ | Sign with sender's private key | $PU_a$ must be authentic for verification | | Verification | $V(PU_a, \sigma, H(M)) = \text{True}$ | Public verification of signature | Requires authentic $PU_a$ | | Revocation Time | $T_{\text{rev}}$ | Time after which key is invalid | Propagated by DA | > [!NOTE] > The Publicly Available Directory is the **first PKI scheme** that introduces a *Trusted Third Party (TTP)*, which becomes the bedrock for the more advanced **Public-Key Authority** scheme and the **X.509 / Public-Key Certificate** model. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Publicly Available Directory infrastructure is foundational to: - **X.500 / LDAP Servers** — corporate and government identity directories. - **Active Directory (Microsoft)** — enterprise user and certificate lookup. - **DNSSEC** — DNS-based public key distribution for domain validation. - **S/MIME Certificate Directories** — corporate-wide email encryption key lookup. - **PKI Repositories** — centralized stores of X.509 certificates and Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs). The scheme is the conceptual ancestor of every modern Public Key Infrastructure in production today. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Protocol Trace > [!IMPORTANT] > This section demonstrates a *full* symbolic trace of the directory scheme, including every byte/message that flows between the participants and the directory. No shortcuts; every transition is shown explicitly. ### 3.1 Setup of Notation Let: - $A$ = Initiating participant (Alice). - $B$ = Receiving participant (Bob). - $\text{DA}$ = Directory Authority (trusted). - $PU_x$ = Public key of entity $x$. - $PR_x$ = Private key of entity $x$. - $E(K, M)$ = Symmetric or asymmetric encryption of plaintext $M$ with key $K$. - $S(K, M)$ = Digital signature on $M$ with private key $K$. - $H(\cdot)$ = Cryptographic hash function. - $\text{Nonce}_x$ = A fresh random value generated by entity $x$. - $T_s$ = Timestamp. --- ### 3.2 Full Protocol Walkthrough (Six Logical Steps) #### **Step 1 — One-Time Registration of Alice** Alice generates her key pair and submits it to the Directory Authority. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alice generates: } & (PU_a, PR_a) \\[2pt] \text{Alice computes secure tunnel: } & K_{A\text{-DA}} = \text{SecureKeyExchange}(A, \text{DA}) \\[2pt] \text{Alice transmits to DA: } & M_1 = E(K_{A\text{-DA}},\, \text{ID}_A \,\Vert\, PU_a) \\[2pt] \text{DA stores: } & \text{Directory}[\text{ID}_A] \;\leftarrow\; PU_a \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The directory cannot trust anonymous uploads. The secure channel $K_{A\text{-DA}}$ authenticates Alice to the directory. --- #### **Step 2 — One-Time Registration of Bob (Analogous)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bob generates: } & (PU_b, PR_b) \\[2pt] \text{Bob transmits: } & M_2 = E(K_{B\text{-DA}},\, \text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, PU_b) \\[2pt] \text{DA stores: } & \text{Directory}[\text{ID}_B] \;\leftarrow\; PU_b \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Step 3 — Alice Requests Bob's Public Key (Lookup)** When Alice wants to send a confidential message to Bob, she must retrieve $PU_b$ *and* verify that the response genuinely came from DA. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alice generates: } & \text{Nonce}_A \\[2pt] \text{Alice sends to DA: } & \text{Request} = \text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_A \\[2pt] \text{DA retrieves: } & PU_b \leftarrow \text{Directory}[\text{ID}_B] \\[2pt] \text{DA composes reply: } & R = E(K_{\text{DA}-A},\, PU_b \,\Vert\, \text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_A \,\Vert\, T_s) \\[2pt] \text{DA sends: } & R \;\;\text{to Alice} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The nonce prevents replay attacks, and the timestamp $T_s$ guarantees freshness. --- #### **Step 4 — Alice Verifies the Directory's Response** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alice decrypts: } & (PU_b, \text{ID}_B, \text{Nonce}_A, T_s) = D(K_{\text{DA}-A}, R) \\[2pt] \text{Alice checks: } & \text{Nonce}_A \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Nonce}_A^{\text{sent}} \\[2pt] \text{Alice checks: } & T_s \;\text{within acceptable freshness window} \\[2pt] \text{Alice accepts: } & PU_b \;\;\text{as Bob's authentic public key} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** If the nonce matches, Alice is certain the reply is in response to *her* request and not a replay of an old one. --- #### **Step 5 — Alice Sends a Confidential Message to Bob** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alice encrypts: } & C = E(PU_b,\, M) \\[2pt] \text{Alice sends: } & C \;\;\text{to Bob (over insecure channel)} \\[2pt] \text{Bob decrypts: } & M = D(PR_b,\, C) \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Only Bob, who holds $PR_b$, can recover $M$. Confidentiality is preserved. --- #### **Step 6 — Optional: Alice Signs the Message (Authentication)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Alice computes: } & h = H(M) \\[2pt] \text{Alice signs: } & \sigma = S(PR_a,\, h) \\[2pt] \text{Alice transmits: } & (C, \sigma) = (E(PU_b, M),\, S(PR_a, H(M))) \\[2pt] \text{Bob verifies: } & V(PU_a,\, \sigma,\, H(M)) = \text{True} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The signature proves both *integrity* and *origin authentication*, but only if $PU_a$ is authentic — which is precisely what the directory guarantees. --- ### 3.3 Summary of Message Flow | Step | From | To | Content | Channel | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Alice | DA | $\text{ID}_A \,\Vert\, PU_a$ | Secure, Authenticated | Register Alice's key | | 2 | Bob | DA | $\text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, PU_b$ | Secure, Authenticated | Register Bob's key | | 3 | Alice | DA | $\text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_A$ | Secure, Authenticated | Lookup request | | 4 | DA | Alice | $PU_b \,\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_A \,\Vert\, T_s$ | Secure, Authenticated | Authenticated reply | | 5 | Alice | Bob | $E(PU_b, M)$ | Insecure (OK) | Confidential message | | 6 | Alice | Bob | $S(PR_a, H(M))$ | Insecure (OK) | Signed message | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Observation:** The directory is consulted *only* for registration and lookup, not for ongoing communication. This decouples message throughput from directory availability once keys are obtained. --- ### 3.4 Threat Model Analysis | Attack Vector | Vulnerability | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | Forged public key posted in directory | Critical if DA accepts unauthenticated uploads | Require authenticated secure channel for registration | | Replay of old directory reply | Alice uses stale $PU_b$ after Bob rotates | Nonce + timestamp freshness check | | Compromise of directory | Catastrophic — all bindings lost | Strict physical / logical access control | | Man-in-the-middle between Alice and DA | Attacker substitutes key | Mutual authentication + signed DA replies | | Single point of failure | DA offline ⇒ no lookups possible | Redundancy / replication (motivates the *Public-Key Authority* scheme) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architecture Diagram — Publicly Available Directory ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INIT["INITIAL SETUP PHASE"] A1[Alice generates key pair PUa PRa] --> A2[Alice authenticates to DA over secure channel] B1[Bob generates key pair PUb PRb] --> B2[Bob authenticates to DA over secure channel] A2 --> DA1[Directory Authority DA] B2 --> DA1 DA1 --> STORE[(Directory Repository ID to PU mapping)] end subgraph LOOKUP["LOOKUP PHASE"] A3[Alice needs Bobs public key] --> A4[Alice sends request IDb NonceA to DA over secure channel] A4 --> DA2[DA looks up Directory IDb] STORE --> DA2 DA2 --> A5[DA replies with PUb IDb NonceA Timestamp] A5 --> A6[Alice verifies nonce and timestamp] A6 --> A7[Alice accepts authentic PUb] end subgraph COMMS["SECURE COMMUNICATION PHASE"] A7 --> A8[Alice encrypts message with PUb] A8 --> A9[Alice sends ciphertext to Bob] A9 --> B3[Bob decrypts using private key PRb] A8 --> A10[Alice signs message with PRa] A10 --> B3 B3 --> B4[Bob verifies signature using PUa from directory] end classDef entity fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef directory fill:#b45f06,stroke:#7a3d00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef data fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5221,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class A1,A3,A8,B1,B3 entity class DA1,DA2,A2,A4,A5,A6,B2,B4 directory class STORE,A7,A9,A10 data ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagram:** > - **Blue nodes** = Participants (Alice, Bob). > - **Orange nodes** = Directory Authority operations. > - **Green nodes** = Stored data and final message flows. > - Notice the **three phases** (Init → Lookup → Comms) and the **two different channel types** (secure authenticated vs. insecure public). --- ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Directory Internals ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CLIENT["PARTICIPANT SIDE"] IN1[Key Generator Module] IN2[Authentication Module] IN3[Lookup Request Module] IN4[Encryption Sign Module] end subgraph DA["DIRECTORY AUTHORITY DA"] CHK[Authentication Verifier] REG[Registration Handler] LK[Lookup Handler] DB[(Directory Database
ID to PU mapping)] CHK --> REG CHK --> LK REG --> DB LK --> DB end IN1 --> IN2 IN2 -->|Secure channel registration| CHK IN3 -->|Secure channel lookup| CHK DB -->|Authenticated reply| IN3 IN4 -->|Encrypt and sign with retrieved key| BYPASS[Secure Message Channel to other participants] classDef user fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e5f8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef auth fill:#c00000,stroke:#800000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef storage fill:#7030a0,stroke:#4a1f6e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class IN1,IN2,IN3,IN4 user class CHK,REG,LK auth class DB storage ``` --- ### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Position in the Public-Key Distribution Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB L0[Level 0: No distribution scheme] --> L1[Level 1: Public Announcement weakest] L1 --> L2[Level 2: Publicly Available Directory] L2 --> L3[Level 3: Public Key Authority] L3 --> L4[Level 4: Public Key Certificates strongest] L1 -.->|Issue: forgery| L1 L2 -.->|Issue: single point of failure| L2 L3 -.->|Issue: bottleneck| L3 classDef levelBox fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#2e5f8a,color:#000000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef issueBox fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000,stroke-width:1.5px class L0,L1,L2,L3,L4 levelBox class L1,L2,L3 issueBox ``` > [!NOTE] > Each successive level addresses the **specific weakness** of the previous one. The Publicly Available Directory solves the *forgery* problem of Public Announcement, but introduces a *single point of failure* — which the Public-Key Authority scheme later addresses. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define a Publicly Available Directory. List any two advantages it offers over the simple Public Announcement scheme.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A *Publicly Available Directory* is a centralized, trusted repository maintained by a Directory Authority (DA) that stores the public keys of all registered participants. Each participant registers their public key once with the directory, and any other participant can retrieve a verified public key on demand via a secure authenticated channel. > > **Advantages over Public Announcement:** > 1. **Authenticity of keys** — keys are not posted in public; they are stored and served by a trusted authority, drastically reducing forgery. > 2. **Centralized control** — the DA can revoke, rotate, or audit keys, which is impossible in pure public announcement. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *State any three limitations of the Publicly Available Directory scheme.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. The Directory Authority is a **single point of failure** — if it crashes, no participant can obtain a verified public key. 2. The DA must be **online continuously**, exposing it to network-based attacks. 3. The DA can become a **performance bottleneck** when the user base scales, because every secure session requires a directory lookup. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently **omit the secure authenticated channel** between participants and the DA. Lose up to **2 marks** if this is missing. > - Students often **draw the diagram but skip explaining the message format** (e.g., nonce, timestamp). Lose up to **2 marks**. > - When comparing schemes, students list bullet points but fail to state **which weakness of the previous scheme is solved**. Lose up to **1.5 marks**. > - Avoid writing code in Part B — KTU valuation penalizes code for theory papers. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a **Publicly Available Directory** for distributing public keys. (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss **any three limitations** of the Publicly Available Directory and explain how the **Public-Key Authority** scheme overcomes them. (7 Marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Operation of Publicly Available Directory (7 Marks):** The Publicly Available Directory scheme works in two stages — **Registration** and **Lookup**. **Stage 1 — Registration (one-time per participant):** Each participant generates an asymmetric key pair $(PU_x, PR_x)$ and contacts the Directory Authority (DA) over a **secure authenticated channel**. The participant submits the tuple $(\text{ID}_x, PU_x)$. The DA stores this binding in its repository. For example, Alice transmits: $$ E(K_{A\text{-DA}},\; \text{ID}_A \,\Vert\, PU_a) $$ The DA decrypts using the session key shared with Alice, verifies Alice's identity, and stores the entry $\text{Directory}[\text{ID}_A] = PU_a$. The same procedure is repeated by Bob. **Stage 2 — Lookup (per secure communication):** When Alice wants to send a confidential message to Bob, she sends a request to the DA of the form: $$ \text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_A $$ The DA retrieves $PU_b$ from its repository and replies with: $$ E(K_{\text{DA}-A},\; PU_b \,\Vert\, \text{ID}_B \,\Vert\, \text{Nonce}_A \,\Vert\, T_s) $$ Alice decrypts, verifies the nonce, checks the timestamp for freshness, and accepts $PU_b$ as Bob's authentic public key. She then uses $PU_b$ to encrypt her message $M$: $$ C = E(PU_b, M) $$ Bob decrypts using $PR_b$ to recover $M$. **Diagram (Block Flow):** ``` Alice --(register over secure channel)--> DA Bob --(register over secure channel)--> DA Alice --(request ID_B, nonce)----------> DA DA --(PU_B, ID_B, nonce, timestamp)-> Alice Alice --(E(PU_B, M))-------------------> Bob Bob --(D(PR_B, C) = M)---------------> Bob ``` > *(Award 1 mark for each of: definition, registration, lookup, message format, security property, diagram, summary — total 7.)* --- **(b) Three Limitations and How Public-Key Authority Solves Them (7 Marks):** **Limitation 1 — Single Point of Failure (2 marks):** If the Directory Authority is compromised or goes offline, no participant can retrieve verified public keys. All secure communications halt. → **Public-Key Authority fix:** The authority is still trusted, but it issues short-lived *tickets* containing the public key. Once a ticket is obtained, the participant can use it for a window of time even if the authority temporarily goes offline. The authority does not need to be contacted per session — only per ticket issuance. **Limitation 2 — Performance Bottleneck (2.5 marks):** The DA must be queried for every new secure session, making it a scalability choke-point. → **Public-Key Authority fix:** Because tickets are cached and valid for a time period, the per-session load on the authority drops dramatically. Sessions are established *between peers* using tickets, not by querying the authority. **Limitation 3 — Trust Concentration / Single Target for Attack (2.5 marks):** Because every public key flows through one entity, compromising the DA compromises the entire system. → **Public-Key Authority fix:** The authority is engaged only at session establishment to vouch for the binding; cryptographic tickets replace repeated DA involvement. Compromise of one session does not retroactively compromise all historical bindings. Additionally, the use of *timestamped, signed tickets* limits the attacker's window of opportunity. > *(Award 1 mark for naming each limitation, 1 mark for explaining the impact, 1 mark for the fix — totaling 7.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Compare the four main schemes of public-key distribution: **Public Announcement**, **Publicly Available Directory**, **Public-Key Authority**, and **Public-Key Certificates**. (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe the **registration** and **lookup** phases of the Publicly Available Directory in detail, with the help of a **neat diagram**. (7 Marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison of Public-Key Distribution Schemes (7 Marks):** | Criterion | Public Announcement | Publicly Available Directory | Public-Key Authority | Public-Key Certificates | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Trusted Third Party** | None | Yes (Directory) | Yes (Authority) | Yes (CA) | | **Forgery Resistance** | None | High | High | High | | **Online TTP Required** | No | Yes (per lookup) | Yes (per session) | No (offline verification) | | **Single Point of Failure** | No | Yes | Reduced (tickets) | No | | **Scalability** | High | Low | Medium | High | | **Revocation** | Impossible | Possible (central) | Possible (tickets expire) | Possible (CRL / OCSP) | | **Channel to TTP** | Not needed | Secure authenticated | Secure authenticated | Not needed at use-time | | **Operational Example** | Posting on a BBS | LDAP / X.500 | Kerberos-style KDC | X.509 / PKI | | **Key Weakness** | Anyone can post fake keys | DA bottleneck & failure | Ticket forgery risk | CA compromise | > *(Award 1 mark per correctly filled row × 7 critical rows.)* --- **(b) Registration and Lookup Phases of Publicly Available Directory (7 Marks):** **Registration Phase (3.5 marks):** 1. Participant A generates key pair $(PU_a, PR_a)$. 2. A establishes a **secure authenticated channel** with the Directory Authority DA. 3. A transmits $(\text{ID}_A, PU_a)$ over this secure channel. 4. DA verifies A's identity, accepts the public key, and stores the entry $\text{Directory}[\text{ID}_A] = PU_a$. 5. DA confirms successful registration to A. 6. Optionally, A's entry carries a validity period $T_{\text{valid}}$. **Lookup Phase (3.5 marks):** 1. Alice decides to send a secure message to Bob and needs $PU_b$. 2. Alice generates a fresh nonce $\text{Nonce}_A$ for replay protection. 3. Alice sends $(\text{ID}_B, \text{Nonce}_A)$ to the DA over a secure authenticated channel. 4. DA retrieves $PU_b$ from its repository. 5. DA composes the reply: $(PU_b, \text{ID}_B, \text{Nonce}_A, T_s)$ and signs/encrypts it for Alice. 6. Alice decrypts, verifies the nonce matches her sent value, and checks timestamp freshness. 7. Alice accepts $PU_b$ as Bob's authentic public key and proceeds to encrypt/sign her message. **Diagram:** ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Alice participant DA as Directory Authority participant B as Bob Note over A,DA: REGISTRATION (one-time) A->>DA: Secure channel - ID_A, PU_a DA-->>A: Registration confirmed B->>DA: Secure channel - ID_B, PU_b DA-->>B: Registration confirmed Note over A,DA: LOOKUP (per session) A->>DA: Request ID_B, Nonce_A DA-->>A: PU_b, ID_B, Nonce_A, Timestamp Note over A: Alice verifies nonce and timestamp A->>B: E(PU_b, Message) B-->>B: Decrypt using PR_b ``` > *(Award 1.5 marks each for the two phases + 2 marks for the diagram = 7 marks.)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** A *Publicly Available Directory* is a centralized trusted repository of public keys, maintained by a *Directory Authority (DA)*. - **Trust Model:** All participants must trust the DA; trust is established via a **secure authenticated channel** between each participant and the DA. - **Two Phases:** (1) **Registration** — one-time deposit of a public key. (2) **Lookup** — per-session retrieval of another participant's key. - **Replay Defense:** Always use a **nonce** and **timestamp** in lookup requests/responses. - **Improvement Over Public Announcement:** Centralized authority *authenticates* keys, removing the forgery problem. - **Three Main Weaknesses:** (1) Single point of failure. (2) Performance bottleneck. (3) DA must be online 24×7. - **Successor Scheme:** *Public-Key Authority* — uses time-limited tickets to remove the bottleneck and reduce continuous online dependency. - **Final Successor:** *Public-Key Certificates* — fully offloads trust to digitally signed certificates (the basis of X.509 / PKI). - **Real-World Implementations:** LDAP, X.500 directories, Active Directory, DNSSEC, certificate repositories. - **Message Format Symbols:** $E(K, M)$, $S(PR, H(M))$, $V(PU, \sigma, H(M))$, $\text{Nonce}$, $T_s$. - **Key Security Property:** Only the *binding* (name → key) must be authentic; public keys themselves are not secret. - **Standard Reference Position (in Stallings' taxonomy):** Scheme #2 of 4 in the evolution of public-key distribution. - **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Always mention (i) the trusted third party, (ii) the secure authenticated channel, (iii) the nonce/timestamp, and (iv) at least two limitations in any 7+ mark answer. - **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing "publicly available" with "publicly announced" — the directory is *centralized and controlled*, not broadcast.

topics.title: Public Key Authority
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Public Key Authority ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Public Key Authority (PKA)** > A *Public Key Authority* is a **centralized, trusted third-party entity** that maintains a dynamic, secure directory of public keys for all registered users in a public-key cryptosystem. It functions as a single, authoritative source-of-truth that binds user identities to their public keys, thereby enabling secure public key distribution in the absence of pre-shared secrets. Formally, if $\mathcal{U} = \{U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n\}$ denotes the set of registered users, then the Public Key Authority holds the public binding map: $$ \Phi : \mathcal{U} \longrightarrow \mathcal{K}_{pub} $$ $$ \Phi(U_i) = PK_i $$ where $PK_i$ is the authentic public key of user $U_i$, certified through the Authority's digital signature. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The Government Gazette Office** > Imagine a country where every citizen owns a unique, locked mailbox. To send someone a secret letter, you need their mailbox's public "drop slot" location. But how do you know which slot belongs to whom, and that no one has forged a fake slot? > The **Public Key Authority** is like the **National Gazette Office**: a single, government-verified office that publishes a sealed, signed register. When Alice wants to mail Bob, she walks to this office, asks for Bob's verified slot number, receives a notarized certificate bearing the official seal (Authority's signature), and only then drops her letter. If the seal is valid, she trusts the address. **Why this matters in cryptography:** - In a pure public-key world, anyone can *claim* any public key — this is the classic **man-in-the-middle vulnerability**. - A PKA solves this by acting as a **Trusted Introducer** — a concept borrowed from real-world identity verification. - The standard **request rate for a key lookup is typically 1 RTT (round-trip time)**, and the *cryptographic trust anchor* is the Authority's well-known public key $PK_{AUTH}$. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |-----------|----------------|--------------| | **Authority Trust Level** | **Absolute / Root** | Highest tier in trust hierarchy | | Key Directory Update Latency | **< 1 second** (real-time) | Maintains dynamism | | Lookup Transaction RTT | **2 messages** | Minimum for request-response | | Signature Algorithm | **RSA-2048 / ECDSA-P256** | Industry standard | | Certificate Validity (if used) | **1 to 3 years** | Limits exposure window | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Public Key Authority The PKA operates as a **centralized, online, real-time lookup service**. Its core responsibilities are: 1. **Registration Phase (One-Time):** - Each user $U_i$ generates a key pair $(PK_i, SK_i)$ locally. - User $U_i$ submits $PK_i$ to the Authority over a *secure, authenticated channel* (often in person or via out-of-band verification). - The Authority stores $(ID_i, PK_i)$ in its public directory $\mathcal{D}$. 2. **Lookup Phase (Per Communication):** - When $A$ wishes to communicate with $B$, $A$ requests $B$'s public key. - The Authority returns $PK_B$ along with a **digital signature** binding $ID_B$ to $PK_B$. 3. **Trust Verification:** - Every user must hold a copy of $PK_{AUTH}$ (the Authority's public key) — distributed **out-of-band** (e.g., embedded in software, shipped on a CD, hardcoded in firmware). - $PK_{AUTH}$ is the **single root of trust** for the entire system. ### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Operational Step > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Centralized Trust Bottleneck** > The entire security of the PKA model **collapses** if $PK_{AUTH}$ is compromised or substituted. This is why $PK_{AUTH}$ is never transmitted over the network — it is *physically* or *out-of-band* distributed. **Step-by-step reasoning:** - **Why a single authority?** Because asymmetric cryptography fundamentally requires *binding* between identity and key. Without a binding, anyone can impersonate anyone. A trusted authority provides this binding via **digital signatures**. - **Why sign the response?** A signed response prevents an active attacker from substituting a *fake* public key. The Authority's signature $\sigma_{AUTH}$ serves as a **notarization**. - **Why out-of-band distribution of $PK_{AUTH}$?** If $PK_{AUTH}$ were transmitted online, an attacker could replace it with their own key, enabling them to forge *all* certificates. This is the **chicken-and-egg problem** of public key infrastructure. ### 2.3 Protocol Variants The PKA framework admits several operational variants: **Variant 1: Simple Public Key Authority (Denning-Sacco-style)** $$ A \rightarrow AUTH : \text{``Request } PK_B\text{''} $$ $$ AUTH \rightarrow A : PK_B \parallel \sigma_{AUTH}(T_1 \parallel ID_B \parallel PK_B) $$ **Variant 2: Verified Public Key Authority (with mutual authentication)** $$ A \rightarrow AUTH : ID_A \parallel ID_B $$ $$ AUTH \rightarrow A : PK_A \parallel PK_B \parallel \sigma_{AUTH}(\ldots) $$ $$ A \rightarrow B : \text{Encrypted message} $$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Notation | Where Used | |--------|---------|----------|------------| | $PK_i$ | Public key of user $i$ | Asymmetric key | Encryption, Verification | | $SK_i$ | Private/Secret key of user $i$ | Asymmetric key | Decryption, Signing | | $PK_{AUTH}$ | Authority's public key | Asymmetric key | Verifying Authority's signature | | $SK_{AUTH}$ | Authority's private key | Asymmetric key | Signing key bindings | | $\sigma_X(M)$ | Signature on message $M$ by entity $X$ | $\sigma_X(M) = \text{Sign}(SK_X, H(M))$ | Authentication | | $E_K(M)$ | Encryption of $M$ with key $K$ | Symmetric/Asymmetric | Confidentiality | | $H(M)$ | Hash of message $M$ | One-way function | Integrity | | $T_i$ | Timestamp $i$ | Anti-replay token | Freshness | | $N_i$ | Nonce $i$ | Random value | Replay prevention | | $ID_X$ | Identity of entity $X$ | String/ID | Naming | | $\Vert$ | Concatenation | Bit/byte join | Message construction | | $\mathcal{D}$ | Public directory | Database | Key storage | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where PKAs are used today:** > - **DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions):** Acts as a hierarchical chain of trust resolving public keys for DNS records. > - **OCSP Responders (Online Certificate Status Protocol):** Real-time authorities providing certificate validity. > - **Key Servers in PGP/GPG:** Centralized directories (e.g., `keys.openpgp.org`, `pgp.mit.edu`) functioning as public key authorities. > - **LDAP-based corporate directories:** Enterprises often run private PKAs for internal PKI. ## 3. Step-by-Step Protocol Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Protocol Walkthrough — Public Key Authority (Simple Model) Let us walk through a complete communication session between **Alice ($A$)** and **Bob ($B$)** via a Public Key Authority $AUTH$. The model assumes: - $A$ and $B$ both trust $PK_{AUTH}$. - The Authority maintains directory $\mathcal{D} = \{(ID_i, PK_i)\}$ for all registered users. **Step 1: Alice requests Bob's public key** Alice initiates communication by sending her identity and Bob's identity to the Authority: $$ A \rightarrow AUTH : \text{Request} = ID_A \parallel ID_B $$ > **Logic:** The Authority needs to know *who* is asking (for logging/auditing) and *whose* key is being requested. This is the lookup query in the directory. **Step 2: Authority responds with Bob's authenticated public key** The Authority retrieves $PK_B$ from $\mathcal{D}$, then constructs a response message $M$: $$ M = PK_B \parallel T_1 \parallel ID_A $$ where $T_1$ is the current timestamp. The Authority then signs $M$ using its private key: $$ \sigma_{AUTH} = \text{Sign}(SK_{AUTH}, H(M)) $$ The response packet is sent: $$ AUTH \rightarrow A : PK_B \parallel T_1 \parallel \sigma_{AUTH} $$ > **Logic:** The signature binds $PK_B$ to the timestamp $T_1$, preventing replay attacks. Including $T_1$ ensures the response is *fresh* — an attacker cannot capture and reuse an old signed response. **Step 3: Alice verifies the Authority's signature** Alice computes the hash of the received plaintext, then verifies: $$ \text{Verify}(PK_{AUTH}, \sigma_{AUTH}, H(PK_B \parallel T_1)) \overset{?}{=} \text{TRUE} $$ She also checks: $$ \vert T_{current} - T_1 \vert < \Delta $$ where $\Delta$ is the acceptable clock skew (typically **30 to 60 seconds**). > **Logic:** Signature verification confirms authenticity (the message came from $AUTH$). Timestamp freshness check confirms it's not a replay. **Step 4: Alice encrypts the message for Bob** Once $PK_B$ is verified, Alice constructs her plaintext message $M_{msg}$ and encrypts: $$ C = E_{PK_B}(M_{msg}) $$ > **Logic:** Only Bob possesses $SK_B$, the corresponding private key, so only Bob can decrypt $C$. **Step 5: Alice transmits the ciphertext to Bob** $$ A \rightarrow B : C $$ This step typically occurs over an *insecure* channel — the encryption provides confidentiality. **Step 6: Bob decrypts the message** $$ M_{msg} = D_{SK_B}(C) $$ > **Logic:** Bob uses his private key $SK_B$ (known only to him) to recover the plaintext. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Protocol Walkthrough — Verified Public Key Authority (Enhanced Model) To prevent a malicious user $A$ from impersonating another user, an **enhanced protocol** adds mutual authentication. **Step 1: Alice initiates key acquisition** $$ A \rightarrow AUTH : ID_A \parallel ID_B $$ **Step 2: Authority returns both keys, timestamped and signed** $$ M' = PK_A \parallel PK_B \parallel T_1 \parallel ID_A $$ $$ \sigma'_{AUTH} = \text{Sign}(SK_{AUTH}, H(M')) $$ $$ AUTH \rightarrow A : PK_A \parallel PK_B \parallel T_1 \parallel \sigma'_{AUTH} $$ **Step 3: Alice verifies and sends an authenticated token to Bob** Alice verifies the signature and timestamp, then constructs: $$ M_A = ID_A \parallel T_2 $$ $$ \sigma_A = \text{Sign}(SK_A, H(M_A)) $$ $$ A \rightarrow B : PK_A \parallel T_1 \parallel \sigma'_{AUTH} \parallel M_A \parallel \sigma_A $$ **Step 4: Bob verifies Alice's authenticity** Bob performs: 1. Verifies Authority's signature on $PK_A$ using $PK_{AUTH}$. 2. Verifies timestamp freshness: $\vert T_{current} - T_1 \vert < \Delta$. 3. Verifies Alice's signature on $M_A$ using $PK_A$ (now trusted via the Authority). Only if all three checks pass does Bob trust that the message is genuinely from Alice. > **Logic:** This enhanced protocol establishes **mutual trust** without requiring prior shared secrets, leveraging the Authority as a single trust anchor. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Security Properties | Attack Vector | Vulnerability in Simple PKA | Mitigation in Enhanced PKA | |---------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------| | **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | High — attacker substitutes $PK_B$ during transmission | Signature binds identity to key | | **Replay Attack** | Medium — old signed responses reusable | Timestamps + nonces enforce freshness | | **Authority Compromise** | **Catastrophic** — single point of failure | Hierarchical PKI / multiple authorities | | **Impersonation of Sender** | High in simple model | Mutual authentication via signatures | | **Denial of Service** | Medium — Authority is a bottleneck | Replication, load balancing | ### 3.4 Mathematical Justification — Why the Signature is Unforgeable The security of the PKA rests on the **existential unforgeability under chosen-message attack (EUF-CMA)** of the Authority's signature scheme. Formally: $$ \Pr[\mathcal{A}(\cdot) \to \sigma^* : \text{Verify}(PK_{AUTH}, \sigma^*, m^*) = \text{TRUE}, m^* \notin \mathcal{Q}] \leq \epsilon(\lambda) $$ where $\lambda$ is the security parameter (e.g., **2048 bits for RSA**), $\mathcal{A}$ is a probabilistic polynomial-time adversary, $\mathcal{Q}$ is the set of queried messages, and $\epsilon(\lambda)$ is a **negligible function**. > **Logic:** This means that no computationally-bounded adversary can forge a valid Authority signature on a message not previously signed — ensuring the integrity of the public key bindings. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Simple Public Key Authority Protocol ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant A as Alice participant AUTH as Public Key Authority participant B as Bob Note over A,B: Both A and B trust PK_AUTH (out-of-band) A->>AUTH: Request PK_B (sends ID_A, ID_B) AUTH->>AUTH: Lookup (ID_B, PK_B) in Directory AUTH->>A: PK_B, Timestamp T1, Sign(SK_AUTH) A->>A: Verify signature with PK_AUTH A->>A: Check timestamp freshness A->>B: Encrypt message M with PK_B B->>B: Decrypt with SK_B to recover M Note over A,B: Channel between A and B is unsecured Note over AUTH,A: Channel to Authority must be authenticated ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Public Key Authority Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph External["External Entities"] U1["User A
ID_A"] U2["User B
ID_B"] U3["User N
ID_N"] end subgraph PKACore["Public Key Authority Core"] REG["Registration
Module"] DIR["Public Key
Directory DB"] SIGN["Digital
Signature Engine"] LOG["Audit
Log"] end subgraph Trust["Trust Anchor"] ROOT["PK_AUTH
(Out-of-Band)"] end U1 -->|Submit PK_A| REG U2 -->|Submit PK_B| REG U3 -->|Submit PK_N| REG REG --> DIR U1 -->|Lookup PK_B| SIGN U2 -->|Lookup PK_A| SIGN SIGN -->|Query| DIR SIGN -->|Sign Response| LOG ROOT -.->|Verify| U1 ROOT -.->|Verify| U2 ROOT -.->|Verify| U3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Registration and Lookup Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A["User Generates
Key Pair PK_i, SK_i"] --> B["Submit PK_i
to Authority
(secure channel)"] B --> C["Authority Stores
ID_i, PK_i in Directory"] C --> D["Directory
Updated"] D --> E{"Lookup
Request?"} E -->|Yes| F["Retrieve PK_j
from Directory"] F --> G["Sign Response
with SK_AUTH"] G --> H["Return Signed
PK_j to Requester"] H --> I["Requester
Verifies Signature"] I --> J["Use PK_j
for Encryption"] E -->|No| K["Idle / Standby"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Threat Model Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Legitimate["Legitimate Channel"] AA["Alice"] -->|Step 1: Request| CAUTH["Authority"] CAUTH -->|Step 2: Signed PK_B| AA AA -->|Step 3: E_PK_B(M)| BB["Bob"] end subgraph Attack["MITM Attack (Blocked by PKA)"] EV["Eve
(Attacker)"] EV -.->|Tries to substitute fake PK_B| AA EV -.->|X| CAUTH end style Attack stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style EV fill:#ffe1e1 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, June 2024 Style]** > **Q1. Define Public Key Authority. Mention any two of its advantages.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A Public Key Authority is a centralized, trusted third-party entity that maintains a directory of public keys bound to user identities. It enables users in a public-key cryptosystem to securely obtain the authentic public key of any other user, eliminating the need for prior key exchange. > > **Advantages:** > 1. **Simplicity:** Users need not pre-share keys or maintain local key rings of all correspondents. > 2. **Centralized Control:** A single administrative point allows rapid revocation and updates. > 3. **Scalability through hierarchy:** Multiple authorities can be organized into trust chains (foundation of modern PKI). > *(Any two well-explained — 1.5 marks each)* > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** > **Q2. What is the role of timestamps in the Public Key Authority protocol?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Timestamps $T_1$ in a Public Key Authority protocol serve to enforce **message freshness**, thereby preventing **replay attacks**. Without a timestamp, an adversary who captures a signed response $(PK_B \parallel \sigma_{AUTH})$ could replay it later, causing the requesting user to use a stale or revoked key. By embedding $T_1$ inside the signed payload and requiring $\vert T_{current} - T_1 \vert < \Delta$ (where $\Delta$ is the acceptable clock skew, typically 30–60 seconds), the protocol ensures that only recently issued responses are accepted. *(Full marks for explaining replay attack and the freshness constraint.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, Dec 2024 ESE]** > > **Question A (14 Marks):** > **a)** Describe the operation of a Public Key Authority with a neat sequence diagram. Explain how the Authority's public key is securely distributed. **(7 marks)** > **b)** Discuss the various security threats faced by a Public Key Authority and explain how they are mitigated. **(7 marks)** > > --- > > **Model Solution for Question A:** > > **Part (a) — 7 marks:** > > The Public Key Authority (PKA) operates in a **client-server model** with a centralized directory $\mathcal{D}$ storing pairs $(ID_i, PK_i)$. > > **Operation sequence:** > 1. **Registration:** Each user $U_i$ generates a key pair $(PK_i, SK_i)$ and submits $PK_i$ to the Authority over a secure, authenticated channel. > 2. **Lookup Request:** When $A$ wants to send a message to $B$: > $$A \rightarrow AUTH : ID_A \parallel ID_B$$ > 3. **Authority Response:** The Authority retrieves $PK_B$, then signs the response: > $$\sigma_{AUTH} = \text{Sign}(SK_{AUTH}, H(PK_B \parallel T_1))$$ > $$AUTH \rightarrow A : PK_B \parallel T_1 \parallel \sigma_{AUTH}$$ > 4. **Verification:** $A$ verifies $\sigma_{AUTH}$ using $PK_{AUTH}$ and checks timestamp freshness. > 5. **Secure Communication:** $A$ encrypts the message: $C = E_{PK_B}(M)$ and sends $C$ to $B$. > 6. **Decryption:** $B$ recovers $M = D_{SK_B}(C)$. > > **Secure distribution of $PK_{AUTH}$:** *(2 marks dedicated)* > - **Out-of-band delivery:** $PK_{AUTH}$ is physically distributed (e.g., embedded in software, shipped on secure media, hardcoded in firmware). > - **Pre-installation:** Operating systems and browsers ship with embedded root certificates. > - **Trust on first use (TOFU):** Initial trust is established through secure context (e.g., in-person verification). > - **Web of trust fallback:** In PGP-style systems, multiple users can cross-certify $PK_{AUTH}$. > > **Part (b) — 7 marks:** > > | Threat | Description | Mitigation | > |--------|-------------|------------| > | **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | Attacker intercepts and substitutes public keys during transmission | Authority's digital signature binds $ID$ to $PK$ | > | **Replay Attack** | Reuse of old signed responses to deceive users | Timestamps $T_1$ and freshness check $\Delta$ | > | **Authority Compromise** | Attacker steals $SK_{AUTH}$ and forges all bindings | Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), air-gapped storage, multi-person key custody | > | **Single Point of Failure** | Authority outage halts all key lookups | Replication, redundant authorities, distributed PKI | > | **Impersonation** | Malicious user claims to be another | Mutual authentication with signatures from both parties | > | **Traffic Analysis** | Attacker observes which users communicate | Use of anonymous credential systems (advanced) | > > **Conclusion (1 mark):** While the PKA provides a robust solution to public key distribution, its centralized nature requires careful hardening against compromise, making modern implementations favor hierarchical PKI with certificate transparency logs. > > --- > > **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** > **a)** Compare and contrast a Public Key Authority with Public Key Certificates as mechanisms for distributing public keys. **(7 marks)** > **b)** Explain the concept of a hierarchical Public Key Authority structure. How does it overcome the single-point-of-failure problem of a flat PKA? **(7 marks)** > > --- > > **Model Solution for Question B:** > > **Part (a) — 7 marks:** > > | Aspect | Public Key Authority | Public Key Certificates | > |--------|----------------------|--------------------------| > | **Architecture** | Centralized, online lookup service | Decentralized, offline verification | > | **Trust Model** | Single root authority ($PK_{AUTH}$) | Chain of trust (Certificate Hierarchies) | > | **Connectivity** | Requires real-time online access to Authority | Works offline once certificate is obtained | > | **Key Storage** | Maintained in a live directory | Embedded in certificate, held by user | > | **Revocation** | Instant — Authority removes from directory | Requires CRL or OCSP check | > | **Failure Mode** | Single point of failure (Authority down = system down) | Certificates remain valid even if CA is offline | > | **Scalability** | Limited by Authority's capacity | Highly scalable via hierarchy | > | **Latency** | 1 RTT per lookup | Zero RTT after certificate acquisition | > | **Example** | LDAP directories, OCSP responders | X.509 certificates in HTTPS | > | **Cost** | High operational cost (24/7 availability) | Lower per-lookup cost | > > **Key insight (1 mark):** Certificates trade real-time revocation for **availability and scalability**, while PKAs trade scalability for **instantaneous key freshness**. > > **Part (b) — 7 marks:** > > In a **hierarchical PKA structure**, multiple authorities are organized in a tree: > > - **Root Authority (RA):** The top of the hierarchy. Its public key $PK_{RA}$ is the ultimate trust anchor, distributed out-of-band. > - **Intermediate Authorities (IAs):** Certified by the Root. Each IA can certify lower-level authorities or end users. > - **End Users (EUs):** Certified by their nearest IA. > > **Trust chain for a certificate $Cert_{EU}$ issued by $IA_2$, certified by $IA_1$, in turn certified by $RA$:** > > $$EU \text{ trusts } PK_{EU} \text{ iff } \text{Verify}(PK_{IA_1}, \sigma_{IA_1}, Cert_{IA_2}) \land \text{Verify}(PK_{RA}, \sigma_{RA}, Cert_{IA_1})$$ > > **How it overcomes single-point-of-failure:** > 1. **Distribution of load:** Multiple intermediate authorities handle certification requests, avoiding bottlenecks. > 2. **Geographic / Organizational partitioning:** Different IAs serve different regions or departments. > 3. **Localized compromise:** If $IA_2$ is compromised, only its subtree is affected; the Root and other branches remain secure. > 4. **Certificate caching:** Once a certificate is obtained, no further online access to the issuing authority is required. > 5. **Cross-certification:** Multiple roots can cross-certify each other, providing redundancy. > > **Conclusion (1 mark):** The hierarchical model is the foundation of modern **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** used in HTTPS, S/MIME, and digital signatures worldwide. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the trust anchor problem (–2 marks):** Students often describe the PKA protocol without addressing *how* $PK_{AUTH}$ is securely distributed. Always mention out-of-band delivery. > 2. **Confusing PKA with PKI (–1 to 2 marks):** A Public Key Authority is a *component* of PKI, not synonymous. PKI is the broader framework. > 3. **Skipping timestamp/nonce logic (–1 mark):** Replay attack mitigation is *not* optional — it is a core security requirement. > 4. **Not using proper notation (–0.5 to 1 mark):** Use formal symbols like $PK_i$, $SK_i$, $\sigma_X$, $E_K$, $D_K$, $H(\cdot)$, $T_i$ for full credit. > 5. **Omitting the sequence diagram (–1 mark):** Part (a) of 7-mark questions *typically requires* a sequence diagram. Always include one. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Public Key Authority** > > - **Core idea:** A **centralized trusted third party** that maintains a directory of public keys bound to user identities. > - **Root of trust:** $PK_{AUTH}$ — the Authority's public key, distributed **out-of-band** (physically, embedded in software, etc.). > - **Two-phase operation:** *Registration* (one-time) → *Lookup* (per communication). > - **Signature mechanism:** Authority signs each response with $SK_{AUTH}$ to prevent substitution attacks. > - **Freshness mechanism:** Timestamps $T_1$ and/or nonces $N_i$ prevent replay attacks; allowed skew $\Delta$ is **30–60 seconds** typically. > - **Trust is transitive but verified:** The Authority's signature makes the trust chain explicit and verifiable. > - **Key notation:** > - $PK_i$ = user $i$'s public key > - $SK_i$ = user $i$'s private key > - $PK_{AUTH}, SK_{AUTH}$ = Authority's key pair > - $\sigma_X(M) = \text{Sign}(SK_X, H(M))$ = signature on $M$ > - $E_K(\cdot), D_K(\cdot)$ = encryption/decryption > - $H(\cdot)$ = cryptographic hash function > - $T_i$ = timestamp, $N_i$ = nonce, $ID_X$ = identity > - $\Vert$ = concatenation > - **Key security properties:** Confidentiality (encryption), Authentication (signatures), Integrity (hash), Freshness (timestamps), Non-repudiation (signatures). > - **Major threats:** MITM, replay, Authority compromise, single-point-of-failure, impersonation, traffic analysis. > - **Mitigations:** Digital signatures, timestamps, HSMs for key custody, hierarchical PKI, replication, mutual authentication. > - **Comparison anchor:** PKA = online, centralized, real-time vs. Certificates = offline, decentralized, cached. > - **Real-world analogues:** LDAP directories, OCSP responders, PGP key servers, X.509 PKI hierarchies. > - **Foundational theorem:** The Authority's signature scheme must satisfy **EUF-CMA** (Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack) for the system to be secure. > - **Exam mantra:** *"The Authority's signed response is the cryptographic equivalent of a notarized identity document."*

topics.title: Public Key Certificates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT756 - SECURE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Public Key Certificates ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Public Key Certificate (PKC)**: A Public Key Certificate is a digitally signed electronic document, conforming to a standardized format (most commonly **ITU-T X.509 v3**), that securely binds a **public key** to an **identity** (an individual, organization, server, or device). This binding is **vouched for and cryptographically attested** by a trusted third party known as a **Certificate Authority (CA)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Secure Communication, a Public Key Certificate is the foundational element that solves the **Public Key Distribution Problem** — the classical challenge of securely associating a public key with a real-world identity over an untrusted network like the Internet. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Passport" Model Imagine a physical passport issued by a Government (the trusted authority). The passport: - **Proves who you are** (Identity Binding) - Contains your **photo and details** (Identity Attributes) - Is **signed and stamped** by the government (Cryptographic Signature) - Has an **expiry date** (Validity Period) - Can be **verified by anyone** who trusts the government (Trust Chain) A **Public Key Certificate** is exactly this — a **digital passport** for cryptographic keys. The **Certificate Authority (CA)** acts as the "Government," your **public key** is the "photo," and the **CA's digital signature** is the "official stamp." Any relying party (browser, server, application) that trusts the CA can verify the authenticity of your public key. ### 1.3 Why Public Key Certificates are Necessary > [!NOTE] > **The Core Problem Solved**: In asymmetric cryptography (RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman), Bob needs Alice's *authentic* public key to send her encrypted messages or verify her digital signatures. However, an attacker **Mallory** could publish a *fake* public key claiming to be Alice. Without a trusted binding mechanism, Bob cannot distinguish Alice's real key from Mallory's fake one. Certificates solve this by introducing a **Trusted Third Party (TTP)** — the CA — whose signature on the certificate vouches for the authenticity of the public key. ### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Certificate Authority (CA)** | Trusted entity that issues and signs certificates | | **Registration Authority (RA)** | Sub-entity that verifies the identity of the applicant before CA issues certificate | | **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** | The complete ecosystem of CAs, RAs, certificates, policies, and procedures | | **X.509** | The ITU-T standard defining the format of public key certificates | | **Distinguished Name (DN)** | A unique identifier for the certificate subject (e.g., CN=www.ktu.edu.in) | | **Self-Signed Certificate** | A certificate where the issuer and subject are the same (typically root CAs) | | **Certificate Revocation** | The process of invalidating a certificate before its expiry date | | **Chain of Trust** | A hierarchy of certificates from leaf → intermediate → root CA | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis**: The examiner will almost always expect you to mention the **X.509 standard** when defining a Public Key Certificate. This is a non-negotiable terminology in the KTU valuation key. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Workflow — How Certificates Work A Public Key Certificate system operates through a structured six-stage lifecycle: **Step 1 — Key Generation** - The subject (Alice) generates a key pair: a private key $\left(K_{priv}\right)$ and a public key $\left(K_{pub}\right)$. - Alice keeps $K_{priv}$ secret and prepares to submit $K_{pub}$ for certification. **Step 2 — Identity Registration (via RA)** - Alice submits a **Certificate Signing Request (CSR)** containing her $K_{pub}$ and identity information (name, email, domain, organization) to a **Registration Authority (RA)**. - The RA performs **out-of-band identity verification** (e.g., domain ownership validation, email verification, document checks). **Step 3 — Certificate Creation by the CA** - The CA constructs the certificate by embedding Alice's $K_{pub}$, identity details, validity period, and other metadata. - The CA then computes a **hash** of the certificate data and signs it using the **CA's own private key** $\left(K_{CA,priv}\right)$. **Step 4 — Certificate Issuance** - The signed certificate is delivered to Alice (the subject), who installs it on her server/application. **Step 5 — Certificate Distribution** - When Bob (the relying party) wishes to communicate securely with Alice, he retrieves her certificate (e.g., during the TLS handshake). **Step 6 — Certificate Verification by Relying Party** - Bob verifies the CA's digital signature on the certificate using the **CA's public key** $\left(K_{CA,pub}\right)$, which is pre-installed in his system as a **trust anchor**. ### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of the CA's Digital Signature The CA's signature is the cryptographic heart of the certificate. Let the certificate data (everything except the signature) be denoted as $M$. The CA performs: $$ H = \text{Hash}(M) $$ $$ \sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{CA,priv}}(H) $$ Here, $H$ is a cryptographic hash (commonly **SHA-256** today, historically MD5/SHA-1) and $\sigma$ is the digital signature. The signature $\sigma$ is appended to $M$ to form the complete certificate. **Verification by Bob** involves: - Recomputing $H' = \text{Hash}(M_{received})$ - Decrypting the signature: $H_{recovered} = \text{Verify}_{K_{CA,pub}}(\sigma)$ - Checking equality: $$ \text{Valid} \iff H' = H_{recovered} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: This signature scheme — sometimes called a **certificate signature** — is what gives certificates their non-repudiation and integrity properties. Any tampering with the certificate data (e.g., swapping Alice's public key with Mallory's) will cause the hash to change, invalidating the CA's signature. This is the same hash-and-sign paradigm used in digital signatures generally, and is directly examinable in KTU Module 2. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip**: For any certificate-related numerical/derivation problem, the following table contains every formula you may need. | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Certificate Hashing | $H = \text{Hash}(M)$ | $M$ = certificate body, $H$ = hash digest | SHA-256 produces 256-bit output | | CA Signature Generation | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{CA,priv}}(H)$ | $K_{CA,priv}$ = CA's private key | Uses RSA, ECDSA, or EdDSA | | Relying Party Verification | $H' \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Verify}_{K_{CA,pub}}(\sigma)$ | $K_{CA,pub}$ = CA's public key | Pre-installed as trust anchor | | RSA Key-Signature Size | $\vert\sigma\vert = \vert K_{CA,priv} \vert$ bits | e.g., 2048-bit RSA $\rightarrow$ 2048-bit signature | Output size equals modulus size | | ECC Signature Size | $\vert\sigma\vert \approx 2 \cdot n$ bits | $n$ = curve order in bits | e.g., P-256 (secp256r1) $\rightarrow$ ~512-bit sig | | Validity Period (days) | $V = D_{expiry} - D_{issue}$ | $D$ = dates in UTC | Typical: 90 days (Let's Encrypt), 1–2 years (commercial) | | Certificate Serial Number Range | $0 \leq S < 2^{n}$ | $S$ = serial, $n$ = bit length | Must be unique per CA | | Hash Comparison Condition | $H' = H_{recovered} \Rightarrow \text{VALID}$ | Equality test | One-bit mismatch = full rejection | ### 2.4 The X.509 v3 Certificate Structure (Mandatory KTU Topic) > [!CAUTION] > The X.509 v3 certificate format is a **direct syllabus topic** in KTU Module 3. You MUST memorize the fields below for the ESE. An X.509 v3 certificate is encoded using **ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One)** and follows this precise structure: | Field | Description | Example Value | |---|---|---| | **Version** | Certificate format version | `v3` (value = 2) | | **Serial Number** | Unique ID assigned by CA | `0A 3F 7C ...` | | **Signature Algorithm** | Hash + PK algorithm used by CA | `sha256WithRSAEncryption` | | **Issuer** | DN of the issuing CA | `CN = VeriSign Class 3 CA, O = VeriSign, C = US` | | **Validity Period** | Not Before / Not After dates | `2024-01-01` to `2025-01-01` | | **Subject** | DN of the certificate holder | `CN = www.ktu.edu.in, O = KTU, C = IN` | | **Subject Public Key Info** | Algorithm + the public key itself | `RSA 2048-bit` | | **Extensions (v3)** | Optional attributes: Key Usage, SAN, CRL Distribution Points, etc. | `subjectAltName = DNS:ktu.edu.in` | | **CA's Digital Signature** | Hash-signed output over all above fields | $\sigma$ (e.g., 2048-bit RSA signature) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Mapping**: When you visit `https://www.ktu.edu.in` and click the padlock icon in your browser, you are viewing exactly these fields. The **Subject Alternative Name (SAN)** extension is what binds a certificate to multiple domain names (e.g., `ktu.edu.in`, `www.ktu.edu.in`, `mail.ktu.edu.in`). ### 2.5 Certificate Hierarchies and Chain of Trust > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality**: No single CA is trusted universally. Real PKI is a **hierarchy** (or web of trust in PGP). The Chain of Trust allows scalability of trust delegation. A typical X.509 PKI has three tiers: - **Tier 1 — Root CA**: Self-signed, kept offline in a Hardware Security Module (HSM). Examples: DigiCert Global Root, ISRG Root X1. - **Tier 2 — Intermediate CA**: Signed by the Root CA, used for day-to-day issuance. Can be revoked without compromising the root. - **Tier 3 — Leaf / End-Entity Certificate**: The actual server/client certificate that Alice or Bob uses. The **Chain of Trust** is constructed as: $$\text{Leaf Cert} \xrightarrow{\text{signed by}} \text{Intermediate CA} \xrightarrow{\text{signed by}} \text{Root CA} \in \text{Trust Store}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU 2-Mark Favourite**: "What is a Chain of Trust?" — Answer: It is the ordered sequence of certificates from the end-entity certificate up to a trusted root CA, where each certificate's signature is verifiable using the public key of the next higher CA in the chain. Trust is **delegated transitively** from the root. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Certificate Verification by a Relying Party This derivation walks through the full mathematical and logical verification of an X.509 certificate by Bob when he receives it from Alice's server during a TLS handshake. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU**: This is the most common 7-mark derivation-style question. You will be expected to write out every step. **Given:** - Alice's certificate $\text{Cert}_A$ with body $M$ and signature $\sigma_A$. - $\text{Cert}_A$ was signed by an Intermediate CA $\text{CA}_{INT}$, whose certificate is $\text{Cert}_{INT}$. - $\text{Cert}_{INT}$ was signed by a Root CA $\text{CA}_{ROOT}$. - Bob's trust store contains $K_{CA_{ROOT}, pub}$. **Step 1 — Bob extracts the certificate components** $$ \text{Cert}_A = \{M_A, \sigma_A\} $$ $$ M_A = \{V, S_N, \text{Alg}, \text{Issuer}, \text{Validity}, \text{Subject}, K_{A,pub}, \text{Ext}\} $$ Here: - $V$ = version field - $S_N$ = serial number - $\text{Alg}$ = signature algorithm identifier - $\text{Issuer} = \text{CN} = \text{CA}_{INT}$ - $K_{A,pub}$ = Alice's claimed public key **Step 2 — Bob obtains the issuing CA's certificate** Bob looks up the **Authority Information Access (AIA)** extension in $\text{Cert}_A$, which contains a URL pointing to $\text{Cert}_{INT}$. He downloads it: $$ \text{Cert}_{INT} = \{M_{INT}, \sigma_{INT}\} $$ **Step 3 — Bob verifies the intermediate CA's certificate** He first verifies $\text{Cert}_{INT}$ is signed by the Root CA: $$ H_{INT} = \text{Hash}(M_{INT}) $$ $$ H_{INT,recovered} = \text{Verify}_{K_{CA_{ROOT},pub}}(\sigma_{INT}) $$ **Validation condition:** $$ \text{Valid}_{INT} \iff H_{INT} = H_{INT,recovered} $$ **Step 4 — Bob extracts the intermediate CA's public key** If $\text{Valid}_{INT} = \text{true}$, Bob trusts $K_{CA_{INT}, pub}$ from the $\text{Subject Public Key Info}$ field of $\text{Cert}_{INT}$. **Step 5 — Bob now verifies Alice's certificate** $$ H_A = \text{Hash}(M_A) $$ $$ H_{A,recovered} = \text{Verify}_{K_{CA_{INT},pub}}(\sigma_A) $$ **Validation condition:** $$ \text{Valid}_A \iff H_A = H_{A,recovered} $$ **Step 6 — Bob checks certificate validity period** $$ \text{Valid}_{time} \iff D_{current} \in [D_{not\_before}, D_{not\_after}] $$ **Step 7 — Bob checks revocation status** Bob queries the **CRL Distribution Point** or **OCSP responder** referenced in the certificate: $$ \text{Valid}_{rev} \iff S_N \notin \text{Revocation List} $$ **Step 8 — Final composite validation** $$ \text{Valid}_{final} = \text{Valid}_{INT} \land \text{Valid}_A \land \text{Valid}_{time} \land \text{Valid}_{rev} $$ If and only if $\text{Valid}_{final} = \text{true}$, Bob trusts $K_{A,pub}$ and proceeds with secure communication (e.g., key exchange). > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Note**: Step 3 verifies the *issuer's* certificate first — this is critical because Bob must establish a chain of valid public keys before he can verify anything signed by them. This bottom-up trust model is what makes PKI scalable: the root only signs a small number of intermediates, and those intermediates sign millions of leaf certificates. ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Certificate Standards & Formats This matrix is **frequently asked** as a 3-mark comparison question in KTU ESE. | Feature | X.509 v3 | PGP Certificates | SPKI/SDSI | |---|---|---|---| | **Standard Body** | ITU-T | OpenPGP (RFC 4880) | IETF (RFC 2692/2693) | | **Trust Model** | Hierarchical CA-based | Web of Trust | Decentralized, name-based | | **Identity Binding** | Distinguished Name (DN) | Email + Name | Public key hash | | **Signature Scheme** | CA signature required | Self-signed + peer signatures | Self-signed | | **Primary Use Case** | HTTPS/TLS, S/MIME, code signing | Email encryption, file signing | Secure distributed systems | | **Revocation** | CRL, OCSP | Revocation certificates | Limited support | | **KTU Relevance** | ★★★★★ (Primary focus) | ★★ (Mentioned only) | ★ (Not in syllabus) | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — RSA-Based Certificate Signature > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern**: The examiner may give you small RSA parameters and ask you to simulate the certificate signing/verification process. This is a high-probability 14-mark question type. **Given** (toy example for illustration): - CA's RSA public key: $n = 3233$, $e = 17$ - CA's RSA private key: $d = 2753$ - Certificate hash (after hashing): $H = 65$ (treated as integer) **Step 1 — CA computes the signature** $$ \sigma = H^d \bmod n $$ $$ \sigma = 65^{2753} \bmod 3233 $$ We compute this using repeated squaring (truncated for brevity, real computation uses modular exponentiation): $$ 65^1 \bmod 3233 = 65 $$ $$ 65^2 \bmod 3233 = 4225 \bmod 3233 = 992 $$ $$ 65^4 \bmod 3233 = 992^2 \bmod 3233 = 984064 \bmod 3233 $$ $$ 984064 \div 3233 \approx 304.38 \Rightarrow 304 \times 3233 = 983232 \Rightarrow 984064 - 983232 = 832 $$ $$ 65^4 \bmod 3233 = 832 $$ (Continuing this process for the full exponent 2753 eventually yields the signature $\sigma$.) **Step 2 — Bob verifies the signature** Bob computes: $$ H' = \sigma^e \bmod n $$ $$ H' = 65^{2753 \times 17} \bmod 3233 $$ $$ H' = 65^{46801} \bmod 3233 $$ By Euler's theorem, since $ed = 17 \times 2753 = 46801 \equiv 1 \bmod \phi(n)$, we have: $$ H' = 65 \bmod 3233 = 65 = H $$ **Verification result:** $H' = H$, so the certificate is **VALID**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Allocation Note**: When presenting this derivation, allocate marks as: 1 for stating the algorithm, 2 for showing exponentiation setup, 2 for modular reduction logic, 1 for verification, 1 for conclusion. Most students lose marks by skipping the modular reduction intermediate steps — **show at least 2–3 squaring steps**. ### 3.4 Certificate Lifecycle States — State Transition Analysis | State | Description | Transition Trigger | |---|---|---| | **Pending** | CSR submitted, awaiting CA validation | RA approves identity | | **Issued** | CA has signed and published the certificate | CA applies signature | | **Active** | Certificate is within validity period and not revoked | Clock crosses $D_{not\_before}$ | | **Expired** | Clock has passed $D_{not\_after}$ | Time-based, automatic | | **Revoked** | CA has explicitly invalidated the certificate | Compromise, key loss, etc. | | **Suspended** *(optional)* | Temporary hold (rare in modern PKI) | Administrative action | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake**: Students often confuse *expiration* (automatic, time-based) with *revocation* (manual, security-based). Expired certificates are simply past their validity date; revoked certificates are explicitly cancelled by the CA, often due to private key compromise. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — X.509 PKI Hierarchy (Chain of Trust) ```mermaid graph TD A["Root CA
Self-Signed
Offline HSM"] --> B["Intermediate CA 1
Domain Validation"] A --> C["Intermediate CA 2
Organization Validation"] A --> D["Intermediate CA 3
Extended Validation"] B --> E["Leaf Cert
www.ktu.edu.in
TLS Server"] B --> F["Leaf Cert
mail.ktu.edu.in
TLS Server"] C --> G["Leaf Cert
employee.ktu.edu.in
S MIME Email"] D --> H["Leaf Cert
erp.ktu.edu.in
EV TLS Highest Trust"] E --> I["Relying Party
Browser TLS Handshake"] F --> I G --> J["Relying Party
Email Client"] H --> I ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The Root CA never directly signs leaf certificates — it only signs Intermediate CAs. - Intermediate CAs are revocable without invalidating the root. - A single Relying Party (browser) can verify certificates from multiple branches because the root is the common trust anchor. ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Certificate Verification Flow (Relying Party Process) ```mermaid graph TD S1["Start: Receive Certificate
from Alice Server"] --> S2["Parse X 509 v3 Fields
Extract Subject Issuer Validity"] S2 --> S3{"Is Cert Within
Validity Period?"} S3 -->|No| S4["REJECT: Expired"] S3 -->|Yes| S5["Check Revocation
via CRL or OCSP"] S5 --> S6{"Is Certificate
Revoked?"} S6 -->|Yes| S7["REJECT: Revoked"] S6 -->|No| S8["Locate Issuer Certificate
via AIA Extension"] S8 --> S9["Verify Issuer Certificate
Signature using Root Public Key"] S9 --> S10{"Issuer Signature
Valid?"} S10 -->|No| S11["REJECT: Issuer Untrusted"] S10 -->|Yes| S12["Extract Issuer Public Key
K CA INT pub"] S12 --> S13["Verify Alice Certificate
Signature using K CA INT pub"] S13 --> S14{"Alice Signature
Valid?"} S14 -->|No| S15["REJECT: Tampered Cert"] S14 -->|Yes| S16["Extract Alice Public Key
K A pub"] S16 --> S17["ACCEPT: Proceed with
Secure Communication"] ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** This flow captures the full RFC 5280 path validation algorithm in visual form. Notice that the process terminates at the first failure — the certificate is rejected without partial trust. ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — X.509 v3 Certificate Internal Structure ```mermaid graph LR A["X 509 v3 Certificate"] --> B["Version Field
Value = 2 for v3"] A --> C["Serial Number
Unique CA Identifier"] A --> D["Signature Algorithm ID
e.g. SHA256 RSA"] A --> E["Issuer Distinguished Name
CA Identity"] A --> F["Validity Period
NotBefore and NotAfter"] A --> G["Subject Distinguished Name
Holder Identity"] A --> H["Subject Public Key Info
Algorithm plus Public Key"] A --> I["v3 Extensions
KeyUsage SAN AIA CRL"] A --> J["CA Digital Signature
Over all above fields"] ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** Every field except `J` (the CA's signature) is hashed together to produce the input for the signature. This is why any modification to fields `B` through `I` invalidates `J` and causes verification to fail. ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — PKI Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "Certificate Authority Tier" CA1["CA Server
HSM Protected"] RA1["Registration Authority
Identity Verification"] DB1["Certificate Database
Audit Log"] end subgraph "Subject Tier" S1["Subject Alice
Generates Key Pair"] S2["CSR Creation
PKCS10 Format"] end subgraph "Relying Party Tier" R1["Relying Party Bob
Trust Store with Root CA"] R2["CRL OCSP Resolver
Revocation Checks"] end S1 --> S2 S2 --> RA1 RA1 -->|Verified Identity| CA1 CA1 -->|Signed Certificate| S1 CA1 --> DB1 S1 -->|Presents Certificate| R1 R1 -->|Validates Chain| CA1 R1 -->|Checks Revocation| R2 R2 --> CA1 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram shows the full PKI architecture as three functional tiers. The Subject generates keys and submits a CSR, the CA Tier (with HSM and RA) issues the certificate, and the Relying Party Tier validates it using the trust store and revocation infrastructure. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a Public Key Certificate. List any four fields of an X.509 v3 certificate.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > A Public Key Certificate is a digitally signed electronic document, conforming to the **X.509 v3** standard, that cryptographically binds a public key to the identity of its owner. The binding is attested by a trusted third party called a **Certificate Authority (CA)**. **Four fields of an X.509 v3 certificate (any 4 × 0.5 = 2 marks, definition 1 mark):** 1. **Version** — indicates the X.509 version (e.g., v3 → value 2). 2. **Serial Number** — a unique integer assigned by the CA to identify the certificate. 3. **Issuer** — the Distinguished Name (DN) of the issuing CA. 4. **Validity Period** — contains `Not Before` and `Not After` dates defining the operational lifetime of the certificate. 5. **Subject** — the DN of the entity owning the public key. 6. **Subject Public Key Info** — the public key and its algorithm (e.g., RSA 2048-bit). 7. **CA's Digital Signature** — the hash-signed output over all preceding fields. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is a Certificate Authority (CA)? What is the role of a Registration Authority (RA)?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Certificate Authority (CA)** (1.5 marks): A Certificate Authority is a **trusted third-party entity** that issues, signs, and manages digital certificates. It acts as the "issuer of trust" in a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). The CA's own private key is used to sign certificates, and its public key is distributed to relying parties as a **trust anchor** (e.g., pre-installed in browsers and operating systems). Examples: DigiCert, Let's Encrypt, ISRG. > **Registration Authority (RA)** (1.5 marks): A Registration Authority is a sub-entity that operates under a CA. Its role is to **verify the identity of certificate applicants** before the CA issues a certificate. The RA performs **out-of-band identity checks** such as domain ownership validation (for DV certs), organization document verification (for OV certs), or extended legal verification (for EV certs). The RA does **not** sign certificates — only the CA does. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** **a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **X.509 Public Key Certificate** format. Describe each field in detail. **(7 Marks)** **b)** Explain the **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) hierarchy** and the concept of **Chain of Trust** with a suitable example. Describe how certificate verification is performed by a relying party. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks** The X.509 v3 Public Key Certificate is structured as follows (refer to Diagram 4.3 for the visual): | Field | Description | Marks | |---|---|---| | **Version** | Identifies the certificate version. Value 2 means X.509 v3. | 0.5 | | **Serial Number** | A positive integer uniquely assigned by the CA to each certificate. | 0.5 | | **Signature Algorithm** | The OID of the algorithm used by the CA to sign, e.g., `1.2.840.113549.1.1.11` for SHA-256 with RSA. | 1.0 | | **Issuer** | Distinguished Name (DN) of the issuing CA — includes CN, O, C, etc. | 0.5 | | **Validity Period** | Two timestamps: `Not Before` and `Not After` in UTC (GeneralizedTime format). | 1.0 | | **Subject** | DN of the entity that owns the public key. | 0.5 | | **Subject Public Key Info** | Contains the public key and its algorithm (e.g., RSA 2048-bit, ECC P-256). | 1.0 | | **Extensions (v3 specific)** | Optional fields: `Key Usage`, `Extended Key Usage`, `Subject Alternative Name (SAN)`, `Basic Constraints`, `CRL Distribution Points`, `Authority Information Access (AIA)`. | 1.0 | | **CA's Digital Signature** | A hash (SHA-256) of all preceding fields, encrypted with the CA's private key (RSA/ECDSA). | 1.0 | **Working Principle (1 mark):** The CA computes a hash over all fields from Version through Extensions, then signs this hash with its private key. Any tampering with any field invalidates the signature, ensuring **integrity and authenticity**. --- #### **Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks** **PKI Hierarchy (3 marks):** A real-world PKI is structured as a three-tier hierarchy to provide scalability, security, and operational flexibility: - **Tier 1 — Root CA (Trust Anchor)**: The topmost CA. It is **self-signed** (Issuer = Subject) and its certificate is **pre-installed** in operating systems and browsers. Root CAs are kept **offline** in Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) to prevent compromise. Examples: `DigiCert Global Root G3`, `ISRG Root X1` (Let's Encrypt). - **Tier 2 — Intermediate CA**: Signed by the Root CA. These CAs perform the **day-to-day certificate issuance** and can be **revoked independently** if compromised, without affecting the root. Multiple intermediates may exist for different validation levels (DV, OV, EV). - **Tier 3 — Leaf / End-Entity Certificate**: The actual certificate installed on a web server, email client, or IoT device. It is signed by an Intermediate CA. **Chain of Trust (2 marks):** The **Chain of Trust** is the ordered sequence: $\text{Leaf} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate} \rightarrow \text{Root}$. Trust is **transitively delegated**: the user trusts the root (via trust store), the root vouches for the intermediate (via signature), and the intermediate vouches for the leaf. Verification proceeds **bottom-up**. **Example:** A browser connecting to `https://www.ktu.edu.in` receives the leaf cert, which references the Intermediate CA in its `AIA` field. The browser fetches the intermediate cert, verifies it against the Root CA in its trust store, then uses the intermediate's public key to verify the leaf. **Relying Party Verification (2 marks):** The relying party performs the following steps: 1. Check the certificate's validity period against the current time. 2. Verify the CA's signature on the certificate using the CA's public key from the next certificate in the chain. 3. Check the certificate's revocation status via **CRL (Certificate Revocation List)** or **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)**. 4. Validate the chain up to a trusted root in the local trust store. 5. If all checks pass, the public key in the certificate is trusted and used for the intended purpose (e.g., TLS key exchange). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified Pattern]** **a)** Explain the **concept and structure** of a Public Key Certificate. How does it solve the **public key distribution problem** in asymmetric cryptography? **(7 Marks)** **b)** Describe the complete **certificate verification algorithm** used by a relying party. Include a discussion of **certificate revocation** mechanisms (CRL and OCSP) with their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks** **Concept of Public Key Certificate (3 marks):** A Public Key Certificate is an electronic document that **binds a public key to an identity** (person, organization, server) through the **digital signature of a trusted Certificate Authority (CA)**. The binding is cryptographically verifiable — any modification to the certificate breaks the CA's signature. The standard format is **X.509 v3** (ITU-T). **Structure of X.509 v3 Certificate (2 marks):** The certificate contains: - Identity fields: Version, Serial Number, Issuer DN, Subject DN. - Cryptographic fields: Signature Algorithm, Subject Public Key Info, CA's Digital Signature. - Validity fields: Not Before, Not After timestamps. - Extensions (v3): Key Usage, SAN, AIA, CRL Distribution Points, etc. Refer to Question A(a) for the detailed field-wise breakdown. **Solution to the Public Key Distribution Problem (2 marks):** In asymmetric cryptography, Bob needs Alice's **authentic** public key. Without a trusted mechanism, an attacker (Mallory) can publish a fake public key claiming to be Alice — this is the **man-in-the-middle attack** on public keys. The Public Key Certificate solves this by: 1. **Out-of-band identity verification** by the CA (or RA) before issuance. 2. **Cryptographic binding** of Alice's public key to her identity via the CA's signature. 3. **Trust delegation**: Bob only needs to trust the CA's public key (one anchor), not every individual's key. 4. **Scalability**: A single CA can vouch for millions of entities, making global secure communication practical (as in HTTPS). --- #### **Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks** **Certificate Verification Algorithm (4 marks):** The relying party (e.g., a web browser) executes the following steps in order: 1. **Parse the certificate** and extract the Issuer, Subject, Validity, and Signature fields. 2. **Check the validity period** — reject if `current_time` is outside `[NotBefore, NotAfter]`. 3. **Construct the certificate chain** from the end-entity certificate up to a trusted root CA in the local trust store. 4. **Verify each signature** in the chain: - For each certificate $C_i$ in the chain, compute $H_i = \text{Hash}(M_i)$ and check $\text{Verify}_{K_{i-1,pub}}(\sigma_i) = H_i$. 5. **Check the key usage and extended key usage** extensions to ensure the certificate is valid for the intended purpose (e.g., `serverAuth` for TLS). 6. **Check revocation status** via CRL or OCSP. 7. If all checks pass, **accept the public key** in the end-entity certificate and proceed with the secure session. **Certificate Revocation Mechanisms (3 marks):** | Mechanism | How It Works | Advantages | Disadvantages | |---|---|---|---| | **CRL (Certificate Revocation List)** | CA periodically publishes a signed list of revoked certificate serial numbers.
